<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define Industrial Hygiene. List its four pillars in sequence.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Industrial Hygiene is the science and art devoted to the anticipation, recognition, evaluation, and control of workplace environmental stressors that may cause sickness, impaired health, or discomfort among workers.

The four pillars in sequence (1 mark each, total 3):
1. **Anticipation** – Predicting hazards from process design and materials.
2. **Recognition** – Identifying chemical, physical, biological, ergonomic, or psychosocial stressors through surveys and SDS review.
3. **Evaluation** – Quantitative measurement (air sampling, noise dosimetry) and comparison with OELs.
4. **Control** – Application of the hierarchy of controls to reduce exposure.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between TLV-TWA and TLV-STEL. Why is the STEL check required even when TWA is within limits?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **TLV-TWA (1 mark):** Time-Weighted Average concentration for a normal 8-hour workday and 40-hour workweek, below which nearly all workers may be repeatedly exposed without adverse effect.
- **TLV-STEL (1 mark):** 15-minute time-weighted average exposure that should not be exceeded at any time during the workday, even if 8-hr TWA is within TLV-TWA.

**Why STEL check is mandatory (1 mark):** Some toxicants cause acute effects (e.g., irritation, narcosis, CNS depression) at short peak exposures that an 8-hr TWA would average out. The TWA may show compliance while short bursts exceed safe levels, hence STEL acts as a supplementary limit.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** *Explain in detail the **Hierarchy of Controls** used in Industrial Hygiene. Discuss the relative effectiveness of each level with two industrial examples for each. **(7 marks)***

**(b)** *A factory operator is exposed to the following solvent concentrations during an 8-hour shift:*
- 06:00–09:00 — 60 ppm (mixing)
- 09:00–12:00 — 5 ppm (idle)
- 12:00–13:00 — 0 ppm (lunch break, outside)
- 13:00–15:00 — 90 ppm (spray painting)
- 15:00–17:00 — 30 ppm (cleanup)

*Given TLV-TWA = 50 ppm and TLV-STEL = 75 ppm, determine: (i) the 8-hour TWA, and (ii) whether any 15-minute STEL excursion occurs. **(7 marks)***

#### Solution to Q.A(a) — Hierarchy of Controls (7 marks)

The Hierarchy of Controls is the foundational framework for **reducing or eliminating workplace exposure**. It is ordered by **effectiveness, not ease of implementation** (1 mark).

1. **Elimination (Most effective — 1 mark):** Physically remove the hazard. Examples: (i) Replace solvent-based paint line with powder coating; (ii) Remove asbestos insulation from old boilers.

2. **Substitution (1 mark):** Replace with a less hazardous alternative. Examples: (i) Replace toluene with water-based cleaning agent; (ii) Use sodium bicarbonate blasting instead of sand (eliminates silica).

3. **Engineering Controls (1 mark):** Isolate workers from the hazard. Examples: (i) Local exhaust ventilation (LEV) at grinding stations; (ii) Enclosure of noisy compressors in acoustic rooms.

4. **Administrative Controls (1 mark):** Change work procedures. Examples: (i) Job rotation to limit cumulative noise dose; (ii) Mandatory rest breaks in cool rooms for heat-stress workers.

5. **PPE (Least effective, last resort — 1 mark):** Personal barrier. Examples: (i) N95 respirators for dust; (ii) Ear plugs/muffs for noise above 85 dB(A).

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Controls higher in the hierarchy are *passive* and *fail-safe*; lower controls (PPE) rely on human compliance and are the least reliable. KTU's standard answer expects explicit ordering: **Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE.**

#### Solution to Q.A(b) — TWA and STEL Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Convert to consistent units (1 mark).**
| Interval | Concentration $C_i$ (ppm) | Time $T_i$ (hr) | Product $C_i \times T_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 06:00–09:00 | 60  | 3.0 | 180 |
| 09:00–12:00 | 5   | 3.0 | 15  |
| 12:00–13:00 | 0   | 1.0 | 0   |
| 13:00–15:00 | 90  | 2.0 | 180 |
| 15:00–17:00 | 30  | 2.0 | 60  |
| **Total** |     | **11.0** | — |

**Step 2 — Normalize to 8 hours (1 mark).**

Sum of $T_i = 11$ hr — but the workday is 8 hours. For TWA, we consider only the **in-shift exposure (8 hr) excluding lunch break (1 hr)**, since lunch is outside the work environment. Re-summing only in-plant time:

$$
\sum T_i = 3.0 + 3.0 + 2.0 + 2.0 = 8.0 \text{ hr} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 3 — Compute 8-hour TWA (1.5 marks).**

$$
\sum C_i T_i = 180 + 15 + 0 + 180 + 60 \quad \text{(but excluding lunch row 0×1=0)}
$$

Wait — including the lunch row: $180 + 15 + 0 + 180 + 60 = 435 \text{ ppm·hr}$.

$$
\text{TWA} = \frac{435}{8} = 54.375 \text{ ppm}
$$

**Step 4 — Compliance check (1 mark):**

$$
\text{TWA} = 54.4 \text{ ppm} \;>\; \text{TLV-TWA} = 50 \text{ ppm}
$$

⇒ **Non-compliant** for TWA. (Excess ≈ 8.75%)

**Step 5 — STEL check (1.5 marks).**
Look for any 15-minute interval with high concentration.
- Spray painting (13:00–15:00) is at **90 ppm** for 2 hours. Within this, a 15-min window is 90 ppm > TLV-STEL = 75 ppm.

⇒ **STEL exceeded** (90 ppm > 75 ppm).

**Step 6 — Conclusion (1 mark):**
The operator is exposed to both a TWA excursion (54.4 vs 50 ppm) and an STEL excursion (90 vs 75 ppm) ⇒ **mandatory engineering/administrative controls required**, e.g., automated spray booth with LEV.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Valuation Pitfalls (Loses 2–3 marks):**
> 1. *Forgetting to include the 0-ppm lunch break* in $C_i T_i$ summation. (If you do include it: $180 + 15 + 0 + 180 + 60 = 435$, same here. But if you mistakenly exclude the 13:00–15:00 spray interval, you'll get 45 ppm — wrong.)
> 2. *Failing to check STEL separately* — TWA compliance does not imply STEL compliance.
> 3. *Using 11 hours as the denominator* — the TWA standard is **8 hours**, not total elapsed time. Lunch breaks *outside the contaminated zone* can be excluded with proper justification.

---

### Question B — **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme Alternative]**

**(a)** *Discuss the **types of chemical hazards** encountered in industrial workplaces. With a neat classification, give two examples and the corresponding TLV/TWA value for each. **(7 marks)***

**(b)** *A worker is exposed to noise levels as follows during an 8-hour shift: 92 dB(A) for 4 hr, 85 dB(A) for 2 hr, and 80 dB(A) for 2 hr. Using OSHA's 5 dB exchange rate and 90 dB(A) criterion level, determine the **Noise Dose (%)** and the **TWA in dB(A)**. State the compliance status. **(7 marks)***

#### Solution to Q.B(a) — Chemical Hazards (7 marks)

Chemical hazards are classified by **physical state and route of exposure** (1 mark for classification).

1. **Dusts (1.5 marks):** Solid particles formed by mechanical processes.
   - Example 1: *Crystalline silica* (SiO₂) — TLV-TWA = **0.025 mg/m³** (ACGIH 2023, A2 carcinogen). Causes silicosis.
   - Example 2: *Coal dust* — TLV-TWA = **1 mg/m³** (respirable fraction). Causes coal workers' pneumoconiosis.

2. **Fumes (1 mark):** Solid particles from condensation of vaporized metals.
   - Example: *Welding fumes (Fe₂O₃)* — TLV-TWA = **5 mg/m³**. Causes siderosis.

3. **Mists (1 mark):** Liquid droplets suspended in air.
   - Example: *Mineral oil mist* — TLV-TWA = **5 mg/m³**. Causes lipoid pneumonia.

4. **Vapors (1 mark):** Gaseous form of a liquid at room temperature.
   - Example: *Benzene* — TLV-TWA = **0.5 ppm** (A1 carcinogen). Causes leukemia.

5. **Gases (1 mark):** Normal state gaseous, no liquid phase at working temp.
   - Example: *Carbon monoxide* — TLV-TWA = **25 ppm**. Causes carboxyhemoglobinemia.

6. **Fibers (0.5 mark):** Elongated particles with aspect ratio > 3:1.
   - Example: *Asbestos* — TLV-TWA = **0.1 f/cc** (fibers/cc). Causes asbestosis, mesothelioma.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners look for **state of the chemical + TLV value + disease/health effect** — all three together. Always state the unit explicitly (ppm vs. mg/m³ vs. f/cc).

#### Solution to Q.B(b) — Noise Dose and TWA (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Apply OSHA's 5 dB exchange rate (2 marks).**
Reference duration $T_{\text{ref}}$ at each level $L$:

$$
T_{\text{ref}}(L) = \frac{8}{2^{(L-90)/5}}
$$

| $L$ (dB(A)) | $(L-90)/5$ | $2^{(L-90)/5}$ | $T_{\text{ref}}$ (hr) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 92 | 0.4  | 1.319 | 6.06 |
| 85 | -1.0 | 0.5   | 16.0 |
| 80 | -2.0 | 0.25  | 32.0 |

**Step 2 — Compute actual exposure time ratio $C_i = T_i / T_{\text{ref}}$ (1.5 marks).**

$$
C_1 = \frac{4}{6.06} = 0.660
$$

$$
C_2 = \frac{2}{16.0} = 0.125
$$

$$
C_3 = \frac{2}{32.0} = 0.0625
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Noise Dose (%) (1.5 marks).**

$$
D(\%) = 100 \times (C_1 + C_2 + C_3) = 100 \times (0.660 + 0.125 + 0.0625) = 100 \times 0.8475
$$

$$
\boxed{D = 84.75\%}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Noise TWA in dB(A) (1.5 marks).**

$$
\text{TWA}_{\text{noise}} = 16.61 \log_{10}\left(\frac{D}{100}\right) + 90
$$

$$
= 16.61 \log_{10}(0.8475) + 90 = 16.61 \times (-0.0718) + 90 = -1.193 + 90
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{TWA}_{\text{noise}} = 88.8 \text{ dB(A)}}
$$

**Step 5 — Compliance statement (0.5 mark):**
Since $D = 84.75\% < 100\%$ and $\text{TWA} = 88.8 \text{ dB(A)} < 90 \text{ dB(A)}$, the worker is **compliant** with OSHA's PEL but the level is still above the NIOSH REL (85 dB(A)) and ACGIH TLV (85 dB(A)) — hearing conservation program still recommended.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfalls in Noise Numericals:**
> 1. *Using the 3 dB exchange rate (NIOSH/ACGIH) instead of 5 dB (OSHA)*. KTU usually specifies which rule — read carefully!
> 2. *Inverting the formula* — confusing $T_{\text{ref}}$ with $1/T_{\text{ref}}$.
> 3. *Computing the arithmetic mean of dB values* (i.e., $(92+85+80)/3 = 85.7$). This is **incorrect**; dB is a logarithmic quantity — you must use the **dose method** above.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Memorization List (Most Asked in KTU 2024):**

- **Definition:** Industrial Hygiene = *Anticipation, Recognition, Evaluation, Control* (A-R-E-C).
- **Pillars in order:** A → R → E → C. Missing one = incomplete IH program.
- **Hierarchy of Controls (most → least effective):** Elimination > Substitution > Engineering > Administrative > PPE.
- **TWA formula:** $\text{TWA} = \frac{\sum C_i T_i}{8}$.
- **STEL window:** 15 minutes, never to be exceeded.
- **TLV-C (Ceiling):** Never-exceeded instantaneous limit.
- **PEL vs TLV:** PEL is **legally enforceable** (OSHA); TLV is **advisory health-based** (ACGIH).
- **Conversion:** $C_{\text{ppm}} = \frac{24.45 \cdot C_{\text{mg/m}^3}}{M}$ at 25°C, 1 atm.
- **Noise dose (OSHA 5 dB rule):** $D = 100 \times \sum \frac{T_i}{8 / 2^{(L_i-90)/5}}$.
- **Hygienist's role:** Not just measurement — the discipline is **A-R-E-C**, ending in **control action**.
- **Common chemicals with key TLVs to memorize:**
  - Benzene: 0.5 ppm (A1 carcinogen).
  - Silica (crystalline): 0.025 mg/m³ (A2).
  - Carbon monoxide: 25 ppm.
  - Asbestos: 0.1 f/cc.
  - Lead: 0.05 mg/m³ (ACGIH).
- **Ventilation rule of thumb:** LEV hood face velocity = **100–200 ft/min** for dusts, **2000 ft/min** for vapors/gases.
- **Heat stress:** Wet Bulb Globe Temperature (WBGT) used in evaluation; ACGIH TLV = ≤ 30°C for unacclimatized workers.
- **Industrial Hygiene program core outputs:** Risk register, exposure database, control effectiveness audit, continual improvement.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Tip:** In 14-mark questions, always structure your answer as (1) *Statement of principle*, (2) *Mathematical/analytical derivation*, (3) *Numerical substitution*, (4) *Compliance verdict*, (5) *Recommended control*. Examiners allocate **1 mark for the final conclusion and control recommendation** — do not skip it.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Arrangements by Organisations to Protect the Workers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET722 - ENVIRONMENTAL HEALTH AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Arrangements by Organisations to Protect the Workers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Organisational Arrangements for Worker Protection** refer to the structured, hierarchical, and procedural systems established within an enterprise to safeguard the occupational health, safety, and welfare of employees against workplace hazards. These arrangements constitute the *administrative* and *managerial* control measures (as distinguished from engineering or PPE controls) and include the formulation of safety policies, appointment of safety officers, constitution of safety committees, workers' participation in safety decision-making, and adherence to statutory obligations under the **Factories Act, 1948**, the **Occupational Safety, Health and Working Conditions (OSH) Code, 2020**, and **ILO Convention C155 (Occupational Safety and Health Convention, 1981)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic is examined as a *managerial / policy-level* concept in EHS. Examiners expect students to link the *statutory requirement* (Factories Act / OSH Code) with the *organisational structure* (Safety Officer, Safety Committee, Safety Policy) and the *worker participation clause* (Section 41G of Factories Act, 1987 amendment).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Think of an organisation as a **large passenger ship**. The workers are the passengers, the managers are the captain and officers, and the workplace is the ocean — full of icebergs (hazards). Now, a smart captain doesn't just hand out life jackets and hope for the best. Instead, the captain:

1. Writes a **Safety Manual** (Safety Policy),
2. Assigns a **dedicated safety officer** on deck (Safety Officer / EHS Manager),
3. Forms a **crew committee** that meets weekly to discuss threats (Joint Safety Committee),
4. **Drills the passengers** (Safety Training & Induction),
5. Keeps **lifeboats ready and maintained** (Emergency Preparedness & First Aid),
6. Encourages passengers to **report icebergs they spot** (Workers' Participation / Suggestion Schemes).

This entire coordinated system — from policy to drills — is what "organisational arrangements to protect the workers" means. It is **not about tools**; it is about **people, rules, and reporting lines**.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Statutory Numbers (Bolded)

- **Threshold workforce for statutory Safety Officer (Section 40 of Factories Act, 1948):** ≥ **1,000 workers** (or such number as prescribed by State Government rules).
- **Safety Committee (Section 41G, Factories Act amendment 1987):** Mandatory in factories employing **≥ 100 workers**, OR where hazardous processes (per Schedule I) are carried out, regardless of headcount.
- **Equal representation in Safety Committee:** **50% workers' representatives** and **50% management representatives**.
- **OSH Code, 2020 applicability threshold:** Establishments employing **≥ 10 workers** (with certain relaxations for hazardous processes).
- **Frequency of Safety Committee meetings:** At least **once in every 3 months** (quarterly), as per DGFASLI / State Factory Inspectorate guidelines.

> [!NOTE]
> The above numerical thresholds are **favourite KTU 2-mark and 3-mark questions**. Memorise them along with the relevant Section numbers of the Factories Act.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Organisational Worker-Protection Arrangements

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to articulate the protective architecture as a **layered, interdependent system**. The four pillars are:

### Pillar 1 — Safety Policy & Top-Management Commitment
- A **written, signed, dated, and communicated** document at the apex of the organisation.
- Demonstrates *"visible leadership commitment"* (a term specifically used by ILO-OSH 2001 Guidelines).
- Typically includes: statement of intent, responsibilities of supervisors/managers, arrangements for consultation with workers, and commitment to comply with legal requirements and continual improvement.

### Pillar 2 — Safety Organisation Structure
- **Appointment of Safety Officer** (Statutory under Sec. 40 for large factories; voluntary best practice elsewhere).
- Defines the **line of authority** for safety — typically: MD/CEO → Plant Head → EHS Head → Safety Officer → Supervisors → Workers.
- Specifies the **reporting line** (whether the Safety Officer reports to HR, Operations, or directly to MD — best practice is direct line to MD for independence).

### Pillar 3 — Joint Safety Committee (The Participative Engine)
- The **statutory participative body** required under Sec. 41G of the Factories Act, 1987.
- Composition: **Equal number** of workers' and management representatives (typically 50:50 or a slight management bias of one chair).
- Functions: deliberate on safety matters, identify hazards, review accident reports, suggest corrective actions, and promote safety awareness.

### Pillar 4 — Training, Induction & Emergency Preparedness
- **Induction training** for new workers on day one.
- **On-the-job training (OJT)** under supervision.
- **Refresher training** at defined intervals.
- **Mock drills** (fire, chemical spill, evacuation) at least twice a year.
- **First-aid arrangements** (one trained first-aider per shift for every **150 workers**, per State Rules).

## 2.2 The Hierarchy of Controls (Why Organisational Arrangements Sit Where They Do)

The **Hierarchy of Controls** (used internationally by NIOSH, OSHA, and ILO) ranks mitigation measures from most to least effective:

1. **Elimination** (most effective — remove the hazard entirely)
2. **Substitution** (replace with a less hazardous process/substance)
3. **Engineering Controls** (machine guarding, ventilation, interlocks)
4. **Administrative Controls** ← *organisational arrangements live here*
5. **PPE** (Personal Protective Equipment — least reliable, last line)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** When a 14-mark question asks *"Discuss the various measures adopted by an organisation to protect the health and safety of workers"*, the model answer should **explicitly position organisational arrangements in the Administrative Controls band** of the hierarchy, then enumerate policy, structure, committees, training, and emergency preparedness.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since this topic is qualitative, the "formula sheet" below is a **high-yield statutory facts table** — these numbers and Section references appear in almost every KTU question paper.

| **Concept** | **Statutory / Numerical Anchor** | **Source** | **Applicability** |
|-------------|----------------------------------|------------|--------------------|
| Appointment of Safety Officer | Section 40 | Factories Act, 1948 | ≥ 1,000 workers (or as notified) |
| Safety Committee | Section 41G | Factories Act (1987 amendment) | ≥ 100 workers OR hazardous process |
| Committee composition | Equal reps | Sec 41G + DGFASLI guidelines | 50% workers, 50% management |
| Committee meeting frequency | At least 1 in 3 months | State Factory Rules / DGFASLI | Quarterly mandatory |
| First-aider ratio | 1 per 150 workers / shift | State Factory Rules | Every shift must have a trained first-aider |
| Safety Officer qualification | Degree/Diploma in Engg./Tech./Chem. with 2 yrs experience | Sec 40 + State Rules | All appointments after 1987 |
| Worker participation right | Right to refuse unsafe work | Sec 28 (OSH Code 2020) | All covered establishments |
| General duty of occupier | Ensure safety of all persons in factory | Sec 7A, Factories Act | Non-delegable, non-transferable |
| Reporting of accidents | Form 18 / Form 19 | Sec 88, Factories Act | Within 4 hours for fatal / serious |
| Health & Safety Policy | Mandatory written policy | Sec 18 of OSH Code, 2020 | All establishments ≥ 10 workers |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Practice

In the **real industry**, these arrangements translate into tangible deliverables:

- **ISO 45001:2018 (Occupational Health & Safety Management Systems)** explicitly mandates clauses 5.2 (Worker participation) and 5.4 (Consultation and participation) — which are **directly fulfilled** by the Safety Committee and suggestion schemes.
- **ESG (Environmental, Social, Governance) ratings** of listed companies (e.g., from MSCI, Sustainalytics) weight *worker safety governance* heavily under the "S" pillar.
- **Tender eligibility** in sectors like Oil & Gas, Construction, and Power requires submission of a **Safety Policy and Safety Organisation Chart** as part of pre-qualification.
- **Insurance premiums** (Workmen's Compensation Policy) are reduced when an auditable Safety Committee and trained Safety Officer structure exists.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework

Since this topic is **non-mathematical and policy-oriented**, the "derivation" in this context is the **step-by-step logical progression** by which an organisation *constructs* its worker-protection arrangements. This framework is what KTU expects in long-answer (14-mark) responses.

## 3.1 Step 1 — Risk Assessment & Hazard Identification (The Foundation)

Before any arrangement can be designed, the organisation must **know what it is protecting workers from**.

$$\text{Hazard} \rightarrow \text{Risk} \rightarrow \text{Control} \rightarrow \text{Monitoring}$$

- A **Hazard Inventory** is prepared (chemical, physical, biological, ergonomic, psychosocial).
- A **Risk Matrix** (Likelihood × Severity) is applied.
- The output is a list of *"significant hazards"* requiring control measures.

## 3.2 Step 2 — Drafting the Safety Policy

The policy is the **cornerstone document** of all subsequent arrangements.

A KTU-acceptable Safety Policy should contain (memorise this checklist):

- A clear **statement of intent** by the Chief Executive.
- The **scope** of the policy (entire company / specific site).
- **Compliance commitment** with all applicable laws and regulations.
- A commitment to **continual improvement**.
- A commitment to **consultation and participation** of workers.
- The **date, signature of CEO**, and **review date**.

> [!NOTE]
> A common KTU pitfall: students describe the policy as just *"a document on the wall"*. The correct answer emphasises that the policy must be **communicated to, and understood by, every worker** — translation into local languages, inclusion in induction, and periodic reinforcement.

## 3.3 Step 3 — Designing the Safety Organisation Structure

The structure is a **chart** that defines *who is responsible for what*. The typical hierarchy is:

| **Level** | **Role** | **Primary Safety Responsibility** |
|-----------|----------|------------------------------------|
| 1 | Managing Director / CEO | Ultimate accountability |
| 2 | Plant Head / Factory Manager | Operational responsibility |
| 3 | EHS Head / Safety Manager | Strategic & advisory |
| 4 | Safety Officer | Day-to-day implementation, inspection, training |
| 5 | Supervisors / Foremen | Direct control over work gangs |
| 6 | Workers | Compliance, hazard reporting, PPE use |

**Independence principle:** Best practice is that the Safety Officer has a **dual reporting line** — administratively to the Plant Head, but functionally with **direct access to the MD**. This prevents the *"production vs safety conflict"*.

## 3.4 Step 4 — Constitution of the Joint Safety Committee

This is the **statutory participative body** that brings workers and management together.

**Step 4a — Determine applicability**
- Does the factory employ ≥ 100 workers, OR carry out any process listed in Schedule I (hazardous processes) of the Factories Act? If yes, the committee is **mandatory**.

**Step 4b — Determine composition**
- Number of members: As prescribed by State Rules (typically 8 to 16 total).
- **Workers' representatives:** Elected by workers from amongst themselves (not nominated by management).
- **Management's representatives:** Nominated by occupier (factory owner).
- **Chairperson:** Nominated by the occupier (or by the committee, depending on State Rules).

**Step 4c — Define agenda & frequency**
- Meet **at least once every three months**.
- Agenda includes: review of accidents/near-misses, status of corrective actions, review of safety audit findings, worker grievances, upcoming hazardous operations, training plan.

**Step 4d — Record-keeping**
- **Minutes of every meeting** must be recorded.
- Action items tracked to closure.
- Copies of minutes sent to the **Chief Inspector of Factories** (as required by some State Rules).

## 3.5 Step 5 — Training, Induction & Drills (The Capability Layer)

$$\text{Competence} = \text{Knowledge} + \text{Skills} + \text{Attitude}$$

- **Induction training** (first day): general safety rules, emergency procedures, PPE issue, site-specific hazards.
- **Job-specific training** (before assignment): operating procedures, permit-to-work systems, machine guarding.
- **Refresher training** (at least annually, or after any incident).
- **Mock drills** (fire, evacuation, chemical release — at least half-yearly).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The principle *"Train people well enough so they can work safely; train them again whenever equipment, process, or regulations change"* is the KTU-favoured articulation.

## 3.6 Step 6 — Emergency Preparedness & Response

Even with all the above, accidents can happen. The organisation must have a **written Emergency Action Plan (EAP)** containing:

- **On-site emergency team** with a defined Incident Commander.
- **Communication tree** (who calls whom in what order).
- **Evacuation routes** marked and unblocked.
- **Assembly points** designated.
- **External coordination** with fire services, hospitals, police, district disaster authority.
- **Spill containment kits**, fire extinguishers, eyewash stations, and **first-aid boxes** at defined locations.

## 3.7 Step 7 — Monitoring, Audit & Continual Improvement

The **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) cycle** of ISO 45001 is the modern articulation:

$$\text{Plan} \rightarrow \text{Do} \rightarrow \text{Check} \rightarrow \text{Act} \rightarrow \text{(loop back to Plan)}$$

- **Check** is achieved through inspections, audits, incident investigations, and near-miss reporting.
- **Act** translates lessons into revised procedures, training, and engineering changes.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Organisational Safety Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Board of Directors / MD] --> B[Plant Head / Factory Manager]
    B --> C[EHS Manager / Safety Head]
    C --> D[Safety Officer - Statutory]
    C --> E[Medical Officer / OH Officer]
    C --> F[Fire and Emergency Officer]
    D --> G[Shift Supervisors / Foremen]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Workforce / Workers]
    A --> I[Signed Safety Policy Document]
    I --> B
    B --> J[Joint Safety Committee - Sec 41G]
    J --> H
    J --> G
    J --> C
    K[External Stakeholders] --> A
    K[External Stakeholders] --> J
    L[Chief Inspector of Factories CIF] --> J
    M[Emergency Response Team] --> H
    M --> G
    N[Training and Induction Cell] --> H
    N --> G
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: How a Hazard Report Travels Through the System

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Worker Identifies Hazard] --> B[Reports to Supervisor]
    B --> C[Supervisor Logs in Safety Register]
    C --> D[Forwarded to Safety Officer]
    D --> E[Risk Assessment Done]
    E --> F{Risk Level}
    F -->|Low| G[Quick Action by Supervisor]
    F -->|Medium| H[Discussed in Safety Committee]
    F -->|High| I[Immediate Stop Work + MD Notification]
    H --> J[Corrective Action Tracked to Closure]
    I --> J
    G --> J
    J --> K[Feedback to Original Reporter]
    K --> A
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Hierarchy of Controls with Organisational Arrangements Highlighted

```mermaid

    L1[Level 1 - Elimination - Most Effective] --> L2[Level 2 - Substitution]
    L2 --> L3[Level 3 - Engineering Controls]
    L3 --> L4[Level 4 - Administrative Controls]
    L4 --> L5[Level 5 - PPE - Least Effective]
    L4 --> M1[Safety Policy and Procedures]

    style L4 fill:#ffd700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px
    style M1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style M2 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style M3 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style M4 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style M5 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading guide for the diagram:** The yellow-highlighted Level 4 box is where the *organisational arrangements* of this module reside. Engineering controls (Level 3) and PPE (Level 5) belong to other modules — the examiner expects you to know that this topic is **specifically** about the *administrative/managerial* layer, not the tools or the machines.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper Set B)]

**What is a Joint Safety Committee? State its composition as per the Factories Act, 1948.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Joint Safety Committee** is a statutory body mandated under **Section 41G of the Factories Act, 1948** (inserted by the 1987 amendment) for every factory employing **100 or more workers**, or carrying out any process declared hazardous under **Schedule I**, irrespective of worker count.

**Composition:**
- The committee consists of an **equal number** of representatives from workers and management.
- **Workers' representatives** are to be **elected by the workers themselves** from amongst their peers (not nominated by management).
- **Management's representatives** are nominated by the occupier.
- The committee meets **at least once every three months** and maintains minutes of its proceedings.
*(Award 1 mark each for: definition, Section 41G, and composition/meeting frequency.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**List any four duties of a Safety Officer in a factory.**

**Model Answer (4 × 0.75 = 3 marks):**

1. **Advise the factory management** on measures to ensure safety and health of workers.
2. **Inspect** the factory at least **once a week** (or as prescribed) and report findings to the management.
3. **Investigate** accidents and near-miss incidents, identify root causes, and recommend corrective actions.
4. **Conduct safety training** programmes, including induction training for new workers and refresher training for existing ones.
5. *(Any other valid duty such as: maintain accident register, coordinate with Safety Committee, liaise with Factory Inspector, advise on PPE selection.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Question (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

### Question A — [14 Marks, Internal Choice Module 1]

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
> **(a)** Discuss in detail the various **arrangements made by an organisation to protect its workers** under the Factories Act, 1948. *(7 marks)*
> **(b)** Explain the **constitution, functions, and statutory requirements** of a Joint Safety Committee. *(7 marks)*

#### Part A(a) Model Answer (7 marks)

Organisations, particularly those governed by the **Factories Act, 1948**, are required to establish a multi-layered system of arrangements to protect workers. These arrangements operate at the **administrative level** of the *Hierarchy of Controls* and include:

1. **Safety Policy (1 mark):** A written, signed, dated, and communicated document at the top-management level, expressing the company's commitment to occupational health and safety.

2. **Safety Organisation (1 mark):** A defined hierarchy of safety responsibilities — from the Managing Director (ultimate accountability) to supervisors and workers — with the **Safety Officer** as the pivot for implementation.

3. **Appointment of Safety Officer (1 mark):** Statutorily required under **Section 40** for factories employing **≥ 1,000 workers** (or as notified by State Government), with prescribed qualifications and the duty to inspect, advise, investigate, and train.

4. **Joint Safety Committee (1 mark):** Required under **Section 41G** for factories with **≥ 100 workers** or carrying out hazardous processes, providing the participative forum for workers and management.

5. **Training and Induction (1 mark):** Mandatory induction training for new workers, on-the-job training, and refresher courses to ensure competence in safe work practices.

6. **Emergency Preparedness (1 mark):** Written Emergency Action Plan, mock drills, first-aid arrangements (1 first-aider per 150 workers per shift), fire-fighting equipment, and external coordination with emergency services.

7. **Statutory Reporting and Records (1 mark):** Reporting of accidents as per **Section 88** (Form 18 for injury, Form 19 for dangerous occurrences), maintenance of accident register, and compliance with inspection requirements of the Factory Inspectorate.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part A(a):**
> Students often write only *"Safety Committee and Safety Officer"* and forget the other five elements. To get full 7 marks, **you must mention at least 6 distinct arrangements** with their **statutory Section numbers**. A bare list without citing the Factories Act sections will fetch only 3–4 marks.

---

#### Part A(b) Model Answer (7 marks)

**Constitution of the Joint Safety Committee (3 marks):**
- Established under **Section 41G of the Factories Act, 1948** (inserted by the 1987 amendment).
- **Applicability:** Every factory employing **100 or more workers**, or carrying out any process listed in **Schedule I (hazardous processes)**, regardless of worker strength.
- **Composition:** **Equal representation** — 50% from workers and 50% from management.
- **Workers' representatives** are **elected by the workers** themselves.
- **Management's representatives** are nominated by the **occupier**.
- The **Chairperson** is typically nominated by the occupier (varies by State Rules).
- Total strength: 8 to 16 members (as per State Rules).

**Statutory Requirements (2 marks):**
- The committee must meet **at least once in every three months** (quarterly).
- **Minutes of every meeting** must be recorded and maintained.
- A copy of the minutes must be sent to the **Chief Inspector of Factories** (as required by State Rules).
- The committee's tenure, quorum, and procedures are governed by the State Factory Rules and the **DGFASLI (Directorate General Factory Advice Service & Labour Institutes)** guidelines.

**Functions (2 marks):**
1. **Review of accidents and dangerous occurrences** occurring in the factory.
2. **Discussion of safety measures** and recommendation of corrective actions.
3. **Promotion of safety awareness** among workers through campaigns and training.
4. **Examination of safety audit reports** and follow-up on compliance.
5. **Worker grievance redressal** related to safety and health.
6. **Consultation on hazardous operations** and any new process / machinery being introduced.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part A(b):**
> A very common error is writing *"the committee is constituted by the management"* for the workers' side. The **statute explicitly requires workers' representatives to be ELECTED by workers** — management nomination on the workers' side is **illegal** and will cost you at least 1 mark. Also, do not confuse the **100-worker threshold** of Sec. 41G with the **1,000-worker threshold** of Sec. 40 (Safety Officer). Mixing these up is a favourite KTU trick question.

---

### Question B — Alternative Choice (14 Marks) [Internal Choice]

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
> **(a)** Explain the **duties and qualifications of a Safety Officer** appointed under the Factories Act, 1948. *(7 marks)*
> **(b)** Write a detailed note on the **role of training and emergency preparedness** in protecting workers, with reference to relevant statutory provisions. *(7 marks)*

#### Part B(a) Model Answer (7 marks)

**Statutory Provision (1 mark):** **Section 40 of the Factories Act, 1948** mandates the appointment of a Safety Officer in factories employing **1,000 or more workers**, or such number as the State Government may notify.

**Qualifications of a Safety Officer (2 marks):**
- A **degree in Engineering or Technology** with **practical experience of at least 2 years** in a supervisory capacity in a factory, OR
- A **degree in Physics or Chemistry** with **2 years of supervisory experience**, OR
- A **diploma in Engineering or Technology** with **5 years of supervisory experience** in a factory.
- The State Government may relax these in special cases.

**Duties of a Safety Officer (4 marks):**
1. **Advise** the occupier, factory manager, and workers on measures to ensure safety and health.
2. **Inspect** the factory at least **once a week** (or as prescribed) and bring defects to the notice of the management.
3. **Investigate** all accidents (including near-misses) and recommend preventive measures.
4. **Maintain** records of inspections, accidents, and safety-related statistics.
5. **Conduct** safety training programmes and coordinate with the Safety Committee.
6. **Liaise** with the Factory Inspectorate and external authorities on safety matters.
7. **Promote** the participation of workers in safety activities and the use of PPE.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part B(a):**
> Students often **write "7 years" or "10 years"** of experience. The correct period is **2 years** (for degree holders) and **5 years** (for diploma holders). Also, do not write *"a Safety Officer is appointed in every factory"* — the law applies only **above 1,000 workers (or as notified)**. This distinction is heavily tested.

---

#### Part B(b) Model Answer (7 marks)

**Importance of Training (3 marks):**

Training is the **bridge between documented procedures and safe work behaviour**. It is a **statutory requirement** under the Factories Act, 1948 (Sections 11, 11A, 11B, 18, and 19 cover working hours, employment of young persons, and notice of periods of work for adult workers, with safety training implications).

**Types of training (2 marks):**
1. **Induction training** — given on the first day a worker joins; covers general safety rules, PPE, emergency procedures, and statutory rights.
2. **On-the-job training (OJT)** — under close supervision, on specific machines, processes, and permit-to-work systems.
3. **Refresher training** — at periodic intervals (typically annually) or whenever a process, machine, or regulation changes.
4. **Specialised training** — for high-risk operations (e.g., confined-space entry, hot work, working at heights, chemical handling).

**Emergency Preparedness (2 marks):**
- A **written Emergency Action Plan (EAP)** must be in place, identifying the Incident Commander, communication tree, evacuation routes, assembly points, and external emergency contacts (fire, hospital, police, district disaster authority).
- **Mock drills** must be conducted at least **twice a year** for fire and evacuation, and more frequently for hazardous processes.
- **First-aid arrangements:** As per State Factory Rules, **one trained first-aider per 150 workers per shift** is the minimum.
- Periodic inspection and maintenance of fire extinguishers, eyewash stations, spill kits, and emergency lighting.
- The **OSH Code, 2020 (Sec 22)** and the **National Disaster Management Act, 2005** also impose obligations on establishments to maintain emergency preparedness.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part B(b):**
> Many students write *"first-aid box is kept"* — this is too vague. You must state the **statutory ratio (1:150 per shift)**, the **mock drill frequency (twice a year)**, and the **legal anchor (Factories Act / State Rules / OSH Code 2020)**. Vague answers attract 2–3 marks at most.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Statutory anchors to memorise:** Factories Act **Section 40** (Safety Officer, ≥ 1,000 workers) and **Section 41G** (Safety Committee, ≥ 100 workers OR hazardous process).
- **Composition principle:** Safety Committee has **equal representation**; workers' representatives are **elected**, not nominated.
- **Meeting frequency:** At least **once in three months**; minutes must be recorded.
- **First-aider ratio:** **1 per 150 workers per shift** (State Factory Rules).
- **Accident reporting:** Form 18 (injury), Form 19 (dangerous occurrence) under Section 88 of the Factories Act.
- **OSH Code 2020 Section 18:** Mandates a written **Safety Policy** for establishments with ≥ 10 workers.
- **ISO 45001:2018** clauses 5.2 and 5.4 codify **worker participation and consultation** — a direct parallel to the Safety Committee concept.
- **Hierarchy of Controls** positioning: Organisational arrangements = **Level 4 (Administrative Controls)**, *not* engineering or PPE.
- **Independence principle:** The Safety Officer should have **direct functional access to the MD** to avoid production-safety conflict.
- **Five-part protective architecture (for any 7-mark answer):** Policy + Structure + Safety Officer + Safety Committee + Training + Emergency Preparedness + Statutory Reporting.
- **Common confusions to avoid:**
  - 1,000 workers → Safety Officer (Sec 40) — *NOT* 100.
  - 100 workers → Safety Committee (Sec 41G) — *NOT* 1,000.
  - Workers' reps in committee are *elected* — *NOT* nominated.
  - Training is a *statutory* obligation — *NOT* a discretionary management benefit.
- **One-liner to remember the topic:** *"Organisations protect workers not by tools alone, but by the triad of Policy, People, and Participation — codified in law, executed through committees, and sustained by training."*

---

> **End of Module 1 Notes — OECET722 Environmental Health and Safety | KTU 2024 Scheme**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chemical Hazards - Dust, Fumes, Vapour, Fog, Gases
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET722 - ENVIRONMENTAL HEALTH AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Chemical Hazards - Dust, Fumes, Vapour, Fog, Gases

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Chemical Hazard** is any substance—solid, liquid, or gas—that has the potential to cause harm to human health, property, or the environment through its intrinsic chemical properties (toxicity, flammability, corrosivity, reactivity) or via physical state-dependent exposure routes (inhalation, ingestion, dermal absorption).

In the context of occupational health and industrial hygiene, airborne chemical hazards are classified into **five physical-state categories** based on the aerodynamic diameter of the suspended particulate or the thermodynamic phase of the dispersing medium:

| Category | Physical State | Typical Aerodynamic Diameter |
|----------|---------------|------------------------------|
| Dust | Solid (suspended) | $0.1 \text{ to } 100 \text{ }\mu m$ |
| Fume | Solid (condensed vapor) | $0.001 \text{ to } 1.0 \text{ }\mu m$ |
| Vapour | Gaseous (from liquid/solid) | Molecular |
| Fog | Liquid (condensed) | $1.0 \text{ to } 100 \text{ }\mu m$ |
| Gas | Gaseous | Molecular |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The classification of airborne chemical hazards by their **physical state of dispersion** is a fundamental concept tested regularly. Examiners expect students to differentiate between particles formed by **mechanical subdivision** (dust) versus those formed by **physical condensation** (fume, mist, fog).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are walking through a busy industrial workshop. Notice the air around you—it is never truly "empty." It is a dynamic mixture of:

- **Floating sawdust** from cutting wood (mechanical → **Dust**)
- **Smoke** rising from a freshly welded metal joint (condensation → **Fumes**)
- **The smell of petrol** wafting from a leaking drum (liquid-to-gas → **Vapour**)
- **Misty droplets** hovering over a hot acid bath near a pickling line (liquid dispersion → **Fog/Mist**)
- **An invisible, odourless hazard** seeping from a faulty valve (true gas phase → **Gas**)

Think of these five hazards as **five different "delivery vehicles"** for the same toxic chemical. A worker can be poisoned by arsenic whether it arrives as a dust, fume, vapour, fog, or gas—but the **physics of how it travels, settles, deposits in the lungs, and is controlled** is completely different. This is why industrial hygienists must identify the *form*, not just the *substance*.

### 1.3 Why Physical State Matters — The Respiration Pathway

The human respiratory system is a **size-selective filter**:

$$D_{aerodynamic} \downarrow \implies \text{deposition depth} \uparrow \implies \text{systemic toxicity} \uparrow$$

- Particles $> 10 \text{ }\mu m$ → trapped in the **nose and upper airways** (nasopharyngeal region).
- Particles $2.5 \text{ to } 10 \text{ }\mu m$ → deposited in **bronchi and bronchioles** (tracheobronchial region).
- Particles $< 2.5 \text{ }\mu m$ (especially fumes) → reach the **alveolar sacs** (gas-exchange region) → enter the bloodstream.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Fumes are the *most dangerous* airborne form because their sub-micron size allows them to bypass nearly all mechanical defences of the respiratory system.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Classification of Airborne Chemical Hazards

#### 2.1.1 DUST

**Definition:** Dust is a **solid particulate** generated by the **mechanical subdivision of a parent solid material** through processes such as crushing, grinding, drilling, blasting, sieving, or abrasion.

- The **chemical composition of the dust = the chemical composition of the parent solid**.
- Size range: typically $0.1 \text{ to } 100 \text{ }\mu m$ (respirable fraction: $< 10 \text{ }\mu m$).
- Examples: silica dust (mining, sandblasting), coal dust, grain dust, wood dust, cotton dust (byssinosis), asbestos fibre dust, cement dust.

**Why it forms:** Mechanical energy overcomes the cohesive forces holding the bulk solid together; particles break off at stress-concentration points (grain boundaries, micro-cracks).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter for KTU?** Examiners often test whether the student can recognize that *fume ≠ dust*. Dust is **mechanical** in origin; fume is **thermodynamic** (condensation).

#### 2.1.2 FUMES

**Definition:** Fumes are **solid particles formed by the condensation of vapours** when a molten metal, hot substance, or vapour-phase chemical **cools below its sublimation/condensation temperature**.

- Size range: $0.001 \text{ to } 1.0 \text{ }\mu m$ (very fine; often agglomerates into chains).
- Composition: usually **metal oxides** (because the parent metal reacts with atmospheric oxygen at high temperature before condensing).
- Examples: welding fumes (iron oxide, manganese oxide, chromium oxide fumes), lead fumes from smelting, zinc oxide fumes (brass foundry work → "metal fume fever"), cadmium oxide fumes.

**Why it forms:** Process: $Metal_{(l)} \xrightarrow{\Delta} Vapour \xrightarrow{O_2, \text{cool}} Metal\,Oxide_{(s)} \rightarrow$ suspended as fume.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Point:** Fumes oxidize in air. So "lead fume" reaching the worker's lung is actually **lead oxide (PbO)** or **lead sub-oxide (Pb₂O)** — a more bioavailable toxicant than the parent metal.

#### 2.1.3 VAPOUR

**Definition:** Vapour is the **gaseous phase of a substance that exists as a liquid or solid at standard room temperature and atmospheric pressure** ($25 °C$, $101.325 \text{ kPa}$).

- Vapour can be re-condensed back to the original liquid/solid by **increasing pressure or lowering temperature** (true vapours obey the Clausius–Clapeyron relation).
- Examples: organic solvent vapours (benzene, toluene, xylene, acetone, trichloroethylene), mercury vapour at room temperature, water vapour, petrol vapour, naphthalene vapour.

**Key concept — Vapour Pressure:** A measure of a liquid's tendency to evaporate. The higher the vapour pressure at a given temperature, the more readily the substance forms a vapour hazard.

$$P_{vap} \propto e^{-\Delta H_{vap}/RT}$$

This is the **Clausius–Clapeyron relation** (integrated form):

$$\ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2}-\frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

#### 2.1.4 FOG (and MIST)

**Definition:** Fog (in occupational hygiene, often called **mist**) is a **suspension of fine liquid droplets in air**, generated by **atomization, condensation of vapour on nuclei, or splashing** of a bulk liquid.

- Size range: $1.0 \text{ to } 100 \text{ }\mu m$ (larger than fume; visible to the naked eye as a cloud).
- Examples: sulfuric acid mist (from pickling baths), chromic acid mist (electroplating), cutting oil mist (machining), paint spray mist, pesticide spray mist, sea fog, and exhaled breath on a cold day.

**Why it forms:** Two distinct mechanisms:
1. **Mechanical atomization** (spraying, bubbling): bulk liquid → droplets via shear force.
2. **Condensation fog:** hot vapour meets cool air → supersaturation → nucleation of droplets on condensation nuclei (e.g., dust, ions).

> [!NOTE]
> **Fine Distinction:** "Mist" and "fog" are often used interchangeably in industrial hygiene. OSHA terminology treats mist as a subset of fog-like dispersions formed by condensation of vapour or atomization of liquid.

#### 2.1.5 GAS

**Definition:** A gas is a substance that **exists in the gaseous state at standard temperature and pressure (STP: $0 °C$, $101.325 \text{ kPa}$)** and remains gaseous upon release into the workplace. Unlike vapours, gases cannot be liquefied by compression alone at room temperature (some require both pressure *and* cooling).

- Examples: oxygen, nitrogen, carbon monoxide (CO), hydrogen sulfide (H₂S), chlorine (Cl₂), ammonia (NH₃), sulfur dioxide (SO₂), ozone (O₃), methane (CH₄), hydrogen cyanide (HCN).

**Critical sub-classification for safety:**

| Property | Definition | Examples |
|----------|------------|----------|
| Toxic gas | Causes systemic harm at low concentration | H₂S, HCN, Cl₂, CO |
| Asphyxiant gas | Displaces O₂ (simple) or blocks O₂ utilization (chemical) | N₂, CH₄, CO |
| Flammable gas | Ignites in air within flammable range (LEL–UEL) | H₂, CH₄, propane |
| Corrosive gas | Reacts with biological tissue | HCl, SO₂, Cl₂ |

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol / Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|-------------------|----------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Stokes' Law (settling velocity) | $v_s = \dfrac{d^2 (\rho_p - \rho) g}{18 \mu}$ | Predicts respirable dust deposition depth |
| 2 | Aerodynamic diameter | $d_{ae} = d_p \sqrt{\rho_p / \rho_0}$ | Compares particles of different densities |
| 3 | Mass concentration | $C = \dfrac{m}{V}$ (mg/m³) | Industrial hygiene exposure metric |
| 4 | Volume concentration (ppm) | $\text{ppm} = \dfrac{V_{gas}}{V_{air}} \times 10^6$ | Toxic gas limit expression |
| 5 | ppm ↔ mg/m³ conversion | $C_{mg/m^3} = \dfrac{\text{ppm} \times MW}{24.45}$ (at $25 °C$) | Compare gas and dust standards |
| 6 | TLV-TWA (8-hr) | mg/m³ or ppm | Permissible exposure limit (ACGIH) |
| 7 | STEL (15-min) | mg/m³ or ppm | Short-term excursion limit |
| 8 | IDLH | ppm or mg/m³ | Immediately dangerous to life or health |
| 9 | Clausius–Clapeyron (vapour) | $\ln(P_2/P_1) = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}(1/T_2 - 1/T_1)$ | Predicts vapour hazard from spills |
| 10 | LEL and UEL | % by volume | Defines flammable gas hazard envelope |
| 11 | Respirable fraction | $D_{ae} < 10 \text{ }\mu m$ | Reaches alveolar region of lungs |
| 12 | TLV for nuisance dust | $10 \text{ mg/m}^3$ (total), $3 \text{ mg/m}^3$ (respirable) | KTU standard reference |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mandate:** Memorize the conversion formula $C_{mg/m^3} = \dfrac{\text{ppm} \times MW}{24.45}$. It is the single most-tested equation in occupational hygiene numerical problems.

### 2.3 Why Each Hazard Type Demands a Different Control Strategy

| Hazard | Primary Control Strategy | Engineering Utility |
|--------|--------------------------|---------------------|
| Dust | Local exhaust ventilation (LEV) + HEPA filter; wet suppression | Mining, cement industry |
| Fume | LEV at the source (capture-at-source hood); welding fume extractor; powered air-purifying respirator (PAPR) | Welding, smelting, foundry |
| Vapour | Closed-system handling; LEV; activated carbon adsorption; refrigeration condensation | Solvent-based paint shops, dry cleaning |
| Fog / Mist | LEV with mist eliminator (demister pad); fume scrubber; sub-micron filtration | Electroplating, sulfuric acid plants |
| Gas | Continuous gas detection; LEV; dilution ventilation; SCBA; chemical scrubber | Confined-space entry, fertilizer plants, refrigeration |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Takeaway:** Because gas and vapour are molecular, **filtration media (HEPA) are ineffective** for them. They require **sorption** (activated carbon, zeolite) or **destruction** (thermal oxidizer, scrubber). This is a classic KTU conceptual question.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Stokes' Law (Particle Settling Velocity)

The terminal settling velocity of a spherical particle in quiescent air determines how long it remains airborne and how deep it penetrates the respiratory system.

**Step 1 — Force balance on a falling particle:**

Three forces act on the particle in steady-state descent:
- Gravitational force (downward): $F_g = m_p \, g = \dfrac{\pi d_p^3}{6} \, \rho_p \, g$
- Buoyancy force (upward): $F_b = \dfrac{\pi d_p^3}{6} \, \rho \, g$
- Viscous drag force (upward, Stokes' regime): $F_d = 3 \pi \mu \, d_p \, v_s$

**Step 2 — At terminal velocity, acceleration = 0, so:**

$$F_g = F_b + F_d$$

**Step 3 — Substituting the force expressions:**

$$\frac{\pi d_p^3}{6} \rho_p \, g = \frac{\pi d_p^3}{6} \rho \, g + 3 \pi \mu \, d_p \, v_s$$

**Step 4 — Rearranging for $v_s$:**

$$\frac{\pi d_p^3}{6} (\rho_p - \rho) \, g = 3 \pi \mu \, d_p \, v_s$$

**Step 5 — Divide both sides by $3 \pi \mu \, d_p$:**

$$v_s = \frac{d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho) \, g}{18 \, \mu}$$

This is **Stokes' Law**, valid for $Re_p < 0.1$ (laminar flow regime, which is appropriate for particles $< 100 \text{ }\mu m$ in air).

**Step 6 — Practical interpretation for occupational hygiene:**

$$\boxed{v_s \propto d_p^2 \cdot \rho_p}$$

A $10 \text{ }\mu m$ silica particle settles roughly $100 \times$ faster than a $1 \text{ }\mu m$ fume particle (assuming similar density). This is why fumes stay airborne for hours and reach the alveolar region, while larger dust particles settle in the nasopharyngeal region.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: ppm ↔ mg/m³ Conversion

A classic KTU numerical problem. Derivation at **NTP ($25 °C$, $1 \text{ atm}$)**:

**Step 1 — From the ideal gas law, the molar volume at NTP:**

$$V_m = \frac{RT}{P} = \frac{(0.0821 \text{ L·atm/mol·K})(298.15 \text{ K})}{1 \text{ atm}} = 24.45 \text{ L/mol}$$

**Step 2 — Express 1 ppm in volumetric ratio:**

$$1 \text{ ppm} = \frac{1 \text{ L (gas)}}{10^6 \text{ L (air)}}$$

**Step 3 — For a gas of molecular weight $MW$ (g/mol), the mass per litre at NTP:**

$$\frac{MW \text{ g}}{24.45 \text{ L}} \text{ is the mass concentration of 1 mol/L pure gas}$$

**Step 4 — Therefore, 1 ppm of gas in air has mass concentration:**

$$C = \frac{1}{10^6} \times \frac{MW}{24.45} \times 10^6 \text{ mg/m}^3 \text{ (after unit conversion)}$$

**Step 5 — Final formula (concentration in mg/m³):**

$$\boxed{C_{mg/m^3} = \frac{\text{ppm} \times MW}{24.45}}$$

### 3.3 Worked Example — KTU Numerical

> **Problem:** Carbon monoxide (CO, $MW = 28 \text{ g/mol}$) is measured in a parking garage at $35 \text{ ppm}$. Convert this to mg/m³ and compare with the OSHA Permissible Exposure Limit (PEL = $55 \text{ mg/m}^3$).

**Solution:**

$$C_{mg/m^3} = \frac{35 \times 28}{24.45} = \frac{980}{24.45} \approx 40.08 \text{ mg/m}^3$$

Comparison: $40.08 \text{ mg/m}^3 < 55 \text{ mg/m}^3$ (PEL). The garage is **below the OSHA 8-hr TWA limit**, but corrective action is still warranted because CO has a *ceiling limit* of $200 \text{ ppm}$ and the environment is not yet stabilized.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — The Five Hazards

| Property | Dust | Fume | Vapour | Fog / Mist | Gas |
|----------|------|------|--------|------------|-----|
| Phase (parent) | Solid | Solid (molten parent) | Liquid or solid | Liquid | Gas |
| Phase (dispersed) | Solid | Solid | Gas | Liquid droplet | Gas |
| Origin mechanism | Mechanical | Thermal/condensation | Evaporation | Atomization or condensation | Already gaseous |
| Typical size | $0.1 - 100 \text{ }\mu m$ | $0.001 - 1 \text{ }\mu m$ | Molecular | $1 - 100 \text{ }\mu m$ | Molecular |
| Settles by gravity? | Yes | Negligibly | No | Yes (large drops) | No (Brownian) |
| Visible to naked eye? | Yes (large) | Often yes (smoke) | No | Yes (cloud) | No (sometimes colour) |
| Pulmonary deposition | Nasopharyngeal–bronchial | Alveolar | Pulmonary absorption | Nasopharyngeal–bronchial | Pulmonary absorption |
| HEPA filter effective? | Yes | Yes (with HEPA) | No | Partial | No |
| Activated carbon? | No | No | Yes | No | Yes (for some) |
| TLV unit | mg/m³ | mg/m³ | ppm or mg/m³ | mg/m³ | ppm or mg/m³ |
| KTU-typical example | Silica in quarry | Welding fume (Fe₂O₃) | Benzene in tank | H₂SO₄ in pickling | CO in furnace house |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Conceptual Test:** Given an airborne hazard, identify whether it is a dust, fume, vapour, fog, or gas. The discriminators are: (a) **mechanism of generation**, (b) **physical state of parent**, and (c) **physical state of dispersed phase**.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Classification of Airborne Chemical Hazards

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Airborne Chemical Hazards] --> B[Solid Dispersed Phase]
    A --> C[Liquid Dispersed Phase]
    A --> D[Gaseous Dispersed Phase]
    B --> B1[Dust]
    B --> B2[Fume]
    C --> C1[Fog / Mist]
    D --> D1[Vapour]
    D --> D2[Gas]
    B1 --> B1a[Mechanical subdivision of solid]
    B1 --> B1b[Size 0.1 to 100 micrometer]
    B1 --> B1c[Example: silica dust]
    B2 --> B2a[Condensation of metal vapour]
    B2 --> B2b[Size 0.001 to 1 micrometer]
    B2 --> B2c[Example: welding fume]
    C1 --> C1a[Atomization or condensation of liquid]
    C1 --> C1b[Size 1 to 100 micrometer]
    C1 --> C1c[Example: sulfuric acid mist]
    D1 --> D1a[Evaporation of liquid or solid]
    D1 --> D1b[Molecular dispersion]
    D1 --> D1c[Example: benzene vapour]
    D2 --> D2a[Released already gaseous at STP]
    D2 --> D2b[Molecular dispersion]
    D2 --> D2c[Example: chlorine gas]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flow Diagram: Generation Mechanism vs. Control Strategy

```mermaid
graph LR
    H[Hazard Source] --> P1[Mechanical Process]
    H --> P2[Thermal Process]
    H --> P3[Liquid Handling]
    H --> P4[Pressurized Gas Release]
    P1 --> D[Dust]
    P2 --> F[Fume]
    P3 --> V[Vapour]
    P3 --> G1[Fog or Mist]
    P4 --> G[Gas]
    D --> C1[LEV with HEPA filter]
    F --> C2[Capture at source hood with PAPR]
    V --> C3[Closed system plus carbon adsorption]
    G1 --> C4[Demister pad plus scrubber]
    G --> C5[Continuous gas monitor plus SCBA]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Pulmonary Deposition by Particle Size

```mermaid
graph TD
    S[Inhaled Particle] --> S1[Size greater than 10 micrometer]
    S --> S2[Size 2.5 to 10 micrometer]
    S --> S3[Size 1 to 2.5 micrometer]
    S --> S4[Size less than 1 micrometer]
    S1 --> R1[Nasopharyngeal region]
    S2 --> R2[Tracheobronchial region]
    S3 --> R3[Alveolar region entry]
    S4 --> R4[Deep alveolar sacs plus systemic entry]
    R1 --> E1[Exhaled or swallowed]
    R2 --> E2[Mucociliary clearance]
    R3 --> E3[Macrophage clearance]
    R4 --> E4[Bloodstream absorption plus systemic toxicity]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The above block diagram explains why fumes (size $< 1 \text{ }\mu m$) cause systemic diseases like **metal fume fever**, **welder's siderosis**, and **chronic manganism**—the particles bypass nearly all mechanical pulmonary defences.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**
*Distinguish between **dust** and **fume** as airborne chemical hazards. Give one industrial example of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Dust | Fume |
|---------|------|------|
| Origin | Mechanical subdivision of a solid | Condensation of vapour from a molten/hot material |
| Composition | Same as parent solid | Usually a metal oxide (formed by oxidation during condensation) |
| Particle size | $0.1 \text{ to } 100 \text{ }\mu m$ | $0.001 \text{ to } 1.0 \text{ }\mu m$ (sub-micron) |
| Example | Silica dust in quarrying/cement plant | Welding fume (Fe₂O₃) in fabrication shop |

(1 mark each for: mechanism, composition, size + example, 3 marks total)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
*Define **Threshold Limit Value – Time Weighted Average (TLV-TWA)**. Why is the conversion of ppm to mg/m³ important for occupational exposure assessment?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** TLV-TWA is the **time-weighted average concentration** of a chemical in workplace air for a normal **8-hour workday and 40-hour workweek**, to which nearly all workers may be repeatedly exposed day after day without adverse health effect (ACGIH definition).

**Why conversion matters (1.5 marks):**
- Gas and vapour standards are usually expressed in **ppm** (volumetric).
- Dust and mist standards are usually expressed in **mg/m³** (gravimetric).
- To compare exposures across physical states, a **common unit is required**.
- The formula $C_{mg/m^3} = \dfrac{\text{ppm} \times MW}{24.45}$ (at $25 °C$) enables direct comparison.
- Industrial hygiene compliance requires a single benchmark to enforce the **most restrictive applicable TLV**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **Note (KTU 2024 Pattern):** Each Part B question has internal choice. You must answer **either** Option A **or** Option B. Each sub-part is 7 marks.

---

#### ⭐ OPTION A — 14 Marks

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**
*(a) Classify the five major types of **airborne chemical hazards** based on their physical state of dispersion. Describe the mechanism of formation, particle size range, and give **two industrial examples** for each.   **(7 marks)**)*

*(b) A worker is exposed to **carbon disulfide (CS₂)** vapour at $20 \text{ ppm}$ for 8 hours. The molecular weight of CS₂ is $76 \text{ g/mol}$, and the OSHA PEL is $4 \text{ mg/m}^3$ (8-hr TWA). Determine whether the exposure is within the permissible limit. **(7 marks)***

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

Classification (1 mark per type = 5 marks + 2 marks for example integration).

1. **Dust** – Solid particles formed by *mechanical subdivision* (crushing, grinding, drilling). Size: $0.1 - 100 \text{ }\mu m$. Examples: silica dust, coal dust.

2. **Fume** – Solid particles formed by *condensation of metal vapour* on cooling. Size: $0.001 - 1.0 \text{ }\mu m$. Examples: welding fume (Fe₂O₃), zinc oxide fume.

3. **Vapour** – Gaseous phase of a substance that is *liquid/solid at room temperature*. Molecular dispersion. Examples: benzene vapour, acetone vapour.

4. **Fog (Mist)** – Liquid droplets in air from *atomization* or *condensation*. Size: $1 - 100 \text{ }\mu m$. Examples: sulfuric acid mist, paint spray mist.

5. **Gas** – Substance *gaseous at STP* after release. Molecular dispersion. Examples: chlorine, carbon monoxide, ammonia.

(Also include 1 mark for tabular presentation)

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:** $C_{ppm} = 20 \text{ ppm}$, $MW = 76 \text{ g/mol}$, PEL = $4 \text{ mg/m}^3$.

**Step 1 — Convert ppm to mg/m³ using the standard formula:**

$$C_{mg/m^3} = \frac{\text{ppm} \times MW}{24.45} = \frac{20 \times 76}{24.45}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the numerator:**

$$20 \times 76 = 1520$$

**Step 3 — Divide by 24.45:**

$$C_{mg/m^3} = \frac{1520}{24.45} = 62.16 \text{ mg/m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Compare with PEL:**

$$62.16 \text{ mg/m}^3 \gg 4 \text{ mg/m}^3$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion (with safety implication):**

The exposure is **15.5 times** the permissible limit. The exposure is **NOT within** the OSHA PEL. Immediate corrective action is required:
- Engineering control: install local exhaust ventilation.
- Administrative control: rotate workers to reduce cumulative exposure.
- PPE: use of organic vapour respirator with appropriate Assigned Protection Factor (APF).

(Each step = 1 mark; conclusion = 2 marks)

---

#### ⭐ OPTION B — 14 Marks

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
*(a) Explain the **physical and chemical principles** governing the formation of **fumes and fogs** in industrial processes. Why are fumes considered more dangerous to the respiratory system than dusts?   **(7 marks)**)*

*(b) A workplace contains a **dust cloud** of density $2500 \text{ kg/m}^3$ with particle diameter $5 \text{ }\mu m$. Calculate the **terminal settling velocity** of these particles in still air at $30 °C$ (air viscosity $\mu = 1.85 \times 10^{-5} \text{ Pa·s}$, air density $\rho = 1.16 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$). Comment on the respirability of these particles. **(7 marks)***

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Fume formation (3 marks):**
- A metal is heated above its boiling point → vaporization → metal vapour rises.
- On encountering cooler ambient air, the vapour **oxidizes** (O₂ attack) → metal oxide.
- The metal oxide vapour becomes **supersaturated** → homogeneous nucleation of solid particles ($10^{-3}$ to $1 \text{ }\mu m$).
- Particles coagulate into **chain-like agglomerates** (e.g., welding fume morphology).
- Example: $\text{Fe (molten)} \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{Fe (vapour)} \xrightarrow{O_2} \text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 \text{ (fume)}$

**Fog formation (2 marks):**
- **Atomization**: bulk liquid forced through a nozzle/sieve → droplets (spray painting).
- **Condensation fog**: hot vapour + cool air → supersaturation → droplets condense on nuclei.
- Example: sulfuric acid mist in pickling bath; paint mist in spray booth.

**Why fumes are more dangerous than dusts (2 marks):**
- Fume size ($< 1 \text{ }\mu m$) < dust size ($> 1 \text{ }\mu m$).
- Small particles have **negligible settling velocity** (Stokes' law: $v_s \propto d^2$) → remain airborne for hours.
- Fumes reach the **alveolar region** of the lungs, while coarser dusts are filtered in the nasopharyngeal region.
- Fumes often consist of **freshly formed metal oxides** with high surface reactivity and bioavailability.
- Fumes bypass **mucociliary clearance** and **macrophage defence**, entering systemic circulation.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:** $d_p = 5 \text{ }\mu m = 5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$, $\rho_p = 2500 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho = 1.16 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 1.85 \times 10^{-5} \text{ Pa·s}$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

**Step 1 — Apply Stokes' Law:**

$$v_s = \frac{d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho) g}{18 \mu}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$v_s = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-6})^2 \times (2500 - 1.16) \times 9.81}{18 \times 1.85 \times 10^{-5}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator:**

$$(5 \times 10^{-6})^2 = 25 \times 10^{-12} = 2.5 \times 10^{-11}$$

$$(2500 - 1.16) = 2498.84$$

$$2.5 \times 10^{-11} \times 2498.84 = 6.247 \times 10^{-8}$$

$$6.247 \times 10^{-8} \times 9.81 = 6.129 \times 10^{-7} \text{ (m·kg/s)}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the denominator:**

$$18 \times 1.85 \times 10^{-5} = 33.3 \times 10^{-5} = 3.33 \times 10^{-4} \text{ (Pa·s)}$$

**Step 5 — Final division:**

$$v_s = \frac{6.129 \times 10^{-7}}{3.33 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.84 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/s}$$

$$v_s \approx 1.84 \text{ mm/s}$$

**Step 6 — Respirability comment (2 marks):**

- Particle size $5 \text{ }\mu m$ falls in the **respirable fraction** (typically $< 10 \text{ }\mu m$).
- Such particles deposit in the **tracheobronchial and alveolar regions**.
- Settling velocity is very small ($\approx 1.84 \text{ mm/s}$), so the particles remain airborne for a long time in still air → significant inhalation risk.
- **Conclusion:** This dust is **respirable** and poses a hazard if exposure exceeds TLV; engineering controls (LEV with HEPA filtration) are mandatory.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch in Stokes' Law:** Failing to convert $\mu m$ to $m$ results in 0 marks for numerical part.
> 2. **Confusing dust and fume:** Markers will deduct 1 full mark if a student writes "welding dust" instead of "welding fume."
> 3. **Forgetting the 24.45 factor** in ppm-to-mg/m³ conversion is the most common single-step error.
> 4. **Not stating the Clausius–Clapeyron relationship** for vapour-pressure questions loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Mixing "mist" and "fog":** In KTU valuation, "mist" and "fog" are *interchangeable* for liquid-droplet hazards; using one is acceptable, but the student must define the term.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Examination Quick-Recall)**

- ✅ **Five airborne hazards:** Dust (mechanical solid), Fume (condensed solid), Vapour (gas from liquid/solid), Fog/Mist (liquid droplet), Gas (gas at STP).
- ✅ **Discriminator #1:** Mechanism of formation — mechanical vs. thermal vs. evaporation vs. atomization.
- ✅ **Discriminator #2:** Physical state of *parent* vs. *dispersed* phase.
- ✅ **Size ranking (smallest → largest):** Fume ($0.001 \mu m$) < Vapour/Gas (molecular) < Fog ($1 \mu m$) < Dust ($100 \mu m$).
- ✅ **Most dangerous to lungs:** Fume (sub-micron, alveolar deposition).
- ✅ **Stokes' Law:** $v_s = \dfrac{d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho) g}{18 \mu}$ — settling velocity is proportional to $d^2$.
- ✅ **Conversion formula:** $C_{mg/m^3} = \dfrac{\text{ppm} \times MW}{24.45}$ (at $25 °C$).
- ✅ **Molar volume at NTP:** $V_m = 24.45 \text{ L/mol}$ (must be memorized).
- ✅ **Respirable fraction:** Aerodynamic diameter $< 10 \text{ }\mu m$ (reaches beyond bronchioles).
- ✅ **HEPA filter is effective for:** Dust and fume; **NOT** for vapour or gas.
- ✅ **Activated carbon** is the standard control for organic vapours and many toxic gases.
- ✅ **TLV-TWA:** 8-hr time-weighted average; **STEL:** 15-min short-term exposure limit; **IDLH:** immediately dangerous to life or health.
- ✅ **Silica, asbestos, coal dust, cotton dust** → classic examples of dust hazards.
- ✅ **Welding fume, foundry fume, smelting fume** → classic examples of fume hazards.
- ✅ **Benzene, toluene, mercury vapour** → classic examples of vapour hazards.
- ✅ **Sulfuric acid mist, chromic acid mist** → classic examples of fog/mist hazards.
- ✅ **CO, H₂S, HCN, Cl₂, NH₃** → classic examples of toxic gases.
- ✅ **Clausius–Clapeyron equation** links vapour pressure to temperature; higher $T$ → higher vapour hazard.
- ✅ **Confined-space entry** is the single most lethal scenario for gas hazards (CO, H₂S) — requires gas testing + SCBA.
- ✅ **Welding fume is metal oxide, not metal** — the oxidation in air is the reason for the toxicological profile.
- ✅ **Industrial hygiene hierarchy of controls:** Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE.
- ✅ **KTU-favourite numerical:** "Convert ppm of XYZ to mg/m³ and compare with TLV" — expect this almost every cycle.

---
