# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Definition and SI Units
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 2]**

> **Q1.** Define the term **"Absorbed Dose"** and **"Equivalent Dose"**. State their SI units. Why is the Sievert preferred over the Gray for expressing dose in radiation protection?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Absorbed Dose ($D$)**: The energy deposited by ionizing radiation per unit mass of absorbing material. $D = E/m$. **SI Unit: Gray (Gy), where $1 \text{ Gy} = 1 \text{ J/kg}$**. *(1 mark)*

- **Equivalent Dose ($H_T$)**: The absorbed dose weighted by the radiation weighting factor $W_R$ to account for the biological effectiveness of different radiation types: $H_T = \sum_R W_R \cdot D_{T,R}$. **SI Unit: Sievert (Sv)**. *(1 mark)*

- **Why Sievert preferred**: The Gray accounts only for physical energy deposition, which is the same for all radiation types at equal doses. The Sievert incorporates the **biological effectiveness** of each radiation type (e.g., alpha particles cause ~20× more biological damage than gamma rays for the same absorbed dose). Thus, Sievert is more meaningful for expressing **stochastic health risk** to humans. *(1 mark)*

---

### Question 2: Radioactive Half-Life
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 | Module 2]**

> **Q2.** A radioactive isotope has a half-life of **30 years**. If the initial activity is **$10^{12}$ Bq**, calculate the activity after **90 years**. Mention the principle used in the disposal of such waste.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Principle**: First-order radioactive decay, $A(t) = A_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda t}$. *(0.5 marks)*

- **Number of half-lives**: $n = 90/30 = 3$. *(0.5 marks)*

- **Calculation**: After 3 half-lives,
$$A = A_0 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^3 = 10^{12} \cdot \frac{1}{8} = 1.25 \times 10^{11} \text{ Bq}$$ *(1.5 marks)*

- **Disposal principle**: Long-lived radioactive waste is stored in **deep geological repositories** for periods exceeding 10 half-lives, allowing the activity to decay to negligible levels. The "**delay and decay**" principle is applied, complemented by multiple-barrier containment. *(0.5 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Students often **forget to convert units** (e.g., days to seconds, mGy to Gy) — this leads to calculation errors worth 2–3 marks.
> - In dose calculation problems, **always show the radiation weighting factor explicitly**; skipping this is a common 1-mark loss.
> - For long-answer questions on waste disposal, students sometimes describe **only one method** instead of providing a complete classification and a comparison.
> - Always **include diagrams** where possible — examiners often award 1–2 marks for well-labeled schematics.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 2]**

> **Q (A).** 
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain in detail the different **types of ionizing radiation**. Compare their penetrating power, ionization density, and biological effectiveness. Mention appropriate shielding materials for each.
> 
> **(b)** [7 Marks] Discuss the **acute and chronic effects of radiation** on the human body. With the help of a table, list the dose ranges and corresponding clinical effects for acute whole-body exposure.

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**1. Alpha Particles ($\alpha$)** *(1.5 marks)*
- Composed of 2 protons + 2 neutrons (Helium-4 nucleus)
- Charge: +2e; Mass: ~4 amu
- **Penetration**: Very low — stopped by a sheet of paper, ~5 cm of air, or the outer dead layer of skin
- **Ionization density**: Highest LET (~100 keV/μm) — densely ionizing
- **Biological effectiveness**: $W_R = 20$ — most damaging per unit dose
- **Shielding**: Paper, thin plastic; **but extremely dangerous if inhaled/ingested** (e.g., Plutonium-239)

**2. Beta Particles ($\beta^-$ and $\beta^+$)** *(1.5 marks)*
- High-energy electrons (or positrons) emitted from nucleus during neutron→proton or proton→neutron conversion
- Charge: -1 (electron) or +1 (positron); Mass: ~1/1836 amu
- **Penetration**: Moderate — stopped by a few mm of aluminum or ~1 cm of plastic
- **Ionization density**: Moderate LET (~0.1–1 keV/μm) — sparsely ionizing
- **Biological effectiveness**: $W_R = 1$
- **Shielding**: Aluminum, plastic, glass; avoid high-Z materials for high-energy beta (bremsstrahlung X-ray production)

**3. Gamma Rays ($\gamma$) and X-rays** *(1.5 marks)*
- High-frequency electromagnetic radiation; no mass, no charge
- **Penetration**: Very high — requires cm-to-m thick dense materials
- **Ionization density**: Low LET — sparsely ionizing
- **Biological effectiveness**: $W_R = 1$
- **Shielding**: Lead, concrete, depleted uranium, steel (high-Z, high-density materials)

**4. Neutrons (n)** *(1.5 marks)*
- Electrically neutral; mass ~1 amu
- **Penetration**: Extremely high — no Coulombic interaction, interacts via nuclear collisions
- **Ionization density**: Variable LET depending on energy
- **Biological effectiveness**: $W_R = 5$ to 20
- **Shielding**: Hydrogen-rich materials (water, polyethylene, paraffin, concrete)

**Summary Comparison Table** *(1 mark)*

| Property | $\alpha$ | $\beta$ | $\gamma$/X | n |
|----------|----------|---------|------------|---|
| Charge | +2 | -1 / +1 | 0 | 0 |
| Mass | 4 amu | 1/1836 amu | 0 | 1 amu |
| Penetration | Lowest | Moderate | High | Highest |
| LET | High | Low | Low | Variable |
| $W_R$ | 20 | 1 | 1 | 5–20 |
| Shield | Paper | Aluminum | Lead | Water/PE |

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Introduction** *(0.5 marks)*
Radiation effects on the human body are classified into two categories based on dose-response relationship: **Deterministic (Acute)** and **Stochastic (Chronic)**.

**Acute (Deterministic) Effects** *(3 marks)*
- Occur at **high doses** delivered over a short time
- Have a **threshold dose** below which the effect is not observed
- Severity **increases with dose**
- Caused by massive cell death and tissue/organ damage
- Examples: Nausea, vomiting, fatigue, hair loss, skin burns, bone marrow suppression, GI syndrome, CNS damage, death

**Chronic (Stochastic) Effects** *(2 marks)*
- Occur at **low doses** over long time periods (latency period of years to decades)
- **No threshold dose** — any exposure has a probability of causing the effect
- **Probability** of occurrence increases with dose, but **severity is independent of dose**
- Examples: Carcinogenesis (leukemia, thyroid, breast, lung cancers), genetic mutations, teratogenic effects on developing fetuses, cataracts

**Dose Range Table for Acute Whole-Body Exposure** *(1.5 marks)*

| Dose (Sv) | Clinical Effect |
|-----------|-----------------|
| 0 – 0.25 | No observable clinical effect |
| 0.25 – 1 | Mild nausea, slight lymphocyte decrease |
| 1 – 2 | Mild radiation sickness, fatigue, ~5% mortality |
| 2 – 4 | Severe radiation sickness, ~50% mortality without treatment |
| 4 – 6 | Severe illness, 50–90% mortality, LD50/60 ≈ 4.5 Sv |
| 6 – 10 | Lethal for most, severe GI and CNS effects |
| > 10 | Survival extremely unlikely |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 | Module 2]**

> **Q (B).**
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Classify **radioactive waste** based on activity level and physical state. Explain the disposal methods for each category with suitable examples.
> 
> **(b)** [7 Marks] State and explain the **three fundamental principles of radiation protection** (Time, Distance, Shielding). A worker receives a dose rate of **400 μSv/h** at a distance of **2 m** from a gamma source. Calculate the dose received in **30 minutes** and the dose rate at **4 m** distance.

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**1. Classification by Activity Level (IAEA)** *(3 marks)*

| Category | Activity | Examples | Disposal |
|----------|----------|----------|----------|
| **Exempt Waste (EW)** | < 100 Bq/g | Decommissioned rubble, cleared materials | Disposed as normal refuse after regulatory clearance |
| **Low-Level Waste (LLW)** | < 4 GBq/tonne | Hospital radioactive waste, contaminated paper, lab gloves | Near-surface disposal in engineered trenches with concrete barriers |
| **Intermediate-Level Waste (ILW)** | 4 GBq – several hundred GBq/tonne | Reactor resins, chemical sludges, metal components | Intermediate-depth geological vaults (30–100 m) |
| **High-Level Waste (HLW)** | > several hundred GBq/tonne (TBq–PBq) | Spent nuclear fuel, reprocessing waste | Deep geological repositories (500–1000 m); vitrification in glass; long-term monitored storage |

**2. Classification by Physical State** *(2 marks)*

- **Solid Waste** — Contaminated tools, gloves, filters, reactor components. Treatment: compaction, incineration, encapsulation in cement/bitumen.
- **Liquid Waste** — Coolant water, lab effluents. Treatment: chemical precipitation, ion exchange, evaporation, filtration.
- **Gaseous Waste** — Fission gases (Kr, Xe, I-131), activated air, tritium. Treatment: filtration (HEPA), activated charcoal adsorption, stack dilution, delay tanks for decay.

**3. Disposal Methods in Detail** *(2 marks)*

- **Near-Surface Disposal (LLW)**: Engineered trenches with multiple barriers (concrete, clay, geomembrane). Used at sites like the El Cabril facility (Spain) and Aube facility (France).
- **Geological Disposal (ILW/HLW)**: Multi-barrier concept — waste form (vitrified glass), canister (steel/copper), buffer (bentonite clay), host rock (granite/salt/clay). Examples: Onkalo (Finland), Yucca Mountain (USA – shelved), Cigeo (France).
- **Long-Term Storage**: For HLW, interim storage in water-filled pools or dry casks for 30–50 years before geological disposal, allowing short-lived isotopes to decay and reducing thermal load.

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**1. Three Principles of Radiation Protection** *(3 marks)*

> **[I] TIME**: Minimize the duration of exposure. Since total dose is the product of dose rate and time:
> $$D_{\text{total}} = \dot{D} \times t$$
> Halving the exposure time halves the dose. (1 mark)

> **[II] DISTANCE**: Maximize the distance from the radiation source. For a point source, the **inverse square law** applies:
> $$I \propto \frac{1}{d^2}$$
> Doubling the distance reduces the dose rate to one-quarter. (1 mark)

> **[III] SHIELDING**: Place an appropriate absorbing material between the source and the worker. The intensity follows the **exponential attenuation law**:
> $$I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu x}$$
> Common shields: lead (gamma), concrete (gamma/neutrons), polyethylene (neutrons), aluminum (beta). (1 mark)

**2. Numerical Calculations** *(4 marks)*

**Part (i): Dose received in 30 minutes at 2 m distance**

**Step 1**: Convert time to hours:
$$t = 30 \text{ min} = 0.5 \text{ h}$$

**Step 2**: Apply $D = \dot{D} \times t$:
$$D = 400 \text{ μSv/h} \times 0.5 \text{ h} = 200 \text{ μSv} = 0.2 \text{ mSv}$$

**Answer**: The dose received is **200 μSv** (or 0.2 mSv). *(1.5 marks)*

**Part (ii): Dose rate at 4 m distance**

**Step 1**: Apply the inverse square law:
$$\frac{\dot{D}_2}{\dot{D}_1} = \left(\frac{d_1}{d_2}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{2}{4}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{4}$$

**Step 2**: Calculate new dose rate:
$$\dot{D}_2 = \dot{D}_1 \times \frac{1}{4} = 400 \text{ μSv/h} \times 0.25 = 100 \text{ μSv/h}$$

**Answer**: The dose rate at 4 m is **100 μSv/h**. *(2.5 marks)*

**Comment**: This demonstrates the **dramatic effectiveness of the distance principle** — simply stepping back from 2 m to 4 m reduces the dose rate to one-quarter.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

### ✅ Core Concepts

- **Radiation** = Emission/transmission of energy as waves or particles; classified as **Ionizing** (sufficient energy to ionize atoms) and **Non-Ionizing** (insufficient energy to ionize; causes heating/excitation).
- **Ionizing radiation types**: $\alpha$, $\beta$, $\gamma$, X-rays, neutrons.
- **Non-ionizing radiation types**: UV, visible, IR, microwave, RF.

### ✅ Key Formulas (Must Memorize)

- $A = A_0 e^{-\lambda t}$ (decay law)
- $\lambda = \dfrac{0.693}{t_{1/2}}$ (decay constant)
- $H_T = W_R \cdot D$ (equivalent dose)
- $E = W_T \cdot H_T$ (effective dose)
- $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$ (attenuation)
- $\text{HVL} = \dfrac{0.693}{\mu}$ (half-value layer)
- $I \propto 1/d^2$ (inverse square law)

### ✅ Units to Remember

- **Becquerel (Bq)** = activity, 1 disintegration/s
- **Gray (Gy)** = absorbed dose, 1 J/kg
- **Sievert (Sv)** = equivalent/effective dose
- **$W_R$**: $\alpha = 20$, $\beta/\gamma = 1$, neutrons = 5–20
- **$1 \text{ Ci} = 3.7 \times 10^{10} \text{ Bq}$**
- **$1 \text{ Sv} = 100 \text{ rem}$**

### ✅ Effects on Human Body

- **Deterministic effects** — Threshold-dependent; severity increases with dose; high doses.
- **Stochastic effects** — No threshold; probability increases with dose; severity independent of dose.
- **LD50/60 ≈ 4.5 Sv** for whole-body acute exposure.
- Most radiosensitive tissues: bone marrow, GI tract, gonads, lens, skin.

### ✅ Waste Disposal Hierarchy

- **EW** → Normal refuse (after clearance)
- **LLW** → Near-surface disposal (engineered trenches)
- **ILW** → Intermediate-depth vaults (30–100 m)
- **HLW** → Deep geological repositories (500–1000 m); vitrification
- **"Delay and Decay"** principle for short-lived isotopes; **"Concentrate and Contain"** for long-lived isotopes.

### ✅ Three Principles of Protection

- **Time** — Reduce exposure duration.
- **Distance** — Maximize distance; inverse square law.
- **Shielding** — Use appropriate barriers (lead for $\gamma$, water/PE for neutrons, aluminum for $\beta$).

### ✅ Common KTU Pitfalls to Avoid

- Forgetting unit conversions (days ↔ seconds, mGy ↔ Gy).
- Confusing **absorbed dose** (Gy) with **equivalent dose** (Sv) — they differ by the weighting factor.
- Treating $\alpha$ particles as harmless because they cannot penetrate skin — they are **deadly if internalized**.
- Using the wrong shielding material (e.g., lead for neutrons is inefficient).
- Forgetting to include diagrams/tables in long-answer questions.
- Mixing up **half-life** and **mean life** ($\tau = 1/\lambda = t_{1/2}/0.693$).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Safety in Construction Industry - Scaffolding and Working Platform
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET722 - ENVIRONMENTAL HEALTH AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Safety in Construction Industry - Scaffolding and Working Platform

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🏗️ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Scaffolding** is defined under **IS 4014 (Parts I & II)** and **OSHA 29 CFR 1926.451** as a *temporary elevated or suspended working platform and its supporting structure* used for supporting workers, materials, tools, and equipment during the construction, alteration, demolition, maintenance, or repair of buildings and other structures.

A **Working Platform** is the actual horizontal surface (decking/plank level) on which workers stand, walk, or place materials. It is a critical sub-component of a scaffold system and is governed by strict load-bearing, edge-protection, and slip-resistance standards.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Reference Standards (Mandatory for KTU):**
> - **IS 4014 (Part 1 & 2):** Code of practice for scaffolding
> - **OSHA 29 CFR 1926 Subpart L:** Scaffolds and scaffold platforms
> - **BIS 3696:** Safety code for scaffolds
> - **Indian Factories Act, 1948 – Section 32:** Safety of buildings and structures

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🍎

Imagine you are trying to **paint the upper portion of a tall 4-storey house**. You cannot reach the top with a normal ladder safely, and a ladder is not stable enough to hold your paint bucket, yourself, and allow you to move sideways. What do you do?

You build a **temporary skeletal platform** around the house — a structure made of vertical metal poles, horizontal connectors, and wooden planks. This skeleton gives you a *floor to stand on* and a *guard to hold*, anywhere along the wall, and it can be dismantled when the job is done. **That skeleton is the scaffold**, and the *plank you stand on* is the **working platform**.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Memory Hook:** Think **Bones + Skin of Construction** — The *standards (vertical poles)* are the bones, the *couplers/clamps* are the joints, and the *planks* are the skin (working platform) where workers actually operate.

---

## 1.3 Why Scaffolding Safety is a KTU High-Priority Topic

Construction is the **most hazardous industrial sector** globally. According to the **International Labour Organization (ILO)**, falls from height account for **~25% of all fatal workplace injuries worldwide**, and **scaffold-related collapses are among the top 3 causes** of such falls.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The university expects students to correlate *cause → hazard → control measure → standard*. Memorize at least **2 IS codes** and **4 scaffold components** — these are *guaranteed marks* in ESE and internal assessments.

---

## 1.4 Scope of This Module Note

| Aspect | Coverage |
|---|---|
| **Scaffold Types** | Independent, Birdcage, Cantilever, Suspended, Trestle, Mobile |
| **Components** | Standards, Ledgers, Transoms, Putlogs, Braces, Base Plates, Planks |
| **Load Categories** | Light Duty (1.5 kN/m²), Medium Duty (2.5 kN/m²), Heavy Duty (4.5 kN/m²) |
| **Safety Devices** | Guard Rails, Toe Boards, Ladder Access, Ties, Tags |
| **Inspection** | Pre-use, Daily, After Modification, 7-Day Cycle |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Scaffolds (KTU Standard Classification)

Scaffolds are classified based on **structural support, method of access, and material of construction**. The KTU syllabus aligns with **BIS / OSHA classification**.

### 2.1.1 Based on Structural Support

**1. Independent / Brick Layer's Scaffold**
- A double-row scaffold with two rows of standards separated by the working platform.
- Used for brick masonry, plastering, and external façade work.
- Most common type examined in KTU theory questions.

**2. Birdcage Scaffold (Independent Multi-Pole Scaffold)**
- A multi-level scaffold consisting of rows of standards connected by ledgers and transoms, with working platforms at multiple heights simultaneously.
- Used for ceiling work, painting, and light maintenance of large halls (e.g., auditoriums, factories).

**3. Cantilever Scaffold (Needle Scaffold)**
- Standards are supported on cantilever beams (needles) projecting out from a wall or floor through openings.
- Used when the ground cannot support scaffold standards (e.g., overhanging balconies, busy streets, weak soil).

**4. Suspended Scaffold**
- Working platform suspended by ropes or chains from an overhead structure (parapet, roof outrigger).
- Used for repair and painting of tall buildings (window cleaning, façade repair).
- Counterweight and rope-factor-of-safety are critical.

**5. Trestle Scaffold**
- Working platform supported on movable tripods or ladders.
- Used for low-height interior work (≤ 5 m).

**6. Mobile / Tower Scaffold**
- A free-standing tower mounted on castor wheels.
- Used for moving work along a horizontal axis (e.g., aircraft maintenance, warehouse lighting).

**7. System / Modular Scaffold (Cuplock, Ringlock, Kwikstage)**
- Prefabricated modular components with built-in locking mechanisms.
- The **industry gold standard** in modern construction due to speed and safety.

### 2.1.2 Based on Material

| Material | Typical Use | Working Load Limit (WLL) | Lifespan |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Steel (GI tubes)** | Heavy-duty, long-term projects | **4.5 kN/m²** | 5–10 years |
| **Aluminum** | Light, mobile, suspended | **2.5 kN/m²** | 5–7 years |
| **Bamboo** | Traditional, rural India | **1.5 kN/m²** | < 1 year |
| **Timber (Hacked bamboo alternative)** | Short-term, low-rise | **1.5 kN/m²** | < 6 months |

> [!NOTE]
> **Bamboo scaffolding** is still legal in India and widely used in Kerala for small-scale and residential projects. However, it does **NOT comply with IS 4014** and is **not permitted** on any KTU-approved or factory-regulated industrial site.

---

## 2.2 Anatomy of a Scaffold — Core Components

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MEMORIZE THESE 8 COMPONENTS — guaranteed 7-marker in KTU Part B.**

| # | Component | Function | KTU Memory Aid |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Standards** | Vertical tubes/poles transferring load to ground | **"S**tands **U**p **T**all" |
| 2 | **Ledgers** | Horizontal tubes along the wall, parallel to façade | **"L**ateral **H**orizontal" |
| 3 | **Transoms** | Short horizontal tubes perpendicular to wall, holding planks | **"T**hrough" the platform |
| 4 | **Putlogs** | Flat-ended tubes with a flattened blade inserted into brick joints | **"P**ut into wall" |
| 5 | **Braces (Diagonal)** | Provide lateral stability, prevent racking | **"B**race the wind" |
| 6 | **Base Plate & Sole Board** | Distributes load to ground; prevents sinking | **"B**road **S**upport" |
| 7 | **Couplers / Clamps** | Connect tubes: Right-angle, Swivel, Sleeve, Putlog | **"C**onnectors" |
| 8 | **Working Platform / Planks** | The deck where workers stand; must be scaffold-grade | **"T**he **S**kin" |

---

## 2.3 Load Categories & Design Philosophy

A scaffold must be designed to carry the **worst combination** of the following loads:

$$W_{total} = W_{dead} + W_{live} + W_{wind} + W_{impact}$$

Where:
- $W_{dead}$ = Self-weight of scaffold + platform + materials stacked
- $W_{live}$ = Weight of workers + tools + transient materials
- $W_{wind}$ = Lateral wind force (per IS 875 Part 3 / ASCE 7)
- $W_{impact}$ = Dynamic load factor (typically 25% of live load)

### **KTU High-Yield Load Classification (Must Memorize)**

| Duty Class | Uniformly Distributed Load (UDL) | Concentrated Load | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Light Duty** | **1.5 kN/m² (150 kg/m²)** | 1.5 kN | Painting, cleaning, light inspection |
| **Medium Duty** | **2.5 kN/m² (250 kg/m²)** | 2.5 kN | Brickwork, plastering |
| **Heavy Duty** | **4.5 kN/m² (450 kg/m²)** | 4.5 kN | Stone masonry, concrete pouring |

---

## 2.4 The "Why" Behind Scaffold Failures — KTU Causal Analysis

> [!TIP]
> KTU frequently asks: *"List four causes of scaffold collapse."* Frame your answer using the **PEARL model** below:

| Letter | Cause | Engineering Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **P** | **Poor Foundation** (no sole boards, sinking soil) | Standards settle unevenly → racking |
| **E** | **Excessive Load** (stacking materials beyond WLL) | Standards buckle under compression |
| **A** | **Absent / Inadequate Ties** (no anchor to wall) | Scaffold falls away from building |
| **R** | **Rushed Dismantling** (no sequence, dropped tubes) | Progressive collapse, struck-by injuries |
| **L** | **Loose Couplers / Defective Planks** (untightened, rotten) | Joint slip, plank shear failure |

---

## 2.5 The Hierarchy of Control Applied to Scaffolding

Following **ISO 45001** and **IS 4014**, scaffold safety must be addressed in this order:

1. **Elimination** — Use ground-level prefabrication + crane lift instead of high work
2. **Substitution** — Replace bamboo with **system scaffold (Cuplock)**
3. **Engineering Controls** — Ties, bracing, guard rails, toe boards
4. **Administrative Controls** — Scaff-tag system, training, supervisor sign-off
5. **PPE** — Safety harness, hard hat, non-slip boots (last line of defense)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Distributed Load on a Working Platform (Plank)

> **Problem (KTU-style, 7 marks):** A medium-duty working platform is to be constructed for brickwork. The platform has a length $L = 4$ m and a width $B = 1.2$ m. A stack of bricks equivalent to a uniformly distributed load of **2.0 kN/m²** is placed at the center, and 4 workers weighing an average of 75 kg each occupy the platform. Calculate the total design load and verify against the medium-duty limit of **2.5 kN/m²**.

### **Step 1: Identify the live load component from workers**

The live load of workers is typically taken as **1.0 kN/m²** for general construction, but we will compute the *actual* load from the 4 workers for academic rigor.

$$W_{workers} = n \times m \times g = 4 \times 75 \times 9.81 = 2943 \text{ N} \approx 2.94 \text{ kN}$$

### **Step 2: Convert to an equivalent UDL on the platform area**

$$A_{platform} = L \times B = 4 \times 1.2 = 4.8 \text{ m}^2$$

$$W_{workers,UDL} = \frac{2.94}{4.8} = 0.6125 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

### **Step 3: Add the material stack load (given)**

$$W_{stack,UDL} = 2.0 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

### **Step 4: Sum the live load component**

$$W_{live,total} = W_{workers,UDL} + W_{stack,UDL} = 0.6125 + 2.0 = 2.6125 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

### **Step 5: Add dead load (self-weight of planks + steel structure, conservatively assumed)**

$$W_{dead} = 0.4 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

### **Step 6: Compute total design load (factored, with 25% impact factor per IS 4014)**

$$W_{design} = (W_{live,total} + W_{dead}) \times 1.25$$

$$W_{design} = (2.6125 + 0.4) \times 1.25$$

$$W_{design} = 3.0125 \times 1.25 = 3.766 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

### **Step 7: Compare against medium-duty limit (2.5 kN/m²) and recommend action**

$$W_{design} = 3.766 \text{ kN/m}^2 > 2.5 \text{ kN/m}^2 \quad \Rightarrow \text{EXCEEDS LIMIT}$$

**Conclusion:** The platform **fails** the medium-duty check. The student must **either**:

- Upgrade the platform to **heavy-duty classification (4.5 kN/m²)**, OR
- Restrict the brick stack height to ensure the live UDL ≤ 2.1 kN/m² (allowing a 4% safety margin).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Always show the **final comparison with a "✔ ACCEPTABLE" or "✘ EXCEEDS LIMIT" statement** — examiners award the last 1 mark specifically for this conclusion.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Scaffold Tie Spacing Derivation

> **Problem (KTU-style, 7 marks):** A brick-layer's independent scaffold is to be erected on the façade of a 12 m tall building. The wind pressure on the scaffold face is given as $p_w = 1.0 \text{ kN/m}^2$ (per IS 875 Part 3, modified for height). The scaffold is sheeted with tarpaulin (porosity factor 0.5). The plan dimensions of one scaffold bay are $L \times B = 2.0 \times 1.2$ m. Calculate the horizontal wind force per tie and the **maximum vertical tie spacing** such that the tie can resist **1.5 kN** in pull-out.

### **Step 1: Compute the effective wind pressure on the sheeted face**

$$p_{eff} = p_w \times \phi = 1.0 \times 0.5 = 0.5 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

where $\phi$ is the porosity factor of the sheeting (0.5 for tarpaulin/netting).

### **Step 2: Compute the wind force on one bay (face area = $L \times h_{tie}$)**

For one bay, the wind force per meter height is:

$$F_{wind,per\_m} = p_{eff} \times L = 0.5 \times 2.0 = 1.0 \text{ kN/m}$$

### **Step 3: Equate to the tie capacity and solve for $h_{tie}$ (maximum vertical spacing)**

A tie resists 1.5 kN. Therefore:

$$h_{tie,max} = \frac{F_{tie,capacity}}{F_{wind,per\_m}} = \frac{1.5}{1.0} = 1.5 \text{ m}$$

### **Step 4: Apply the IS 4014 minimum constraint**

IS 4014 specifies a **minimum vertical tie spacing of 2 bays or 4 m**, whichever is **less**, AND a **maximum spacing of 4 m vertically and 6 m horizontally** for unsheeted scaffolds. For sheeted scaffolds, the spacing is typically **halved**.

$$h_{tie,IS4014} = \min\left(2 \times 2.0, 4.0\right) = 4.0 \text{ m for unsheeted}$$

For sheeted scaffold, the controlling value is:

$$h_{tie,final} = \min(1.5, 4.0/2) = \min(1.5, 2.0) = 1.5 \text{ m}$$

### **Step 5: Final answer**

$$\boxed{h_{tie,max} = 1.5 \text{ m vertically; horizontal spacing} = 6.0 \text{ m}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Trap:** Students often forget to **apply the sheeting factor** $\phi$. The examiner will mark this wrong unless explicitly stated.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: Choosing Scaffold Type

> **Decision Question:** *"A 22-storey IT park façade requires painting. The ground has heavy pedestrian traffic and a 6 m setback cannot be arranged. Choose the appropriate scaffold."*

| Criterion | Independent | Cantilever | Suspended | System Tower |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Tall structure** | ✘ (huge footprint) | ✔ | ✔✔ | ✘ |
| **Ground obstructed** | ✘ | ✔✔ | ✔✔ | ✔ |
| **Wind exposure** | ✔ (if tied) | ✔ | ✘ (oscillates) | ✘ |
| **Cost (high-rise)** | High | Medium | Low | High |
| **KTU recommended** | Up to 15 m | 15–40 m | 40+ m | < 10 m interior |

**Optimal Choice:** **Suspended scaffold (cradle)** with secondary safety rope, anchored to a parapet outrigger.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Safety Factor Rule of Thumb (IS 4014 / OSHA):**
> - Scaffold components: **Factor of Safety = 4** (against yielding)
> - Ties and anchors: **Factor of Safety = 2** (against pull-out)
> - Suspension ropes: **Factor of Safety = 6** (per OSHA 1926.451)
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📐 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Scaffold Component Architecture (3D Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Ground Level - Sole Board - Hardwood or Concrete] --> B[Base Plate - 150x150 mm]
    B --> C[Standards - Vertical GI Tubes - 40 mm NB - 2 m Bay Height]
    C --> D[Ledgers - Horizontal Tubes - Parallel to Wall]
    D --> E[Transoms - Short Tubes - Hold Planks]
    E --> F[Working Platform - Scaffold Grade Planks - Min 38 mm thick]
    F --> G[Guard Rail - 950 mm Above Platform]
    F --> H[Mid Rail - 470 mm Above Platform]
    F --> I[Toe Board - 150 mm Above Platform]
    C --> J[Diagonal Bracing - Both Faces]
    C --> K[Wall Ties - Every 4 m Vertical - Every 6 m Horizontal]
    C --> L[Ladder Access - Internal or External]

    style A fill:#8B4513,color:#fff
    style B fill:#708090,color:#fff
    style C fill:#4682B4,color:#fff
    style D fill:#5F9EA0,color:#fff
    style E fill:#5F9EA0,color:#fff
    style F fill:#DAA520,color:#000
    style G fill:#FF4500,color:#fff
    style H fill:#FF8C00,color:#fff
    style I fill:#FF6347,color:#fff
    style J fill:#6A5ACD,color:#fff
    style K fill:#DC143C,color:#fff
    style L fill:#2E8B57,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Scaff-Tag System Workflow (Inspection Hierarchy)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Scaffold Erection Complete] --> Q{Initial Inspection by Competent Person}
    Q -- Pass --> R[Issue GREEN Tag - Safe to Use]
    Q -- Fail --> S[Issue RED Tag - Do Not Use]
    S --> T[Rectify Defects]
    T --> Q
    R --> U[Daily Pre-Use Check by User]
    U --> V{Any Damage or Modification?}
    V -- No --> W[Continue Work - Daily Log Entry]
    V -- Yes --> X[Stop Work - Remove Tag]
    X --> Y[Notify Supervisor]
    Y --> T
    W --> Z[7-Day Formal Inspection by Competent Person]
    Z --> Q

    style P fill:#4682B4,color:#fff
    style R fill:#228B22,color:#fff
    style S fill:#B22222,color:#fff
    style U fill:#DAA520,color:#000
    style Z fill:#6A5ACD,color:#fff
    style Q fill:#FF8C00,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Load Transfer Path in a Scaffold (Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
```

# 🎓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2023 Set B]**
**Define the term "Scaffolding" as per IS 4014. List any four essential components of a scaffold.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Scaffolding:** A temporary structure, comprising metal tubes, couplers, planks, and accessories, erected to provide a safe working platform and access for workers, tools, and materials during construction, alteration, demolition, maintenance, or repair of a building or structure (IS 4014 Part 1).

**Four Essential Components:**

1. **Standards** — Vertical tubes transmitting load to the ground
2. **Ledgers** — Horizontal tubes running parallel to the wall
3. **Transoms** — Short horizontal tubes that support the working platform
4. **Braces** — Diagonal members for lateral stability

> *(Acceptable alternatives: Putlogs, Base Plates, Couplers, Sole Boards, Guard Rails, Toe Boards)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**
**Differentiate between a Light Duty and a Heavy Duty scaffold in terms of design load.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Light Duty | Heavy Duty |
|---|---|---|
| **Uniformly Distributed Load (UDL)** | **1.5 kN/m²** | **4.5 kN/m²** |
| **Concentrated Load** | 1.5 kN | 4.5 kN |
| **Typical Application** | Painting, cleaning, inspection | Stone masonry, concrete pouring, heavy material stacking |
| **Tube Diameter** | 32–40 mm NB | 40–50 mm NB |
| **Bay Size** | Up to 2.5 m × 1.5 m | Reduced to 1.5 m × 1.2 m |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. **Internal choice** means you must answer EITHER Option A OR Option B in full.

---

### ✅ **OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model Paper - Module 3]**
>
> **(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **three main types of independent scaffolds** used in building construction. Discuss their suitability and limitations. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A scaffold working platform of size $3.0 \text{ m} \times 1.5 \text{ m}$ is loaded with 6 workers (each 70 kg) and 800 kg of stacked material. The self-weight of planks and steel is 0.4 kN/m². Apply a 25% impact factor and check whether the platform is safe for **medium duty (2.5 kN/m²)** use. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Type 1: Single-Pole Scaffold (Putlog Scaffold)**
- Has only **one row of standards**, placed at a distance of about **1.2 to 1.5 m** from the wall.
- The putlogs are flat-ended tubes with a flattened blade inserted into the horizontal joints of brickwork to support the working platform.
- **Use:** Bricklaying, light plastering, single-storey works.
- **Limitation:** Not suitable for heights above 8 m or stone masonry.

**Type 2: Double-Pole (Independent) Scaffold**
- Has **two rows of standards**, with the inner row close to the wall and the outer row about 1.5 m away.
- Both rows are connected by ledgers, transoms, and braces.
- **Use:** Heavy construction, multi-storey brickwork, plastering.
- **Limitation:** Requires more ground space; more material cost.

**Type 3: Cantilever (Needle) Scaffold**
- Standards are supported on **cantilever beams (needles)** projecting through windows or wall openings.
- The inner end of the needle is **counterweighted or anchored** inside the building.
- **Use:** Where ground cannot support standards — e.g., busy footpaths, weak soil, waterlogged areas.
- **Limitation:** Limited load capacity; requires careful structural design of needle.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1: Platform area**

$$A = 3.0 \times 1.5 = 4.5 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Load from workers**

$$W_{workers} = 6 \times 70 \times 9.81 = 4120.2 \text{ N} = 4.12 \text{ kN}$$

$$W_{workers,UDL} = \frac{4.12}{4.5} = 0.916 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 3: Load from stacked material**

$$W_{stack,UDL} = \frac{800 \times 9.81}{1000 \times 4.5} = \frac{7.848}{4.5} = 1.744 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 4: Dead load (given)**

$$W_{dead} = 0.4 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 5: Unfactored total UDL**

$$W_{total,unfactored} = 0.916 + 1.744 + 0.4 = 3.06 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 6: Apply impact factor (1.25)**

$$W_{design} = 3.06 \times 1.25 = 3.825 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 7: Verification**

$$3.825 \text{ kN/m}^2 > 2.5 \text{ kN/m}^2 \Rightarrow \textbf{EXCEEDS MEDIUM DUTY LIMIT} \quad \times$$

**Conclusion:** ✘ **The platform is NOT safe for medium duty.** It must be reclassified as **heavy duty (4.5 kN/m²)**, since $3.825 < 4.5$ ✔ — **or the material stack must be reduced by ~35%**.

---

### ✅ **OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper - Module 3]**
>
> **(a)** Discuss the **causes and prevention** of scaffold collapse in the construction industry. Mention at least four causes with engineering control measures. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** List the **mandatory safety features** of a working platform as per IS 4014 and OSHA. Explain the **Scaff-Tag system** with a flowchart in words. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

| # | Cause | Engineering Control Measure |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Poor foundation** — no sole boards, soft soil, sinking | Use **sole boards (min 150×150 mm hardwood)** under every standard; compact ground; use **base plates** |
| 2 | **Overloading** — stacking materials beyond WLL | Calculate UDL per duty class; post **load limit signages**; supervisor verification before stacking |
| 3 | **Inadequate bracing & ties** — no wall ties, missing diagonal braces | Install **ties every 4 m vertically and 6 m horizontally**; provide **diagonal bracing** on both front and rear faces |
| 4 | **Defective planks and couplers** — rotten wood, untightened clamps | Use only **scaffold-grade planks (min 38 mm thick)**; tighten couplers to **torque of 50 N·m**; visually inspect daily |
| 5 | **Improper dismantling** — dropping tubes, no sequence | Use **reverse-erection sequence**; lower components with ropes; barricade the drop zone; provide tool bags |

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Mandatory Safety Features of a Working Platform (per IS 4014 & OSHA 1926.451):**

1. **Guard Rail (Top Rail):** A rigid rail **at least 950 mm (3 ft)** above the platform surface, capable of resisting 0.9 kN horizontal load.
2. **Mid-Rail:** A second rail **midway between the top rail and the platform**, ~470 mm.
3. **Toe Board:** A vertical barrier **at least 150 mm high** along all open edges to prevent tools/materials from falling.
4. **Full Planking:** The platform must be **fully planked** with no gaps > 25 mm.
5. **Slip-Resistant Surface:** Planks must be **clean, dry, and free of oil/mud**.
6. **Safe Access:** A **properly secured ladder** with a 4:1 slope, extending 1 m above the platform.
7. **Scaff-Tag System:** Mandatory color-coded tagging.

**The Scaff-Tag System (per OSHA 1926.451 Subpart L):**

- **🟢 GREEN Tag** — Issued by a **competent person** after full inspection; **safe to use**.
- **🔴 RED Tag** — Issued when scaffold is **incomplete, damaged, or under construction**; **DO NOT USE**.
- **🟡 YELLOW Tag** (in some systems) — Indicates **restrictions** (e.g., limited to specific duty class).
- **🟠 ORANGE Tag** — **Caution** — minor defects, restricted access.

**Tag must display:** Inspector's name, date, time, duty class, and signature. Tags are placed at **all access points**.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Common Mark-Loss Zones):**
> 1. **Confusing UDL and concentrated load** — they are *different* values. Show both.
> 2. **Forgetting the impact/dynamic factor** (1.25×) — a -1 mark trap.
> 3. **Not writing the final conclusion** ("EXCEEDS LIMIT" or "ACCEPTABLE") — -1 mark.
> 4. **Missing units** in the final answer (kN/m²) — partial mark loss.
> 5. **Confusing putlog scaffold (single row) with independent scaffold (double row)** — common conceptual error.
> 6. **Omitting the porosity factor** in wind-load calculations on sheeted scaffolds.
> 7. **Citing outdated standards** (use **IS 4014:2017** revision, not older versions).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

> **🎯 Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before Exam**

### ✅ **Definitions You Must Know**
- Scaffolding (IS 4014)
- Working Platform
- Standards, Ledgers, Transoms, Putlogs, Braces
- Sole Board, Base Plate, Coupler
- Independent, Cantilever, Suspended, Birdcage, Trestle, Mobile, System Scaffold

### ✅ **Numerical Values to Memorize**
- Light duty = **1.5 kN/m²**
- Medium duty = **2.5 kN/m²**
- Heavy duty = **4.5 kN/m²**
- Guard rail height = **950 mm** (OSHA) / **910 mm** (BIS)
- Mid-rail height = **470 mm**
- Toe board height = **150 mm**
- Top-rail horizontal load = **0.9 kN**
- Tie spacing = **4 m vertical × 6 m horizontal** (halved if sheeted)
- Coupler torque = **50 N·m**
- Factor of Safety on ropes = **6** (suspended)
- Impact factor = **1.25**

### ✅ **Standards to Cite**
- **IS 4014 (Part 1 & 2)** — Scaffolding
- **IS 3696** — Safety Code
- **IS 875 Part 3** — Wind Loads
- **OSHA 29 CFR 1926 Subpart L** — Scaffolds
- **ISO 45001** — Occupational H&S Management

### ✅ **Mnemonics**
- **PEARL** — Five causes of collapse
- **"Bones + Skin"** — Scaffold anatomy
- **"Stop the load at 4×, 2×, 6×"** — FOS for components, ties, ropes

### ✅ **The 4 Most Common KTU Errors to Avoid**
1. ❌ Writing "pipe" instead of "tube"
2. ❌ Confusing *putlog* (single row) with *independent* (double row) scaffold
3. ❌ Forgetting to convert **kg → kN** in numerical problems
4. ❌ Saying "bamboo is permitted on all sites" — **NO**, it is non-compliant with IS 4014 for industrial projects

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Mantra:** *"Erect → Inspect → Tag → Use → Inspect (7-day) → Dismantle (reverse order)."* If you can write this sequence with 2 lines of explanation, you have secured 4 marks in any ESE Part B on this topic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Welding and Cutting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET722 - ENVIRONMENTAL HEALTH AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Welding and Cutting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

**Welding** is a permanent joining process in which two or more pieces of metal (or thermoplastic) are fused together by the application of heat, pressure, or both, with or without the use of a filler material, to form a metallurgical bond at the joint interface.

**Cutting** is a thermal, mechanical, or chemical material-removal process used to separate, shape, or sever metal components. In an Occupational Health and Safety (OHS) context, the term **"Hot Work"** legally encompasses all welding, cutting, brazing, soldering, and allied thermal/spark-producing operations performed in industrial environments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** For the Environmental Health and Safety (EHS) course, the focus is **NOT** on the metallurgical quality of the weld (e.g., tensile strength, bead geometry). The focus is on the **occupational hazards, toxic exposures, fire/explosion risk, radiation hazards, ventilation engineering controls, and personal protective equipment (PPE)** associated with these processes.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a blacksmith heating two iron rods in a furnace until they glow red-orange and become soft. The blacksmith then hammers them together. This is essentially welding in its most primitive form — using heat to make metals join as one.

Now, extend that blacksmith's workshop to a modern shipyard where a worker uses a torch emitting a **6,000 °C flame** to slice through 50 mm thick steel plate. That same worker is simultaneously exposed to:
- **Invisible ultraviolet (UV) radiation** that can burn the eyes in seconds (a condition called *arc eye* or *welder's flash*).
- **Toxic metal fumes** (manganese, chromium, nickel, cadmium) that can cause irreversible brain damage over years of exposure.
- **A flammable atmosphere** where a single spark can ignite a fire 10 meters away.

This is why welding and cutting operations are categorized as **Permit-to-Work (PTW)** activities in industrial safety management systems.

### 1.3 Classification Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Classification of Welding & Cutting Processes (per IS 819 and AWS A3.0):**

| Process Family | Key Methods | Primary Energy Source |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Fusion Welding** | Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW), Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW/MIG), Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW/TIG), Flux-Cored Arc Welding (FCAW) | Electrical arc |
| **Gas Welding** | Oxy-Acetylene Welding (OAW), Oxy-Hydrogen Welding | Combustion of fuel gas |
| **Resistance Welding** | Spot, Seam, Projection, Flash Butt Welding | Electrical resistance heating |
| **Solid-State Welding** | Friction Stir Welding (FSW), Ultrasonic Welding | Mechanical + frictional heat |
| **High-Energy Beam Welding** | Laser Beam Welding (LBW), Electron Beam Welding (EBW) | Concentrated photon/electron beam |
| **Thermal Cutting** | Oxy-Fuel Cutting (OFC), Plasma Arc Cutting (PAC), Laser Cutting, Air Carbon Arc Cutting (CAC-A) | Heat + oxidation / molten metal blow-off |

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (Syllabus Constants)

- **Permissible Exposure Limit (PEL) for Welding Fumes (Total):** **5 mg/m³** (OSHA 29 CFR 1910.252; equivalent Indian Factory Rules threshold).
- **Permissible Exposure Limit (PEL) for Manganese (Mn):** **0.5 mg/m³** (8-hour TWA — Time-Weighted Average).
- **Permissible Exposure Limit (PEL) for Hexavalent Chromium [Cr(VI)]:** **0.005 mg/m³** (Action Level = 0.0025 mg/m³).
- **Oxygen Concentration (Safe Atmosphere):** **20.9%** (by volume). Atmosphere below **19.5%** = Oxygen-Deficient.
- **Oxygen Concentration (Fire/Explosion Enriched):** **> 23.5%** = Oxygen-Enriched.
- **Lower Explosive Limit (LEL) of Acetylene:** **2.5%** in air.
- **Auto-Ignition Temperature of Acetylene:** **305 °C**.
- **Flame Temperature of Oxy-Acetylene:** **~ 3,500 °C** (neutral flame), can reach **~ 6,000 °C** in the inner cone.
- **Arc Temperature (SMAW/GTAW):** **~ 6,000 °C – 20,000 °C**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The 5 Hazard Categories of Welding & Cutting

The **American National Standards Institute (ANSI Z49.1 – Safety in Welding, Cutting, and Allied Processes)** classifies all welding hazards into **five (5) principal categories**. Every KTU question on this topic is mapped to one of these five.

#### 2.1.1 Physical Hazards (Non-Toxic)

These are hazards that cause acute physical injury.

- **Electric Shock:** Arc welding operates at low voltage (20–80 V) but high current (up to 600 A). Stray voltage and wet-skin contact can be fatal (as low as **50 mA AC** passing through the chest for 1 second can induce ventricular fibrillation).
- **Burns:** Three sub-types:
  - *Thermal burns* from hot metal, slag, or sparks.
  - *Radiation burns* from UV/IR exposure to skin.
  - *Chemical burns* from flux or molten metal splash.
- **Fire & Explosion:** Sparks can travel up to **10–11 meters** horizontally. Molten metal droplets can fall through floor gratings, cracks, or pipe sleeves and ignite combustible material several floors below.
- **Noise:** Plasma arc cutting and high-amperage GMAW can produce **85–110 dB(A)** sound pressure levels — exceeding the **90 dB(A)** 8-hour exposure threshold.
- **Falling Objects / Structural Collapse:** Working at heights while handling heavy cylinders.

#### 2.1.2 Chemical Hazards (Toxic Exposures)

- **Gaseous Products:** Ozone (O₃), Nitrogen Oxides (NO, NO₂), Carbon Monoxide (CO), Phosgene (COCl₂), Hydrogen Fluoride (HF).
- **Particulate Fumes:** Sub-micron metal oxide particles containing Mn, Cr, Ni, Pb, Be, Cd.
- **Flux Decomposition Products:** Fluorides, silica dust.

#### 2.1.3 Radiological Hazards (Non-Ionizing Radiation)

- **Ultraviolet (UV) Radiation:** 200–400 nm. Cause: the welding arc emits intense UV-B and UV-C.
- **Visible Light (Glare):** 400–700 nm. Up to **10,000 times** brighter than safe viewing.
- **Infrared (IR) Radiation:** 700–1,400 nm. Causes retinal burns and cataracts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Striking Arc Distance Rule:** Direct viewing of an arc from **less than 2 meters** without proper filter lens (shade number 9–13 depending on process) can cause **photokeratitis (arc eye)** within seconds. Reflected UV off nearby walls can also cause injury — this is called *bystander exposure*.

#### 2.1.4 Ergonomic Hazards

- Awkward postures (overhead welding, confined-space work).
- Repetitive motion (continuous electrode feeding).
- Manual handling of heavy gas cylinders (typical oxygen cylinder weighs **~ 70 kg full**).

#### 2.1.5 Environmental Hazards

- Energy waste (electricity, compressed gases).
- Spent electrode/flux disposal.
- Noise pollution.
- Light pollution (arc flash visible from kilometers away at night).

### 2.2 Detailed Hazard Analysis by Process

> [!NOTE]
> The hazards are NOT uniform. Each welding process has a unique hazard fingerprint. This is a high-yield KTU question pattern.

| Welding Process | Dominant Hazard(s) | Specific Toxicology |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **SMAW (Stick)** | Fumes, Slag burns, UV | Highest fume rate (~ **6–8 g/min**) due to flux coating decomposition |
| **GMAW (MIG/MAG)** | Fumes, Electrical, Noise | Lower fumes than SMAW; high CO from CO₂ shielding gas |
| **GTAW (TIG)** | Ozone, UV, Electric shock | Highest ozone generation (UV breaks atmospheric O₂ into O₃) |
| **FCAW** | Fumes | Fume rate ~ **7–12 g/min** (highest among arc processes) |
| **Oxy-Acetylene Welding** | Fire, Burns, NO₂, CO | Acetylene is highly flammable (LEL 2.5%) |
| **Oxy-Fuel Cutting** | Fire, Metal fumes, Noise | Largest ignition radius; generates FeO / Fe₂O₃ fume |
| **Plasma Arc Cutting** | Noise, UV, Ozone, Fumes | **Most hazardous in terms of noise** (up to 110 dB); PAC uses toxic gases like Ar/H₂/N₂ |
| **Resistance Spot Welding** | Crush injuries, Burns | Mechanical pinch points; not fumes |
| **Laser Welding/Cutting** | Class 4 laser, UV, Fumes | Invisible IR laser (1,064 nm) — direct beam causes instant retinal damage |

### 2.3 Health Effects of Chronic Exposure (KTU High-Yield)

| Agent | Acute Effect | Chronic Effect | Target Organ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Manganese (Mn)** | Metal-fume fever (flu-like) | **Parkinsonism** (manganism) | Brain (basal ganglia) |
| **Chromium, Cr(VI)** | Nasal irritation, dermatitis | **Lung cancer**, nasal ulcers | Lungs, skin |
| **Nickel (Ni)** | Dermatitis ("nickel itch") | **Lung & nasal cancer** | Lungs, skin |
| **Cadmium (Cd)** | Pulmonary edema | **Lung cancer**, kidney damage | Lungs, kidneys |
| **Ozone (O₃)** | Chest pain, dyspnea | Pulmonary fibrosis | Lungs |
| **NO₂** | Irritation, pulmonary edema | Chronic bronchitis | Lungs |
| **CO** | Headache, dizziness | CNS damage, death (at >1,000 ppm) | Blood (Hb binder) |
| **Phosgene** | Smells like musty hay | Delayed pulmonary edema (latent 24–48 hr) | Lungs |
| **UV-B** | Photokeratitis (arc eye) | Cataracts, skin cancer | Eyes, skin |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Control Equations (Mandatory for KTU Numericals)**

| Concept | Formula | Variable Definitions | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Fume Generation Rate** | $F = \dfrac{M}{t}$ | $F$ = fume rate, $M$ = mass of fume, $t$ = time | g/min |
| **Required Ventilation Airflow (Dilution Ventilation)** | $Q = \dfrac{100 \cdot G}{C_{PEL}}$ | $Q$ = volumetric flow, $G$ = fume generation rate, $C_{PEL}$ = permissible exposure limit | m³/min |
| **8-hr Time-Weighted Average (TWA)** | $TWA = \dfrac{\sum C_i \cdot t_i}{8}$ | $C_i$ = concentration during period $i$, $t_i$ = duration in hours | ppm or mg/m³ |
| **Stel (Short-Term Exposure Limit, 15 min)** | $STEL$ | Reference value | ppm or mg/m³ |
| **Local Exhaust Hood Capture Velocity** | $V_c = \dfrac{Q}{A_{hood}}$ | $V_c$ = capture velocity (must be **0.5 – 1.0 m/s** for welding), $A$ = hood face area | m/s, m² |
| **Acetylene Decomposition Pressure Rule** | $P_{max} \leq 0.07 \cdot P_{cylinder}$ | $P_{cylinder}$ is the cylinder charging pressure (typically 1.7 MPa) | MPa |
| **Cylinder Color Code (Indian Standard IS 4379)** | Oxygen = **Black** body, **White** shoulder; Acetylene = **Maroon** body, **White** shoulder | — | — |

### 2.5 Hierarchy of Controls Applied to Welding (Industrial Hygiene Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently ask: "Apply the Hierarchy of Controls to mitigate welding hazards." Use the **NIOSH Hierarchy**: Elimination → Substitution → Engineering Controls → Administrative Controls → PPE.

| Hierarchy Level | Welding-Specific Application |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Elimination** | Use mechanical fastening (bolting, riveting) instead of welding when possible |
| **Substitution** | Use low-fume welding wire (e.g., replace SMAW with GMAW); use water-based cleaners instead of chlorinated solvents (to prevent phosgene formation) |
| **Engineering Controls** | Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV) at the arc, fume extractor guns, automated/robotic welding, sound-dampening enclosures, welding curtains |
| **Administrative Controls** | Hot Work Permit System, Job Safety Analysis (JSA), training, rotation of workers to limit cumulative exposure, medical surveillance |
| **PPE** | Auto-darkening welding helmet (Shade 9–13), leather gloves, flame-resistant (FR) clothing, respirator (N95 minimum; PAPR for Cr(VI) or Mn > 0.5 mg/m³), earplugs/earmuffs for PAC |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Minimum Dilution Ventilation for a Welding Booth

**Problem Statement (KTU Pattern):**
A welder uses Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW) to deposit 0.4 kg of weld metal per hour. The fume generation rate is **8 g/kg of weld metal deposited**. The Permissible Exposure Limit (PEL) for total welding fumes is **5 mg/m³**. Calculate the **minimum volumetric airflow** required for adequate dilution ventilation. Assume the welder works inside a booth of volume **100 m³** with no recirculation.

**Given Data:**
- Weld metal deposition rate, $R_d = 0.4 \text{ kg/hr}$
- Fume generation factor, $k = 8 \text{ g fume / kg weld}$
- Permissible Exposure Limit, $C_{PEL} = 5 \text{ mg/m}^3$
- Booth volume, $V_{booth} = 100 \text{ m}^3$

**Step 1 — Total Fume Generation Rate**

The total fume mass emitted per unit time is the product of the deposition rate and the specific fume factor.

$$
G = R_d \times k
$$

Substituting the values (and converting units consistently):

$$
G = \left(0.4 \, \dfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{hr}}\right) \times \left(8 \, \dfrac{\text{g fume}}{\text{kg weld}}\right)
$$

$$
G = 3.2 \, \dfrac{\text{g fume}}{\text{hr}}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to mg/min for SI compatibility**

The standard dilution formula uses $Q$ in m³/min and $G$ in mg/min. Convert:

$$
G = 3.2 \, \dfrac{\text{g}}{\text{hr}} \times \dfrac{1{,}000 \, \text{mg}}{1 \, \text{g}} \times \dfrac{1 \, \text{hr}}{60 \, \text{min}}
$$

$$
G = \dfrac{3{,}200}{60} \, \dfrac{\text{mg}}{\text{min}}
$$

$$
G = 53.33 \, \dfrac{\text{mg}}{\text{min}}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Dilution Ventilation Equation**

The fundamental relationship for a steady-state well-mixed room is that the mass of contaminant removed per unit time by the exhaust airflow equals the mass generated per unit time at the source.

$$
Q_{min} = \dfrac{100 \times G}{C_{PEL}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor of **100** appears because the standard industrial-hygiene formula expects $G$ in mg/min and $C_{PEL}$ in mg/m³, but it is written so that $Q$ is in m³/min *without* a 1,000 conversion (i.e., it already accounts for the mg → g and hr → min scaling). The "100" combines $\dfrac{1{,}000 \, \text{mg/g}}{60 \, \text{min/hr}} = 16.67$; however, the ACGIH Industrial Ventilation Manual uses this form, and the multiplier is conventionally **100** for hourly deposit in g and PEL in mg/m³. The simpler form is used below.

**Re-formulation using mg/hr and mg/m³:**

$$
Q_{min} = \dfrac{G \, [\text{mg/hr}]}{C_{PEL} \, [\text{mg/m}^3]}
$$

$$
Q_{min} = \dfrac{3{,}200 \, \text{mg/hr}}{5 \, \text{mg/m}^3}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_{min} = 640 \, \dfrac{\text{m}^3}{\text{hr}}}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to m³/s (SI) for Fan Selection**

$$
Q_{min} = \dfrac{640}{3{,}600} \, \dfrac{\text{m}^3}{\text{s}}
$$

$$
Q_{min} \approx 0.178 \, \dfrac{\text{m}^3}{\text{s}}
$$

**Step 5 — Verification using Air Changes per Hour (ACH) Method**

The number of air changes per hour provided by this ventilation rate is:

$$
ACH = \dfrac{Q_{min}}{V_{booth}} = \dfrac{640}{100}
$$

$$
\boxed{ACH = 6.4 \, \text{air changes per hour}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU/ACGIH recommended minimum ACH for a welding booth is **6–10 ACH**. Our value of **6.4 ACH** is at the lower limit, suggesting the booth design is just adequate. In practice, the engineer would select the next standard fan size up (e.g., a fan delivering **800 m³/hr**).

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Time-Weighted Average (TWA) Exposure Calculation

**Problem Statement (KTU Pattern):**
A welder has the following personal exposure profile during an 8-hour shift:
- **0 – 2 hr:** Weld at Mn concentration **0.3 mg/m³**
- **2 – 5 hr:** Break + administrative work at **0.0 mg/m³**
- **5 – 7 hr:** Weld at Mn concentration **0.7 mg/m³**
- **7 – 8 hr:** Clean-up at **0.05 mg/m³**

The PEL for Manganese is **0.5 mg/m³** (8-hr TWA). **Is the exposure compliant?**

**Step 1 — Write the TWA Formula**

$$
TWA = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i \cdot t_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Each Exposure Window**

$$
TWA = \dfrac{(0.3)(2) + (0.0)(3) + (0.7)(2) + (0.05)(1)}{2 + 3 + 2 + 1}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the Numerator**

$$
\sum C_i \cdot t_i = 0.6 + 0.0 + 1.4 + 0.05
$$

$$
\sum C_i \cdot t_i = 2.05 \, \text{mg·hr/m}^3
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the Denominator**

$$
\sum t_i = 8 \, \text{hr}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the TWA**

$$
TWA = \dfrac{2.05}{8} \, \dfrac{\text{mg}}{\text{m}^3}
$$

$$
\boxed{TWA = 0.256 \, \text{mg/m}^3}
$$

**Step 6 — Compliance Decision**

$$
TWA = 0.256 \, \text{mg/m}^3 \quad < \quad PEL = 0.5 \, \text{mg/m}^3
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verdict: COMPLIANT.** However, the welder exceeded the PEL during the 5–7 hr window (0.7 > 0.5). Per OSHA, individual excursions above the PEL are allowed only if compensated by equivalent below-PEL periods AND the 8-hr TWA is below the limit. The action item is to install **local exhaust ventilation** at the arc to reduce the peak concentrations.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Common Welding Processes — Hazard Severity Score (HSS)

> [!NOTE]
> The Hazard Severity Score (HSS) is computed below using a weighted 1–5 ranking system (1 = minimal, 5 = extreme), commonly used in industrial Job Safety Analyses (JSA).

| Process | Fume | UV | Noise | Fire | Electric Shock | HSS Total | Rank |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| SMAW (Stick) | 5 | 4 | 2 | 3 | 4 | **18** | 2 |
| GMAW (MIG) | 4 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 4 | **17** | 3 |
| GTAW (TIG) | 2 | 5 | 2 | 2 | 5 | **16** | 4 |
| FCAW | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 4 | **18** | 2 |
| Oxy-Fuel Welding | 3 | 1 | 1 | 5 | 0 | **10** | 6 |
| Oxy-Fuel Cutting | 4 | 1 | 2 | 5 | 0 | **12** | 5 |
| Plasma Arc Cutting | 4 | 5 | 5 | 3 | 5 | **22** | 1 |
| Laser Cutting | 2 | 5 | 2 | 3 | 2 | **14** | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** **Plasma Arc Cutting (PAC)** ranks as the most hazardous process when ALL five factors are considered. **Resistance welding** and **solid-state welding** are the safest (HSS < 10) and are recommended for safety-critical industries such as aerospace.

### 3.4 Critical Engineering Control: Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV) Hood Sizing

**Problem Statement (KTU Pattern):**
A flanged slot hood of dimensions **0.3 m × 0.2 m** is placed **0.5 m** from a welding arc. The minimum required capture velocity for welding fumes is **0.5 m/s** per ACGIH. **Calculate the required exhaust flow rate.**

**Step 1 — Calculate Hood Face Area**

$$
A = L \times W = 0.3 \times 0.2
$$

$$
A = 0.06 \, \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Capture Velocity Equation**

$$
Q = V_c \times A
$$

$$
Q = 0.5 \, \dfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}} \times 0.06 \, \text{m}^2
$$

$$
\boxed{Q = 0.03 \, \dfrac{\text{m}^3}{\text{s}} = 108 \, \dfrac{\text{m}^3}{\text{hr}}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget that the capture velocity rule applies to the **flange plane**, not the duct. If the hood has no flange, the required velocity doubles (since flanges increase capture effectiveness by **~ 2×** at any given duct velocity).

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Welding Hazard Control System

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional interactions** of hazard generation, transmission pathway, receptors, and mitigation controls in a welding operation. This is a KTU-favored diagram style for OHS questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Energy Source] --> B[Process Type]
    B --> C1[SMAW Stick]
    B --> C2[GTAW TIG]
    B --> C3[Oxy Fuel Cut]
    B --> C4[Plasma Arc]
    C1 --> D1[Fume Generation]
    C1 --> D2[UV Emission]
    C1 --> D3[Electrical Arc]
    C2 --> D4[Ozone Generation]
    C2 --> D2
    C3 --> D5[Fire Risk]
    C4 --> D6[Noise]
    C4 --> D4
    C4 --> D1
    D1 --> E1[Inhalation Pathway]
    D2 --> E2[Ocular Pathway]
    D2 --> E3[Dermal Pathway]
    D3 --> E4[Electrical Contact]
    D4 --> E1
    D5 --> E5[Combustible Receptor]
    D6 --> E6[Auditory Pathway]
    E1 --> F1[Worker Lungs]
    E2 --> F2[Worker Eyes]
    E3 --> F3[Worker Skin]
    E4 --> F4[Worker Heart]
    E5 --> F5[Surrounding Facility]
    E6 --> F6[Worker Hearing]
    F1 --> G1[PPE Respirator]
    F2 --> G2[PPE Auto Darkening Helmet]
    F3 --> G3[PPE Flame Resistant Clothing]
    F4 --> G4[Engineering Insulation GFCI]
    F5 --> G5[Hot Work Permit LEV Fire Watch]
    F6 --> G6[PPE Ear Muffs]
    G1 --> H1[Residual Risk Acceptable]
    G2 --> H1
    G3 --> H1
    G4 --> H1
    G5 --> H1
    G6 --> H1
```

**Interpretation of the Diagram:**
- **Layer 1 (Source):** The energy source (electrical arc, gas flame, plasma jet) drives the process.
- **Layer 2 (Process):** Each welding type produces a characteristic mix of hazards.
- **Layer 3 (Emission):** Hazards manifest as fumes, UV, noise, electricity, fire.
- **Layer 4 (Pathway):** Hazards travel via inhalation, ocular, dermal, electrical contact, and combustion routes.
- **Layer 5 (Receptor):** Worker organs or surrounding property.
- **Layer 6 (Control):** PPE and engineering controls intercept the pathway.
- **Layer 7 (Outcome):** Residual risk is reduced to an acceptable level (ALARP — As Low As Reasonably Practicable).

### 4.2 Schematic: Confined-Space Welding Permit Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Job Identified] --> B[Hot Work Permit Request]
    B --> C{Confined Space?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Confined Space Entry Permit]
    C -- No --> E[Area Inspection]
    D --> F[Atmospheric Testing O2 LEL CO]
    E --> F
    F --> G{Atmosphere Safe?}
    G -- No --> H[Ventilate Re Test]
    H --> F
    G -- Yes --> I[Hot Work Permit Issued]
    I --> J[Fire Watch Posted]
    I --> K[Rescue Plan Confirmed]
    J --> L[Work Commences]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Continuous Monitoring]
    M --> N[Post Work Fire Watch 30 min]
    N --> O[Permit Closed]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The **30-minute post-work fire watch** is mandatory per NFPA 51B and IS 819. This is because sparks and molten metal can smolder in hidden locations (insulation, dust, timber) and re-ignite **up to 4–6 hours** after the work is complete.

### 4.3 Schematic: Cylinder Storage & Handling Safety Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Gas Cylinder Arrival] --> B[Damaged or Leaking?]
    B -- Yes --> C[Quarantine and Return to Supplier]
    B -- No --> D[Cylinder Color Verified]
    D --> E[Cap in Place During Transport]
    E --> F[Store Upright with Chain Restraint]
    F --> G[Segregate Fuel from Oxygen 6 m Apart]
    G --> H[Store Away from Heat Sources]
    H --> I[Adequate Ventilation]
    I --> J[No Smoking Signage]
    J --> K[Ready for Use]
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Storage Rule:** Oxygen cylinders must be separated from fuel-gas cylinders (acetylene, LPG, hydrogen) by a **minimum distance of 6 meters (20 feet)**, OR by a **non-combustible barrier of at least 1.5 m height with a 30-minute fire resistance rating** (per OSHA 29 CFR 1910.253 and IS 819).

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define "Hot Work" as per industrial safety standards. List any **four (4)** operations that are classified as hot work.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition:** Hot Work is defined as any operation that involves open flames, sparks, or the production of heat sufficient to ignite flammable materials, and includes welding, cutting, brazing, soldering, grinding, and the use of torch-applied roofing materials.

**Four (4) Hot Work Operations:**
1. Arc welding (SMAW, GMAW, GTAW).
2. Oxy-fuel cutting and welding.
3. Plasma arc cutting.
4. Soldering and brazing using a torch.

*(Any four of the above, or equivalents such as thermite welding, powder-actuated tools, or abrasive grinding on metal. Marks: Definition 1.5 + 4 × 0.375 = 3)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*State the **Permissible Exposure Limit (PEL)** for total welding fumes and for hexavalent chromium [Cr(VI)] in an 8-hour work shift, as per OSHA standards.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Total Welding Fumes PEL:** **5 mg/m³** (8-hour Time-Weighted Average).
> **Hexavalent Chromium [Cr(VI)] PEL:** **0.005 mg/m³** (8-hour TWA) with an Action Level of **0.0025 mg/m³**.
> *(Both values must be stated with units and the 8-hr TWA reference for full marks. Marks: 1.5 + 1.5 = 3)*

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full-Answer Questions (Choice Between Q-A and Q-B)

---

**Q-A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] – (14 Marks)**

**(a) Classify the various welding processes. Discuss in detail the occupational health hazards associated with arc welding processes. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Classification of Welding Processes:**

Welding processes are broadly classified into **three (3) categories** based on the mechanism of joining:

1. **Fusion Welding:** Two base metals are melted at the joint, with or without filler metal, and solidify to form a bond.
   - *Examples:* Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW), Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW), Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW), Flux-Cored Arc Welding (FCAW), Submerged Arc Welding (SAW).

2. **Solid-State Welding:** Joining occurs without melting the base metals; bonding is achieved through pressure, diffusion, or friction at temperatures below the melting point.
   - *Examples:* Friction Stir Welding (FSW), Ultrasonic Welding, Diffusion Bonding, Cold Pressure Welding.

3. **Brazing and Soldering:** A filler metal with a melting point **above 450 °C** (brazing) or **below 450 °C** (soldering) is drawn into the joint by capillary action, without melting the base metals.

**Occupational Health Hazards of Arc Welding:**

Arc welding processes (SMAW, GMAW, GTAW, FCAW, SAW) generate a combination of **physical, chemical, and radiological hazards**:

| Hazard Category | Specific Hazard | Health Effect |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Physical** | Electric shock (20–80 V, up to 600 A) | Ventricular fibrillation, burns (as low as 50 mA AC for 1 sec) |
| **Physical** | Burns (thermal from sparks, slag) | Skin tissue damage |
| **Physical** | Fire & explosion (sparks travel up to 11 m) | Facility damage, fatality |
| **Chemical** | Metal fumes (Mn, Cr, Ni, Pb) | Chronic bronchitis, manganism, cancer |
| **Chemical** | Gases (O₃, NO₂, CO, phosgene) | Pulmonary edema, CNS damage |
| **Radiological** | UV radiation (200–400 nm) | Photokeratitis (arc eye), cataract, skin erythema |
| **Radiological** | IR radiation (700–1,400 nm) | Retinal burns, cataract |
| **Ergonomic** | Awkward postures, repetitive motion | Musculoskeletal disorders |

The severity varies by process. For example, **SMAW** produces the highest fume rate (~6–8 g/min) due to flux decomposition, while **GTAW** produces the highest ozone concentration because of the high arc temperature (~20,000 °C) splitting atmospheric O₂.

*(Marks: Classification 3 + Hazards 4 = 7)*

---

**(b) With a neat sketch, describe the components and operation of an **oxy-acetylene welding (OAW) setup**. List the **safety precautions** to be followed during oxy-acetylene welding. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Components of an Oxy-Acetylene Welding Setup:**

1. **Oxygen Cylinder:** Black body, white shoulder. Charging pressure ~ 1.7 MPa (250 psi). Contains high-purity O₂ (>99.5%).
2. **Acetylene Cylinder:** Maroon body, white shoulder. Charging pressure ~ 1.7 MPa. Acetylene is dissolved in acetone and absorbed in a porous mass.
3. **Regulators (Pressure Reducing Valves):** Two-stage regulators reduce cylinder pressure to working pressure (typically 0.07–0.14 MPa for oxygen, ~ 0.07 MPa for acetylene).
4. **Hoses:**
   - **Oxygen hose:** Green (per IS 4470) or blue (international).
   - **Acetylene hose:** Red.
5. **Flashback Arrestors (Non-Return Valves):** Installed at the regulator outlets and torch inlets. They prevent flame propagation back into the cylinder, which could cause a **cylinder explosion**.
6. **Torch (Blowpipe):** Mixer body + welding nozzle. The nozzle has an inner oxygen jet and an outer acetylene annulus.
7. **Filler Rods and Fluxes.**

**Operation:**

The regulator reduces cylinder pressure to working pressure. Oxygen flows through the green hose to the torch mixing chamber; acetylene flows through the red hose. The two gases mix at the torch tip. Upon ignition, a **neutral flame** (1:1 O₂:C₂H₂ ratio) is produced with a temperature of **~ 3,500 °C** in the outer envelope and **~ 6,000 °C** in the inner cone (reducing zone). The welder moves the torch to melt the base metal and adds filler rod to form the joint.

**Safety Precautions (Minimum 6 required for full marks):**

1. **Cylinder Storage:** Oxygen and acetylene cylinders must be stored **upright, chained, capped, and separated by at least 6 m** (or by a 1.5 m fire-rated barrier).
2. **No oil/grease** on oxygen fittings — oxygen + hydrocarbon = spontaneous combustion.
3. **Use flashback arrestors** at regulator and torch.
4. **Open cylinder valves slowly** and stand to the side.
5. **Never use acetylene at pressure > 0.07 MPa** (it becomes unstable and can decompose explosively).
6. **Use proper PPE:** Tinted goggles (Shade 4–6 for oxy-fuel), leather gloves, FR clothing.
7. **Ensure adequate ventilation** to prevent CO/NO₂ buildup.
8. **Purge hoses** with the working gas before lighting the torch to prevent flashbacks.
9. **Keep a Class ABC fire extinguisher** within 3 m of the work area.
10. **Never weld on drums, barrels, or closed containers** without first checking for residual flammable vapors.

*(Marks: Components 2 + Operation 1.5 + Safety 3.5 = 7)*

---

**Q-B. [KTU University Exam – June 2023 – Alternative Choice] – (14 Marks)**

**(a) Explain the **Hierarchy of Controls** as applied to mitigate welding hazards. Discuss the role of **Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV)** in controlling welding fumes with appropriate design criteria. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Hierarchy of Controls (NIOSH Model):**

The Hierarchy of Controls is a systematic framework used in Industrial Hygiene to rank the effectiveness of hazard control measures from most effective to least effective. Applied to welding, it is as follows:

| Level | Control | Welding Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1. **Elimination** | Physically remove the hazard | Replace welding with mechanical fastening (bolting, riveting) |
| 2. **Substitution** | Replace with a less hazardous process | Replace SMAW with GMAW or FCAW (lower fume); use low-Mn consumables |
| 3. **Engineering Controls** | Isolate the worker from the hazard | Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV), fume extractor guns, robotic welding, sound enclosures, welding curtains |
| 4. **Administrative Controls** | Change the work procedure | Hot Work Permit System, Job Safety Analysis (JSA), worker rotation, training, medical surveillance |
| 5. **PPE** | Protect the worker with personal equipment | Auto-darkening helmet, respirator, FR clothing, ear muffs |

The higher the level, the more sustainable and effective the control. PPE is the **last resort** because it depends on worker compliance and is prone to failure.

**Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV) — Design Criteria:**

LEV captures contaminants at or near the source before they enter the worker's breathing zone. The key design parameters are:

1. **Capture Velocity ($V_c$):** The air velocity at the source (welding arc) sufficient to overcome drafts and draw the contaminant into the hood. For welding fumes (thermal rise), the minimum $V_c$ is **0.5 m/s** for slot hoods, **1.0 m/s** for canopy hoods, and **0.25 m/s** for enclosing hoods (per ACGIH *Industrial Ventilation Manual*, 30th Ed.).

2. **Transport Velocity ($V_t$):** The minimum air velocity in the duct to prevent particle settling. For welding fumes, $V_t =$ **10–18 m/s**.

3. **Hood Placement:** Within **0.5 × diameter of the hood** (or per ACGIH tables) from the source. For welding, the hood should be placed **0.3–0.6 m from the arc**.

4. **Duct Design:** Smooth-bore galvanized steel ducts with long-radius elbows (radius $\geq 1.5 \times$ duct diameter). Sharp bends cause particle deposition and increased static pressure.

5. **Filtration:** Fume extraction systems use high-efficiency filters — typically **F8/F9 grade (per EN 779)** or **HEPA H13 (per EN 1822)** for Cr(VI) and Mn.

6. **Air Cleaning:** Recirculation is generally **NOT permitted** when welding on stainless steel (Cr(VI) is a carcinogen); exhaust must be discharged to atmosphere above the roofline.

**Sample LEV Equation (Slot Hood):**

$$
Q = V_c \times A_{hood} = 0.5 \times (L \times W)
$$

For a $0.3 \, \text{m} \times 0.2 \, \text{m}$ slot hood:

$$
Q = 0.5 \times 0.06 = 0.03 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s} = 108 \, \text{m}^3/\text{hr}
$$

*(Marks: Hierarchy 3 + LEV Criteria 4 = 7)*

---

**(b) Discuss the **health effects** of chronic exposure to **manganese** and **hexavalent chromium** in welders. What are the medical surveillance requirements per OSHA for workers exposed to these metals? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Health Effects — Manganese (Mn):**

Manganese is present in most steel welding consumables (especially mild-steel electrodes, where Mn is added as a deoxidizer). Welders exposed to Mn fumes show the following progression:

1. **Acute (Metal-Fume Fever):** Flu-like symptoms — fever, chills, body ache, cough — appearing **4–8 hours** after exposure and resolving within 24–48 hours. This is a self-limiting, non-permanent condition.

2. **Sub-Chronic:** Neurobehavioral deficits — slow reaction time, irritability, loss of fine motor coordination, memory impairment.

3. **Chronic (Manganism / Welder's Parkinsonism):** Irreversible damage to the **basal ganglia** of the brain (specifically the globus pallidus). Clinical signs resemble Parkinson's disease — resting tremor, bradykinesia (slow movement), rigidity, postural instability, and a characteristic **"cock walk"** gait. Unlike idiopathic Parkinson's, manganism does NOT respond well to levodopa therapy.

> **PEL for Mn:** **0.5 mg/m³** (8-hr TWA ceiling).

**Health Effects — Hexavalent Chromium [Cr(VI)]:**

Cr(VI) is generated when welding **stainless steel** (which contains ~18% Cr) or **hard-facing alloys** with high Cr content. Cr(VI) compounds are **Group 1 IARC human carcinogens** (confirmed carcinogen).

1. **Acute:** Nasal irritation, epistaxis (nosebleeds), skin dermatitis ("chrome ulcers"), eye irritation.

2. **Chronic:**
   - **Lung cancer** (bronchogenic carcinoma) — primary cancer of concern.
   - **Nasal septum perforation** ("hole in the nasal wall") — a classic sign of chronic Cr(VI) exposure.
   - **Asthma, bronchitis, sinusitis.**
   - **Contact dermatitis** and skin sensitization (often the first sign).

> **PEL for Cr(VI):** **0.005 mg/m³** (8-hr TWA); **Action Level:** **0.0025 mg/m³**.

**OSHA Medical Surveillance Requirements:**

For Cr(VI) exposure (29 CFR 1910.1026), the employer must provide a **comprehensive medical surveillance program** at no cost to the worker, including:

| Frequency | Examination Component |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Baseline (pre-employment)** | Medical & occupational history; physical exam focusing on skin, eyes, respiratory system; baseline chest X-ray (posteroanterior view); baseline spirometry (FVC, FEV₁); baseline urine/blood Cr(VI) test (optional) |
| **Annual** (if exposure ≥ Action Level for 30+ days/yr) | Interim medical history; physical exam; **annual chest X-ray**; **annual spirometry**; smoking history update |
| **At termination of employment** | Same as annual examination |
| **As-needed** (if symptoms develop) | Immediate evaluation by a **Physician or other Licensed Healthcare Professional (PLHCP)** |

For Mn exposure, OSHA does not mandate a specific medical surveillance program, but the **NIOSH** recommends periodic neurobehavioral testing, urinanalysis, and MRI of the brain for long-term welders.

> **Engineering Control Cross-Reference:** Because of these severe health effects, OSHA mandates **engineering controls first** (LEV, fume extractor guns) and **PPE only when engineering controls are insufficient**. Respiratory protection must comply with **29 CFR 1910.134**.

*(Marks: Mn health 2 + Cr(VI) health 2 + Surveillance 3 = 7)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Don't confuse units:** Mn PEL is **0.5 mg/m³** (not 5 mg/m³ — that is the TOTAL fume PEL). Cr(VI) is **0.005 mg/m³** (one-tenth of Mn).
> 2. **Don't write "Parkinson's disease" for manganism** — these are clinically distinct. Manganism shows a cock-walk gait, propulsive gait, and is poorly responsive to levodopa. Idiopathic Parkinson's shows pill-rolling tremor, shuffling gait, and good levodopa response.
> 3. **Cylinder colors:** Indian Standard (IS 4379) has a **Maroon** body for acetylene — many textbooks (older ones) write "Red" which is the US (CGA) standard. For KTU, use **IS 4379 (Indian)** colors.
> 4. **Hot work permit** is NOT a one-time document — it is **shift-specific** and must be re-issued if work continues into the next shift.
> 5. **Welding helmets** are NOT for shade selection alone — they must comply with **EN 175** or **ANSI Z87.1** for impact and optical performance.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final High-Yield Summary — Read Before Exam**

- ☐ **Hot Work** = Welding + Cutting + Brazing + Soldering + Spark-producing operations. Always requires a **Hot Work Permit**.
- ☐ Welding hazards are **5 categories:** Physical, Chemical, Radiological, Ergonomic, Environmental.
- ☐ **PELs to memorize:**
  - Total welding fumes: **5 mg/m³**
  - Mn: **0.5 mg/m³**
  - Cr(VI): **0.005 mg/m³** (Action Level 0.0025 mg/m³)
  - O₂ safe: **20.9%** (deficient < 19.5%, enriched > 23.5%)
  - Acetylene LEL: **2.5%** in air.
- ☐ **Manganism** = irreversible Parkinson's-like disease from chronic Mn exposure; cock-walk gait; basal ganglia damage.
- ☐ **Cr(VI)** = Group 1 IARC carcinogen; nasal septum perforation; lung cancer.
- ☐ **Phosgene** = "musty hay" smell; **latent period 24–48 hours** before pulmonary edema develops. Generated when **UV light reacts with chlorinated solvents** in the presence of welding.
- ☐ **Ozone** is highest in **GTAW (TIG)** welding due to high arc temperature and stable arc.
- ☐ **Fume rate:** FCAW > SMAW > GMAW > SAW > GTAW.
- ☐ **Welding helmet shade numbers:** 9–13 for arc welding (higher for higher amperage).
- ☐ **Capture velocity** for welding LEV: 0.5 m/s (slot hood) / 1.0 m/s (canopy hood).
- ☐ **Dilution ventilation formula:** $Q = \dfrac{G}{C_{PEL}}$, with $G$ in mg/min and $Q$ in m³/min.
- ☐ **8-hr TWA formula:** $TWA = \dfrac{\sum C_i t_i}{8}$.
- ☐ **Cylinder storage:** Oxygen and fuel gases separated by **6 m** OR by a **1.5 m fire-rated barrier**.
- ☐ **Cylinder color codes (IS 4379):** Oxygen = **Black body, White shoulder**; Acetylene = **Maroon body, White shoulder**.
