| Repetitive strain (typing) | 4 | 3 | **12** | Ergonomic chair (L3) + breaks (L4) | 2 | 2 | **4** | **67 %** |
| Asbestos exposure | 2 | 5 | **10** | Enclosure + HEPA + full Tyvek suit (L3+L5) | 1 | 4 | **4** | **60 %** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Note:** A control measure is considered **ALARP** (*As Low As Reasonably Practicable*) only if the post-control risk is ≤ 4 (low) AND further reduction would require grossly disproportionate cost (i.e., the "Grossly Disproportionate Test"). Always mention ALARP in the conclusion.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchy of Controls — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hazard Identified in Risk Assessment] --> B{Is Elimination Possible?}
    B -- Yes --> C[L1 Elimination - Physically Remove Hazard]
    B -- No --> D{Is Substitution Feasible?}
    D -- Yes --> E[L2 Substitution - Replace with Lower Hazard]
    D -- No --> F{Engineering Control Achievable?}
    F -- Yes --> G[L3 Engineering - LEV, Enclosure, Guards, Interlocks]
    F -- No --> H{Administrative Control Applicable?}
    H -- Yes --> I[L4 Administrative - SOP, Training, Rotation, Signs]
    H -- No --> J[L5 PPE - Last Resort - Verify APF, Fit, Maintenance]
    C --> K[Re-evaluate Residual Risk]
    E --> K
    G --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L{Residual Risk <= 4 AND ALARP?}
    L -- Yes --> M[ACCEPT - Document and Monitor]
    L -- No --> N[Return to Hierarchy - Move Up a Tier]
```

## 4.2 Local Exhaust Ventilation System — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Contaminant Source - e.g. Welding Arc] --> A2[Hood - Capture Velocity Vc 0.5 m/s]
    A2 --> A3[Duct - Transport Velocity Vt 18 m/s]
    A3 --> A4[Air Cleaning Unit - Filter or Scrubber]
    A4 --> A5[Exhaust Fan - Sized by Q and Delta P]
    A5 --> A6[Discharge Stack - 3 m above roofline]
    A6 --> A7[Atmospheric Release - Within AAQ Norms]
    A3 -.Friction Loss.-> A5
    A2 -.Hood Entry Loss.-> A5
```

## 4.3 PPE Selection Logic Flow (Sequential)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Identified Hazard] --> P2{Type?}
    P2 -- Respiratory --> Q1[Respirator Class - Air Purifying vs SCBA]
    P2 -- Auditory --> Q2[Hearing Protector - NRR rating check]
    P2 -- Chemical --> Q3[Chemical Compatibility Chart - Glove Material]
    P2 -- Fall --> Q4[Harness Class A/B/E - Anchor strength 22 kN]
    Q1 --> R1[Check APF >= Contaminant TLV Ratio]
    Q2 --> R2[Check NRR method - OSHA 29 CFR 1910.95]
    Q3 --> R3[Check Permeation Breakthrough Time]
    Q4 --> R4[Check Fall Clearance - 5.5 ft min]
    R1 --> S1[Fit Test - Qualitative or Quantitative]
    R2 --> S1
    R3 --> S1
    R4 --> S1
    S1 --> T1[Train on Use, Care, Replacement]
    T1 --> U1[Document Issue and Acknowledgement]
```

## 4.4 Permits-to-Work System — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    W1[Permit Requester] --> W2[Permit Issuer - Authorised Person]
    W2 --> W3{Hazard Class?}
    W3 -- Hot Work --> X1[Hot Work Permit - Welding, Cutting]
    W3 -- Confined Space --> X2[Confined Space Entry Permit]
    W3 -- Electrical --> X3[Electrical Isolation LOTO Permit]
    W3 -- Height --> X4[Work at Height Permit]
    W3 -- Chemical --> X5[Chemical Work Permit]
    X1 --> Y1[Precautions Verified - Fire Watch, Gas Test]
    X2 --> Y1
    X3 --> Y1
    X4 --> Y1
    X5 --> Y1
    Y1 --> Z1[Permit Valid for Max 8-12 Hours]
    Z1 --> Z2[Permit Closure and Handover]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]**
**List the five levels of the Hierarchy of Controls in decreasing order of effectiveness and state which level is considered the most reliable.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The five levels in decreasing order of effectiveness are:
> 1. **Elimination (L1)** — physical removal of the hazard.
> 2. **Substitution (L2)** — replacing the hazard with a less harmful alternative.
> 3. **Engineering Controls (L3)** — physical systems like LEV, guards, interlocks.
> 4. **Administrative Controls (L4)** — SOPs, training, rotation, signage.
> 5. **Personal Protective Equipment (L5)** — respirators, gloves, helmets, etc.
>
> **Elimination (L1)** is the most reliable because it completely removes the hazard, making the risk *mathematically zero* irrespective of human behaviour. (1 mark for the list, 1 mark for ranking, 1 mark for justification.)

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Differentiate between Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV) and general dilution ventilation. State one limitation of each.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Feature | **LEV** | **General Dilution** |
> |:---|:---|:---| 
> | Principle | Captures contaminant *at source* | Mixes contaminant with large volume of air |
> | Air volume | Small, targeted | Very large |
> | Effectiveness for toxic/point sources | High | Low |
> | Cost | Moderate–High | High (large fans) |
> | Limitation | Ineffective if hood is far from source; depends on worker positioning. | Ineffective for highly toxic or carcinogenic substances; very energy-intensive. |
>
> *(1 mark definition each, 1 mark for limitations.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT** recommend PPE as the *first* control — it is the *last* tier. Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for placing PPE at the top.
> 2. **Do NOT** skip the *pre-control* and *post-control* risk score comparison. The KTU 2024 answer key explicitly requires the $R_{pre}$ vs. $R_{post}$ table for full 14 marks.
> 3. **Do NOT** forget the **ALARP** conclusion — many students stop at the residual risk value and lose the final **1 mark** allocated to it.
> 4. Always quote **at least one standard** (e.g., OSHA 29 CFR 1910.95, Indian Factory Act Sec. 14, ACGIH TLV booklet) — this is a 1-mark *application* marker.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]*

**A manufacturing unit uses toluene as a degreasing agent in an open tank. Workers are reporting headaches and dizziness, and air monitoring shows toluene concentration at 280 mg/m³ against a TLV-TWA of 188 mg/m³. Propose a complete control strategy using the Hierarchy of Controls, and show that the residual risk is ALARP.**

#### (a) **Risk Assessment and Identification of Root Causes (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Confirm the hazard.** Toluene is a neurotoxic aromatic hydrocarbon; chronic exposure affects the central nervous system and is a suspected reproductive toxin.

**Step 2 — Quantify the exposure.**

$$
\text{Excess concentration} = 280 - 188 = 92 \;\text{mg/m}^3
$$

$$
\text{Overshoot ratio} = \frac{280}{188} = 1.49 \;\;(49\% \text{ above TLV})
$$

> **Logic:** An overshoot of nearly 50 % means exposure is unacceptable and *must* be controlled immediately.

**Step 3 — Pre-control risk score using $5 \times 5$ matrix.**

| Parameter | Score | Justification |
|:---|:-:|:---|
| Likelihood (L) | 5 | Exposure occurs every shift |
| Severity (S) | 4 | Chronic neurotoxicity, possible unconsciousness |
| **$R_{pre} = L \times S$** | **20** | **HIGH RISK** — intervention mandatory |

**Step 4 — Identify root causes.**
- Open tank allows evaporation into the breathing zone.
- No LEV hood above the tank.
- No vapour-recovery system.
- Workers are unaware of long-term effects (no training).
- No gloves/suits worn (toluene defats skin).

#### (b) **Proposed Control Strategy with Post-Control Risk (7 marks)**

| Tier | Control Implemented | Justification |
|:-:|:---|:---|
| **L1 Elimination** | Stop using toluene for degreasing; switch to aqueous ultrasonic cleaning tank. | Removes the hazard completely. |
| **L2 Substitution** | (If elimination is impossible) Replace with a higher-flash-point, lower-toxicity solvent such as d-limonene or aqueous detergents. | Lowers inhalation toxicity. |
| **L3 Engineering** | Install a **push-pull LEV hood** with capture velocity ≥ 0.5 m/s at tank edges; provide slot velocity 10 m/s; duct to activated-carbon vapour recovery. | Captures vapour at source; reliable regardless of worker behaviour. |
| **L3 Engineering** | Add a **slotted lip extraction** along the tank perimeter; interlock tank heater with hood fan. | Stops vapour generation when LEV is off. |
| **L4 Administrative** | Implement a **solvent-management plan**, **chemical hygiene plan**, **annual air monitoring**, **medical surveillance** (urinary hippuric acid), and **worker training** on MSDS. | Reduces duration/frequency of exposure. |
| **L5 PPE** | Issue **organic-vapour half-mask respirators** with APF = 10 (verify fit-tested); nitrile gloves; chemical-splash goggles. | Backup protection; APF check: $188 \times 10 = 1880$ mg/m³ ≫ 280 mg/m³ ✔ |

**Post-control risk score:**

| Parameter | Score | Justification |
|:---|:-:|:---|
| Likelihood (L) | 1 | Vapour concentration now < 50 mg/m³ (≈ 27 % of TLV) |
| Severity (S) | 3 | Residual risk if LEV fails |
| **$R_{post} = L \times S$** | **3** | **LOW RISK** |

**Risk Reduction Ratio (RRR):**

$$
\text{RRR} = \frac{20 - 3}{20} \times 100\,\% = 85\,\%
$$

**ALARP conclusion:** Post-control risk is ≤ 4 and the cost of further improvement (e.g., enclosed robotic cleaning) would be *grossly disproportionate* to the marginal benefit. The system is **ALARP-compliant** as per the KTU EHS framework and IS:14489 (Indian Standard on Hazard Control).

> *(Distribution: 1 mark for L1, 1 for L3 LEV design, 1 for ALARP check, 1 for RRR calculation, plus justifications and numerical work.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]*

**A stamping press in a sheet-metal workshop produces a continuous noise level of 102 dB(A) at the operator's station. Workers have been observed working without ear protection. Design a multi-tier control programme to bring the exposure below the KTU/OSHA action level of 85 dB(A) and the permissible exposure limit (PEL) of 90 dB(A) for 8 hours.**

#### (a) **Hazard Quantification and Engineering Control Design (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Measure existing exposure.**

$$
L_{A,8h} = 102 \;\text{dB(A)}
$$

The exposure exceeds the **PEL** (90 dB) by 12 dB. Because every 3-dB increase *doubles* the permissible exposure time:

$$
T_{permissible} = 8 \;\text{hr} \cdot 2^{-(102-90)/3} = 8 \cdot 2^{-4} = 8 \cdot 0.0625 = 0.5 \;\text{hr}
$$

> **Logic:** Workers should not be exposed for more than 30 minutes per shift without protection.

**Step 2 — Engineering controls (Tier 3).**

1. **Isolation by distance:** Relocate the operator's control panel to a sound-proof booth 4 m away. From the inverse-square law:
$$
L_2 = 102 - 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4}{1}\right) = 102 - 12.04 = 89.96 \;\text{dB}
$$
   Marginal improvement; still exceeds 85 dB.

2. **Acoustic enclosure (partial):** Wrap the press with a 3-mm steel sheet ($m = 23.5$ kg/m²) plus 50-mm mineral wool. Transmission loss at 1 kHz:
$$
TL = 20 \log_{10}(1000 \times 23.5) - 47 = 20 \log_{10}(23500) - 47 = 20(4.371) - 47 = 87.4 - 47 = 40.4 \;\text{dB}
$$
   Real-world insertion loss (typically half the TL for reflective barriers) ≈ 15 dB. New SPL:
$$
L_2 = 90 - 15 = 75 \;\text{dB}
$$
   This is well below the 85 dB action level.

3. **Damping:** Apply constrained-layer damping pads to the press frame to reduce structure-borne vibration.

4. **Maintenance:** Replace worn bearings, balance rotating parts, and tighten loose panels — all low-cost Tier 3 measures.

#### (b) **Administrative Controls, PPE Selection, and Verification (7 marks)**

| Tier | Control | Detail |
|:-:|:---|:---|
| **L2 Substitution** | Replace mechanical press with a **servo-electric press** that produces 75 dB(A) at the same stroke rate. | Long-term, capital-intensive but **most sustainable** engineering upgrade. |
| **L3 Engineering** | **Acoustic enclosure** as above; **vibration isolators** beneath the press. | Provides ≥ 15 dB attenuation. |
| **L4 Administrative** | (i) Mark a 2 m exclusion zone with floor markings. (ii) Post the **85 dB boundary** with a mandatory hearing-protection sign. (iii) Conduct **annual audiometric tests**. (iv) Implement **audiometric surveillance programme** as per KTU/OSHA. (v) Provide **noise awareness training** every 6 months. (vi) Maintain a **Hearing Conservation Programme (HCP)**. | Reduces exposure duration and ensures medical monitoring. |
| **L5 PPE** | Issue **ear muffs** with NRR = 30 dB. Protected exposure using OSHA formula: $L_{protected} = 102 - (30 - 7) \div 2 = 102 - 11.5 = 90.5$ dB → still above PEL. Upgrade to **double protection (ear plugs + muffs)** with combined NRR ≈ 36 dB → $L_{protected} = 102 - (36 - 7) \div 2 = 90.75$ dB. **Final PPE choice:** Active noise-cancellation headset with NRR ≥ 35 dB, providing $L_{protected} \le 87$ dB. | **PPE is the LAST resort** — used as a *supplement* to the engineering controls, not as the primary defence. |

**Verification of ALARP:**

| Parameter | Value |
|:---|:---|
| $R_{pre}$ (Likelihood 5 × Severity 4) | 20 — HIGH |
| $R_{post}$ (Likelihood 1 × Severity 3) | 3 — LOW |
| $\text{RRR}$ | $\dfrac{20 - 3}{20} \times 100\,\% = 85\,\%$ |
| Engineering controls in place | ✔ Acoustic enclosure + damping + isolation |
| Administrative controls in place | ✔ HCP + training + medical surveillance |
| PPE for residual/peak exposure | ✔ NRR ≥ 35 dB dual protection |
| **ALARP demonstrated** | ✔ Yes — further reduction is grossly disproportionate in cost |

**Reference standards cited:**
- OSHA 29 CFR 1910.95 (Occupational Noise Exposure)
- IS: 11767 (Specification for Ear Protectors)
- ACGIH TLV booklet, 2024 edition
- KTU EHS Code of Practice (Module 3)

> *(Distribution: 1 mark acoustic calculations, 1 mark NRR derivation, 1 mark HCP design, 1 mark ALARP, balance for engineering/admin/PPE justifications.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ⚡

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Control Measures to Reduce the Risk (Module 3, OECET722)**

- ⭐ **Hierarchy of Controls (5 levels):** Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE. Remember the mnemonic **"Every Safe Engineer Adds PPE"**.
- ⭐ **Most reliable** level is **Elimination (L1)** because risk becomes zero by removing the hazard.
- ⭐ **PPE is the LAST resort**, *not the first*. Examiners will deduct marks if PPE is the first option in your answer.
- ⭐ **Inherently Safer Design (ISD)** comprises L1, L2, and L3 — these are built into the system and do not rely on human behaviour.
- ⭐ **Ventilation formulae** to remember:
  - $Q = V_c \cdot A_h$ (capture)
  - $Q = K \cdot G \div (C_1 - C_2)$ (dilution)
  - $D_d = \sqrt{4Q \div \pi V_t}$ (duct sizing)
- ⭐ **Noise formulae:**
  - $T_{eq} = T_1 \div 2^{(L_1 - L_2)/3}$ (OSHA time-intensity trade-off)
  - $L_2 = L_1 - 20 \log_{10}(r_2 / r_1)$ (inverse-square for point source)
  - $TL = 20 \log_{10}(f \cdot m) - 47$ (barrier transmission loss)
  - OSHA NRR adjustment: subtract 7 dB, then halve the result.
- ⭐ **Risk Reduction Ratio (RRR):** $\dfrac{R_{pre} - R_{post}}{R_{pre}} \times 100\,\%$. A 70 %+ reduction is generally considered effective.
- ⭐ **ALARP principle:** Post-control risk ≤ 4 (low) AND further reduction is grossly disproportionate to cost.
- ⭐ **PPE selection uses APF:** $C_{ambient} \le APF \times TLV$. Always verify fit test, comfort, and compatibility.
- ⭐ **Permits-to-Work (PTW)** are an administrative control for high-risk tasks: hot work, confined space, electrical LOTO, work at height, chemical work. Valid for a *limited time period* (8–12 hours typical).
- ⭐ **Cost hierarchy of controls:** L1 (highest one-time cost) is also the *most economical in the long run*; L5 (PPE) has recurring cost and is *least* economical over time.
- ⭐ **Standards to cite in KTU answers:** OSHA 29 CFR 1910, Indian Factories Act 1948 (Sec. 14, 21, 22, 35–37), ACGIH TLV booklet, IS 14489 (Hazard Control), IS 11767 (Ear Protectors), NFPA 70E (Electrical Safety).
- ⭐ **ALARP** is the standard *closing line* of any KTU 14-mark answer on control measures.
- ⭐ **Don't forget** to compare pre-control and post-control risk scores in a *table* — examiners award **2 marks** specifically for this matrix.
- ⭐ **Engineering controls outrank** administrative controls because they do not depend on human compliance — a frequent 1-mark KTU question.
- ⭐ **Cross-reference** with Module 2 (Risk Assessment) and Module 4 (Emergency Response) for a complete KTU answer — Module 3 controls are *interlinked* with these modules.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET722 - ENVIRONMENTAL HEALTH AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Electrical Hazards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ MODULE 3 — ELECTRICAL HAZARDS: CORE CONCEPTUAL FRAMEWORK

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electrical Hazard (KTU 2024 OECET722 Definition):**
> An *electrical hazard* is any unsafe condition, practice, or circumstance involving energized electrical equipment, conductors, or circuits that has the potential to cause injury, illness, fatality, or property damage. It encompasses the **dangerous release of electrical energy** through shock, arc flash, arc blast, thermal burns, fire, or explosion arising from the improper use, installation, or failure of electrical systems.

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, electrical hazards are classified as a subset of **physical hazards** under the broader *Occupational Health and Safety (OHS)* domain, governed in India by the **Central Electricity Authority (CEA) Regulations, 2010**, the **Factories Act 1948 (Section 36 – Safety Measures)**, the **Indian Electricity Rules 1956**, and harmonized with **IEC 60364** and **NFPA 70E** standards.

### Key Terminology (KTU Glossary)

| Term | Precise Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Voltage (V)** | Electric potential difference measured in **Volts (V)** |
| **Current (I)** | Rate of flow of charge measured in **Amperes (A)** |
| **Resistance (R)** | Opposition to current flow measured in **Ohms (Ω)** |
| **Impedance (Z)** | Total AC opposition (R + jX) measured in **Ω** |
| **Let-Go Threshold** | Maximum current a person can still release muscle grip; **~9 mA AC (men)**, **~6 mA AC (women)** |
| **Ventricular Fibrillation** | Disorganized heart rhythm leading to cardiac arrest, typically occurring above **~100 mA for >0.2 s** |
| **Arc Flash** | A violent release of energy through ionized air (plasma) caused by a fault, with temperatures up to **19,000 °C** |
| **Arc Blast** | The explosive pressure wave and shrapnel accompanying an arc flash |
| **Touch Potential** | Voltage between an energized object and the feet of a person in contact |
| **Step Potential** | Voltage between the feet of a person standing near an energized grounded point |

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Water-Pipe System":**
> Think of electricity as water flowing through a garden hose.
> - **Voltage** = Water pressure pushing the water
> - **Current** = Actual water flow (the dangerous part, NOT the pressure)
> - **Resistance** = The narrowness of the hose
>
> A high-pressure *thin jet* (high V, low I) can prick your skin, but a low-pressure *flood* (low V, high I) can sweep you off your feet. **It is the current (water) that harms you, not the voltage (pressure).** A 230 V household supply can kill you because your body, when wet, has low resistance, allowing a lethal current (>100 mA) to flow through your heart.

### The Three Pillars of Electrical Danger (KTU Mnemonic — "S-P-E")

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **S — P — E Framework:**
> 1. **S**ource of Energy (live conductor, capacitor, induced voltage)
> 2. **P**ath of Current (hand-to-hand, hand-to-foot, earthing loop)
> 3. **E**nvironment (wet, confined space, presence of flammable vapours)
>
> All three must coexist for a hazard to materialize. Eliminating any **one** pillar prevents injury.

---

## 1.3 Why Electrical Hazards Are Unique in EHS

Unlike mechanical or chemical hazards, electricity is **invisible, odorless, and instantaneous**. There is no warning sign before a shock. Furthermore, the severity of injury is governed by a **non-linear dose-response curve** — doubling the current does not double the harm; it can leap from "perception" to "fibrillation" within milliseconds.

### Physical Constants & Reference Metrics (Must Memorize)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Threshold Currents (AC 50 Hz, body path hand-to-hand):**
> - **1 mA** → Threshold of perception (tingling)
> - **5 mA** → Slight shock, no pain, no loss of muscular control
> - **9 mA / 6 mA** → Let-go threshold (men / women)
> - **16 mA** → Muscle tetany begins (cannot release grip)
> - **20–50 mA** → Painful shock, respiratory muscle paralysis possible
> - **100 mA** → **Ventricular fibrillation threshold** (duration > 0.2 s)
> - **> 200 mA** → Severe burns, cardiac arrest
> - **> 5 A** → Internal organ damage, deep tissue burning
>
> Standard body resistance assumed in IEC 60479: **Internal resistance = 500 Ω**, dry skin adds **≈ 100,000 Ω**, wet skin reduces skin resistance to **≈ 1,000 Ω**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU FORMULA CHEAT SHEET

## 2.1 Ohm's Law Applied to the Human Body

The human body, when in contact with two points at different potentials, behaves as a **non-linear voltage-dependent resistor** because skin resistance drops sharply with voltage (due to dielectric breakdown of the stratum corneum).

### Analytical Logic Chain (Step-by-Step Logic)

- **Step 1 — Model the body as a circuit element.** The body between two contact points is a series combination of: (a) skin resistance at the entry point $R_{s1}$, (b) internal tissue resistance $R_{int}$, and (c) skin resistance at the exit point $R_{s2}$.

- **Step 2 — Identify what is dangerous.** It is the **current through the heart region** that is lethal. The Heart Current Factor (F) quantifies this:
  - Hand-to-hand path: $F = 1.0$ (most dangerous)
  - Hand-to-foot path: $F = 1.0$ (also very dangerous)
  - Foot-to-foot path: $F = 0.4$ (less dangerous)

- **Step 3 — Apply Ohm's Law to the body circuit.** $I_{body} = V_{touch} / R_{body}$, then scale by F to get the effective heart current.

- **Step 4 — Compare with IEC 60479 time-current zones** to predict the physiological effect.

- **Step 5 — Implement engineering controls** (insulation, earthing, GFCI/RCCB) to reduce $I_{body}$ below the let-go threshold.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use this table as a one-stop revision resource for KTU 2024 ESE. All quantities are in **SI base units** unless otherwise specified.

| # | Formula | Description / Variable Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V = I \cdot R$ | Ohm's Law (DC) | $V$, $A$, $\Omega$ |
| 2 | $V = I \cdot Z$ | Ohm's Law (AC) where $Z = \sqrt{R^2 + X^2}$ | $V$, $A$, $\Omega$ |
| 3 | $I_{body} = \dfrac{V_{touch}}{R_{body}}$ | Current flowing through human body on contact | A |
| 4 | $R_{body} = R_{s,entry} + R_{int} + R_{s,exit}$ | Total body resistance | $\Omega$ |
| 5 | $I_{heart} = F \cdot I_{body}$ | Current through heart region (F = path factor) | A |
| 6 | $V_{touch} = V_{source} \cdot \dfrac{R_{body}}{R_{body} + Z_{source}}$ | Actual touch voltage when source has internal impedance | V |
| 7 | $P = I^2 \cdot R$ | Power dissipated in body (heat) causing burns | W |
| 8 | $E_{arc} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot C \cdot V^2$ | Arc flash incident energy (capacitive discharge approx.) | J |
| 9 | $IE = 4.184 \cdot C_f \cdot E_n \cdot (t/0.2) \cdot (610/D^x)$ | NFPA 70E Arc Flash Incident Energy Equation | cal/cm² |
| 10 | $I_{trip} \cdot t_{trip} \le 50 \text{ mA·s}$ | Typical RCD/ELCB trip characteristic (safety limit) | mA·s |
| 11 | $R_{earth} \le 10\ \Omega$ (CEA 1956 rule) | Maximum permissible earth-electrode resistance for installations | $\Omega$ |
| 12 | $\%Z_{transformer} = \dfrac{V_{sc}}{V_{rated}} \times 100$ | Transformer impedance (used in arc-flash calculation) | % |
| 13 | $I_{sc} = \dfrac{V_{LL}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot Z_{system}}$ | Available short-circuit current at fault point | kA |
| 14 | $E_{incident} = 1.5 \cdot V_{LL} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot t \cdot 10^{-3}$ | Simplified bolted-fault arc energy | kJ |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Electrical Power Industry:** Used to design **personal protective equipment (PPE)** arc-flash suits rated in **cal/cm²** (typically 4, 8, 12, 25, 40, 75 cal/cm² categories).
- **Building Services:** Used to size **RCCBs (Residual Current Circuit Breakers)** at **30 mA** for personal protection and **300 mA** for fire protection (per IS 12640).
- **Industrial Safety:** Used to determine **safe approach boundaries** to energized conductors (NFPA 70E defines *Limited Approach*, *Restricted Approach*, and *Prohibited Approach* distances).
- **First Responder Training:** Used to teach first-aiders when and how to safely disconnect a victim.

### Variables Glossary (Quick Reference)

| Symbol | Quantity | Symbol | Quantity |
|---|---|---|---|
| $V_{touch}$ | Touch potential (V) | $F$ | Heart-current factor (dimensionless) |
| $R_{body}$ | Total body resistance ($\Omega$) | $E_n$ | Normalized arc energy |
| $I_{heart}$ | Heart-region current (A) | $C_f$ | Calculation factor (1.0 for LV) |
| $Z_{source}$ | Source impedance ($\Omega$) | $D$ | Working distance (mm) |
| $I_{sc}$ | Short-circuit current (A) | $t$ | Arcing time (s) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:**
> The non-linear drop in skin resistance with rising voltage is *why* a 230 V shock feels less severe than a 415 V industrial shock, even though both are nominally "low voltage." Above **≈ 200 V**, the skin is dielectrically punctured, and current is limited only by internal body resistance (≈ 500 Ω). This is also why **Low Voltage (LV)** does **not** mean "safe voltage" in the IEC sense.

# 🧮 EXHAUSTIVE STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Worked Problem: Calculating Body Current Under Practical Conditions

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU-Style Numerical):**
> An electrician in Kerala is working on a **230 V, 50 Hz** single-phase AC supply. He accidentally touches a live conductor with his **right hand** (dry) and the neutral wire is being held with his **left hand** (wet with sweat).
> Given:
> - Dry skin resistance (right hand) $R_{s1} = 100{,}000\ \Omega$
> - Internal body resistance (chest) $R_{int} = 500\ \Omega$
> - Wet skin resistance (left hand) $R_{s2} = 1{,}000\ \Omega$
> - Source impedance $Z_s \approx 0\ \Omega$ (stiff grid)
> - Path factor hand-to-hand $F = 1.0$
>
> **Required:**
> 1. Calculate the body current $I_{body}$.
> 2. Calculate the heart-region current $I_{heart}$.
> 3. Identify the physiological effect from the IEC 60479 chart.
> 4. Determine the required body resistance to limit $I_{body}$ to the **let-go threshold of 9 mA**.

### 🔎 Full Exhaustive Derivation

**Step 1 — Formulate the equivalent electrical circuit.**

The body between the two hands forms a series circuit:

$$R_{body} = R_{s1} + R_{int} + R_{s2}$$

We substitute the given numerical values:

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{body} &= 100{,}000\ \Omega + 500\ \Omega + 1{,}000\ \Omega \\
&= 101{,}500\ \Omega
\end{aligned}$$

> **Conversion logic:** Resistances in series add directly. Skin resistances are the dominant terms; internal resistance is comparatively negligible in this dry-hand scenario.

---

**Step 2 — Apply Ohm's Law to find the current.**

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{body} &= \frac{V_{touch}}{R_{body}} \\
&= \frac{230\ \text{V}}{101{,}500\ \Omega} \\
&= 0.002266\ \text{A} \\
&\approx 2.27\ \text{mA}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Conversion logic:** Ohm's Law: current equals voltage divided by resistance. We divide volts by ohms to get amperes, then convert to milliamperes by multiplying by 1,000. The result (2.27 mA) is just above the perception threshold.

---

**Step 3 — Calculate the heart-region current.**

Because the path is hand-to-hand, the heart-current factor $F = 1.0$:

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{heart} &= F \cdot I_{body} \\
&= 1.0 \cdot 2.27\ \text{mA} \\
&= 2.27\ \text{mA}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Conversion logic:** Multiplying by 1.0 leaves the value unchanged. The current through the heart equals the body current in this path.

---

**Step 4 — Identify the physiological effect (IEC 60479).**

From the standard time-current zones for 50/60 Hz AC:

| Current Range | Physiological Effect | Applicable? |
|---|---|---|
| 0.1 – 1 mA | Threshold of perception (tingling) | No (we are above this) |
| 1 – 5 mA | Mild shock, no loss of control | **Yes — 2.27 mA falls here** |
| 5 – 9 mA | Painful but tolerable | No |
| 9 – 16 mA | Let-go threshold (9 mA men / 6 mA women) | No |
| 16 – 50 mA | Severe shock, muscle tetany | No |
| 50 – 100 mA | Possible ventricular fibrillation | No |
| > 100 mA | High probability of fibrillation, burns | No |

**Conclusion:** At 2.27 mA, the electrician would feel a **slight tingle** but not lose muscular control. The shock is **not lethal** under this dry-skin condition, but the situation is unsafe and warrants immediate disconnection.

---

**Step 5 — Calculate the required resistance to limit current to the let-go threshold (9 mA).**

We need the new body resistance $R_{body,\text{new}}$ such that:

$$I_{body} = 9\ \text{mA} = 0.009\ \text{A}$$

Rearranging Ohm's Law:

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{body,\text{new}} &= \frac{V_{touch}}{I_{body,\text{max}}} \\
&= \frac{230\ \text{V}}{0.009\ \text{A}} \\
&= 25{,}555.6\ \Omega \\
&\approx 25.6\ \text{k}\Omega
\end{aligned}$$

> **Conversion logic:** The body resistance must not fall below ~25.6 kΩ for the current to remain below the let-go threshold. The internal resistance (500 Ω) plus the smallest possible skin resistance (~25 kΩ) gives a working safety floor.

---

**Step 6 — Sensitivity analysis: what if both hands are wet?**

If both hands are wet, $R_{s1} = R_{s2} = 1{,}000\ \Omega$:

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{body,wet} &= 1{,}000 + 500 + 1{,}000 = 2{,}500\ \Omega \\
I_{body,wet} &= \frac{230}{2{,}500} = 0.092\ \text{A} = 92\ \text{mA} \\
I_{heart,wet} &= 1.0 \cdot 92\ \text{mA} = 92\ \text{mA}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Conversion logic:** With wet hands, the current jumps to **92 mA** — dangerously close to the **ventricular fibrillation threshold (100 mA)**. This is precisely why KTU emphasizes that **moisture is the silent killer** in electrical safety.

---

### 📊 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Condition | $R_{body}$ | $I_{body}$ | Physiological Zone | Lethal? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Both hands dry | 201,000 Ω | 1.14 mA | Barely perceptible | ❌ No |
| One hand dry, one wet (this problem) | 101,500 Ω | 2.27 mA | Mild shock | ❌ No |
| Both hands wet | 2,500 Ω | 92 mA | Pre-fibrillation | ⚠️ Borderline |
| Hands wet + skin puncture | 1,000 Ω | 230 mA | Severe burns, fibrillation | ☠️ Yes |
| Immersed in water | 500 Ω | 460 mA | Massive burns, cardiac arrest | ☠️ Yes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:**
> The examiner expects students to demonstrate that **it is the current, not the voltage, that kills**, and that **skin resistance acts as the body's natural fuse**. Always include the units, the assumption for F, and refer to IEC 60479 zones.

## 4.4 Lockout/Tagout (LOTO) Procedural Flow

The **LOTO procedure** is the gold standard for safe electrical work. KTU examiners frequently test this sequence.

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Step 1: Identify all energy sources and isolation points] --> S2[Step 2: Notify all affected workers of impending shutdown]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Shut down the equipment using normal stop procedure]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Isolate the energy source physically open isolator]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Apply Lockout device personal padlock with unique key]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Apply Tagout danger tag with name and date]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Release any stored energy capacitors hydraulic pneumatic]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Verify zero energy state using voltage tester or try start]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Perform the work task safely]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10: Re-energise in reverse order after removing all locks and tags]
    S10 --> Done[Safe Re-energisation]

    style S8 fill:#f9ca24,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style S5 fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style Done fill:#6ab04c,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

# 📚 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **Define the term "let-go threshold" in the context of electrical shock. State its typical value for AC 50 Hz current for adult males and adult females.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Let-go threshold** is the maximum magnitude of electric current flowing through a person's body at which the muscles of the hand and forearm are still voluntarily able to release a gripped conductor. Beyond this current, involuntary muscle contraction (tetanus) sets in, and the victim **cannot let go**, prolonging the exposure.
>
> **Typical Values (per IEC 60479):**
> - Adult males: **≈ 9 mA** (RMS, 50/60 Hz AC)
> - Adult females: **≈ 6 mA** (RMS, 50/60 Hz AC)
>
> The value depends on the frequency, the current path, and individual physiology. Above the let-go threshold, exposure for more than a few seconds can lead to respiratory muscle paralysis, exhaustion, and ultimately ventricular fibrillation.
>
> **Keyword index for 3 marks:** Definition (1 m) + Male value (1 m) + Female value (1 m).

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> **List any four common causes of electrical accidents in industrial workplaces. Briefly explain how each can be prevented.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Cause | Prevention |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Unsafe equipment** (damaged cords, broken plugs) | Use **IS-marked** equipment, periodic inspection, replace damaged cables |
| 2 | **Unsafe environment** (wet floor, cramped switch rooms) | Provide **IP-rated** enclosures, rubber matting, dry working platforms |
| 3 | **Unsafe work practice** (working on live equipment) | Always **isolate, lockout, test before touch (TBT)**, and use permit-to-work |
| 4 | **Lack of training / awareness** | Conduct regular **electrical safety induction**, PPE training, refresher courses |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024):** *Answer both Part (a) and Part (b) from the chosen question.*

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks)

> **Q A.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`
> **(a) [7 marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **defense-in-depth** concept in electrical safety. List the seven layers of protection in order.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** An industrial worker wearing damp gloves touches a **415 V, 3-phase AC** bus bar. Assuming body resistance hand-to-hand = **1,500 Ω** and heart-current factor F = 1.0, calculate:
> (i) The current through the body.
> (ii) The probable physiological effect using IEC 60479 zones.
> (iii) The arc-flash incident energy (in cal/cm²) at a working distance of **D = 610 mm** if the bolted-fault current is **I_sc = 25 kA** and the protective device operates in **t = 0.18 s**. (Use NFPA 70E simplified formula.)

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

The **defense-in-depth** principle states that no single safety measure is sufficient on its own; multiple independent layers of protection must be in place so that failure of one layer does not result in injury. This is mandated by **CEA Safety Regulations 2010** and harmonized with **NFPA 70E** and **IEC 60364**.

**The Seven Layers (from primary to last-resort):**

1. **Insulation** of live conductors (PVC, XLPE, EPR) — prevents direct contact.
2. **Earthing and Bonding** — provides a low-impedance fault path; limits touch voltage.
3. **Overcurrent Protection** (HRC fuse, MCB, MCCB) — clears short circuits within milliseconds.
4. **Earth-Leakage Protection** (RCCB/ELCB, 30 mA) — detects small leakage currents through the body.
5. **Double or Reinforced Insulation** (Class II equipment) — independent backup insulation.
6. **Safe Work Practices** (LOTO, Permit to Work, TBT) — administrative controls on human behavior.
7. **Personal Protective Equipment** (insulated gloves, arc-flash suit, face shield) — last line of defense.

*(Block diagram: see Section 4.3 of these notes — present the same flowchart in the answer book with arrows.)*

> **Mark split:** Diagram = 3 marks; Layer list = 3 marks; Brief explanation of 1 layer = 1 mark.

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Sub-part (i): Body current calculation**

$$I_{body} = \frac{V}{R_{body}} = \frac{415\ \text{V}}{1{,}500\ \Omega} = 0.2767\ \text{A} = 276.7\ \text{mA}$$

**Sub-part (ii): Physiological effect**

With $I_{body} = 276.7\ \text{mA}$ through a hand-to-hand path, this falls in the IEC 60479 zone for **> 200 mA** — **severe burns, ventricular fibrillation highly probable, cardiac arrest likely within seconds.** Outcome is **potentially fatal**.

**Sub-part (iii): Arc-flash incident energy**

Using the simplified NFPA 70E formula:

$$IE = 1.5 \cdot V_{LL} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot t \cdot 10^{-3}$$

Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned}
IE &= 1.5 \cdot 415\ \text{V} \cdot 25{,}000\ \text{A} \cdot 0.18\ \text{s} \cdot 10^{-3} \\
&= 1.5 \cdot 415 \cdot 25{,}000 \cdot 0.18 \cdot 10^{-3}\ \text{kJ} \\
&= 2{,}801.25\ \text{kJ}
\end{aligned}$$

This is an extremely high value, far exceeding the **40 cal/cm²** limit of standard arc-rated PPE. The actual *incident energy at the working surface* must be computed using the full empirical formula:

$$IE_{cal/cm^2} = 4.184 \cdot C_f \cdot E_n \cdot (t/0.2) \cdot (610/D^x)$$

For a quick estimate using $D = 610\ \text{mm}$ and $x = 1.473$ (typical for LV box electrodes), $C_f = 1.0$, $E_n \approx 0.005$ for 415 V:

$$IE \approx 4.184 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 0.005 \cdot (0.18/0.2) \cdot 1.0 \approx 0.0188\ \text{cal/cm}^2$$

This is in **PPE Category 0 (< 1.2 cal/cm²)** — low for the working distance, but the energy at the arc source itself is catastrophic. The worker should use **Category 4 PPE (75 cal/cm² rated suit)** at the fault point.

> **Mark split (b):** (i) 2 marks, (ii) 2 marks, (iii) 3 marks.

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

> **Q B.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Supplementary]`
> **(a) [7 marks]** What is **arc flash**? Explain the four major hazards associated with an arc-flash incident. State the **arc-flash protection boundary** distances for various PPE categories.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** With a neat flowchart, explain the **Lockout/Tagout (LOTO) procedure** step by step. Mention at least five safety devices used in electrical installations and their function.

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Arc flash** is a sustained electric arc fault occurring through air between two conductors (or conductor to ground) due to insulation failure, accidental contact, or contamination. The arc temperature can reach **≈ 19,000 °C** (hotter than the surface of the sun), vaporizing the conductors and producing an explosive release of energy.

**Four Major Hazards:**

1. **Thermal hazard** — third-degree burns from the arc's IR/UV radiation and copper vapor (skin can absorb energy at > 1.2 cal/cm² in less than a second).
2. **Pressure-wave / blast hazard** — up to **100 kPa** blast pressure can throw a worker several metres and rupture eardrums.
3. **Shrapnel / projectile hazard** — vaporized copper and broken insulation can become high-velocity projectiles.
4. **Toxic-fume hazard** — copper vapor, NOx, ozone, and decomposed insulating oil create inhalation hazards and possible lung damage.

**PPE Categories (NFPA 70E) and Boundaries:**

| PPE Category | Incident Energy (cal/cm²) | Limited Approach Distance |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 – 1.2 | Avoid contact |
| 1 | 1.2 – 4 | ~1.0 m |
| 2 | 4 – 8 | ~1.0 m |
| 3 | 8 – 25 | ~1.2 m |
| 4 | 25 – 40+ | ~1.5 m or greater |

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**LOTO Flowchart** (refer to Section 4.4 for the visual; describe verbally in exam):

1. Identify all energy sources and isolation points.
2. Notify all affected workers.
3. Shut down equipment via normal stop.
4. Isolate energy source (open isolator/MCB).
5. Apply personal lockout padlock.
6. Attach danger tag with name and date.
7. Discharge stored energy.
8. **Verify zero-energy** state (TBT — Test Before Touch).
9. Perform the work.
10. Remove locks in reverse after all workers sign off.

**Five Safety Devices:**

| Device | Function |
|---|---|
| **Fuse (HRC)** | Melts and breaks circuit on overcurrent |
| **MCB (Miniature Circuit Breaker)** | Trips on overload/short circuit; resettable |
| **MCCB (Moulded Case Circuit Breaker)** | Higher current rating MCB for industrial use |
| **RCCB / RCD** | Detects imbalance in live-neutral current (earth leakage); trips at 30 mA |
| **ELCB (Earth Leakage Circuit Breaker)** | Voltage-operated earth-fault protection |
| **Surge Protection Device (SPD)** | Clamps transient overvoltages (lightning/switching surges) |
| **Double-pole isolator** | Simultaneously disconnects line and neutral |

> **Mark split (b):** LOTO flowchart = 4 marks, Devices = 3 marks.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that cost marks in KTU 2024 ESE (Electrical Hazards):**
>
> 1. **Confusing current and voltage:** Writing "230 V is lethal" without explaining it is the *current* that kills and the role of body resistance. ❌ **-2 marks**
> 2. **Missing units:** Writing $I = 0.002$ without specifying **A or mA**. ❌ **-1 mark**
> 3. **Forgetting the Heart-Current Factor (F):** Many students compute $I_{body}$ and stop. Always ask "what is the current through the heart?" ❌ **-1 mark**
> 4. **Not citing IEC 60479 zones:** When asked for physiological effects, name the zone (e.g., "pre-fibrillation zone, 50–100 mA") not just "very dangerous." ❌ **-1 mark**
> 5. **LOTO in wrong order:** Applying the lock *before* isolating the source. The correct sequence is: Stop → Isolate → Lock → Tag → Verify. ❌ **-2 marks**
> 6. **Wrong earth resistance limit:** Writing "earth resistance should be 0" instead of the CEA value "**≤ 10 Ω**" (or 5 Ω for sensitive installations). ❌ **-1 mark**
> 7. **Confusing RCCB and MCB:** MCB protects the *wire* from overcurrent; RCCB protects the *person* from earth leakage. They are complementary, not alternatives. ❌ **-1 mark**
> 8. **No diagram or improperly drawn diagram:** Even for 7-mark questions, KTU awards **at least 1.5 to 2 marks** for a well-labelled block diagram. A blank space here is the most common reason for scoring 8/14 instead of 10/14.

---

## 5.4 📝 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Save this for the night before the exam):**
>
> ✅ **Electrical hazard = Source + Path + Environment** — eliminate any one to prevent injury.
>
> ✅ **Current kills, voltage is just the driver** — typical threshold currents: 1 mA (perception), 9/6 mA (let-go), 100 mA (fibrillation).
>
> ✅ **Ohm's Law for the body:** $I = V/R_{body}$; skin resistance drops with moisture and voltage.
>
> ✅ **Heart-Current Factor (F):** Hand-to-hand = Hand-to-foot = 1.0; Foot-to-foot = 0.4.
>
> ✅ **Body resistance:** Dry skin ≈ 100 kΩ; wet skin ≈ 1 kΩ; internal ≈ 500 Ω.
>
> ✅ **Defense-in-depth (7 layers):** Insulation → Earthing → Overcurrent → Earth leakage → Double insulation → Safe practices → PPE.
>
> ✅ **LOTO sequence:** Stop → Isolate → Lock → Tag → Discharge → Verify (TBT) → Work → De-isolate in reverse.
>
> ✅ **RCCB/ELCB trips at 30 mA** for personal protection, **300 mA** for fire protection (per IS 12640).
>
> ✅ **Earthing resistance ≤ 10 Ω** as per CEA Regulations 2010; soil resistivity drives electrode design.
>
> ✅ **Arc flash temperatures ≈ 19,000 °C**; use Category 1–4 PPE depending on incident energy in cal/cm².
>
> ✅ **Indian Standards to remember:** IS 732 (wiring), IS 12640 (RCCB), IS 3043 (earthing), IEC 60479 (body effects), NFPA 70E (arc flash).
>
> ✅ **First aid for shock:** DO NOT touch the victim if still in contact; first disconnect supply; then check ABC (Airway, Breathing, Circulation); start CPR if no pulse; call ambulance.
>
> ✅ **Diagram must include:** title, labels with units, arrows showing flow/direction, and a key/legend if more than one symbol is used.
>
> ✅ **Numericals:** Always show the substitution step with units, then a final boxed answer with the correct unit (V, mA, kA, cal/cm², Ω).
>
> ✅ **Mnemonic to remember the 7 layers:** **"I Eat Onions, Really Delicious, Served Plain"** = Insulation, Earthing, Overcurrent, RCCB, Double insulation, Safe practices, PPE.

---

*End of Module 3 — Electrical Hazards (KTU 2024 Scheme, OECET722)*
*Prepared per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 Specification, NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education framework.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Protection Against Voltage Fluctuations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET722 - ENVIRONMENTAL HEALTH AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Protection Against Voltage Fluctuations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Voltage Fluctuation** is defined as any unwanted, rapid deviation of the RMS (Root Mean Square) supply voltage from its nominal rated value, occurring over a short duration ranging from a few microseconds (transients) to several seconds (sustained events). In the context of **Environmental Health and Safety (EHS)**, voltage fluctuations are classified as **Electrical Power Quality Hazards** because they directly threaten equipment integrity, occupational safety, fire prevention, and the safe operation of life-critical medical and industrial control systems.

The primary classifications recognized by **IEEE Std 1159-2019** and **IEC 61000-2-2** are:

- **Surge (Impulsive Transient):** A sudden, brief increase in voltage lasting less than 50 ms, typically caused by lightning or switching events.
- **Sag (Dip / Brownout):** A reduction in RMS voltage to between **0.1 pu and 0.9 pu** for a duration of 0.5 cycles to 1 minute.
- **Swell:** An increase in RMS voltage to between **1.1 pu and 1.8 pu** for a duration of 0.5 cycles to 1 minute.
- **Spike:** A very fast, high-energy transient lasting microseconds to milliseconds.
- **Sustained Overvoltage/Undervoltage:** Deviations lasting more than 1 minute.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Per-Unit (pu) System Context:** 1 pu = the nominal system voltage. For a 230 V single-phase residential line, 1 pu = **230 V**, while 0.9 pu = 207 V and 1.1 pu = 253 V.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the electrical supply to your house as **water flowing through a pipe** at a steady, constant pressure (let's say 2 bar).

- **Voltage Sag (Brownout):** The municipal pump momentarily weakens, so the pressure drops to 1.5 bar. Your washing machine's drum slows down, and the shower becomes a trickle.
- **Voltage Swell:** A pressure surge pushes water at 3 bar. The delicate faucet cartridge may leak or burst.
- **Spike (Transient Surge):** A worker suddenly slams a valve shut somewhere down the line, sending a sharp "water hammer" shockwave through your pipes. This water hammer can rupture old pipes instantly.
- **Sustained Overvoltage:** The municipal pump is stuck on "high" for hours, slowly fatiguing every joint in your plumbing.

In this analogy, **Protection Against Voltage Fluctuations** is equivalent to installing a **Pressure Reducing Valve (PRV)**, a **water hammer arrestor**, and **expansion tanks** — all of which absorb shocks and keep pressure stable. Electrical protection devices (SPDs, AVRs, UPS) serve exactly the same purpose for current and voltage.

> [!NOTE]
> **EHS Relevance:** Voltage fluctuations are not just a nuisance. They are direct causes of:
> - Electrical fires (overheating due to sustained overcurrent from undervoltage)
> - Data loss in hospital life-support systems
> - Insulation breakdown leading to shock hazards
> - Premature equipment failure generating e-waste (an environmental concern)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Categories of Voltage Fluctuations — Structural Breakdown

Voltage fluctuations are categorized by **magnitude**, **duration**, and **spectral content**:

1. **Impulsive Transients (Spikes)**
   - Duration: 5 ns to 50 µs
   - Rise time: < 1 µs
   - Primary cause: Lightning electromagnetic coupling, inductive load switching
   - EHS impact: Insulation puncture, fire ignition, PCB damage

2. **Oscillatory Transients**
   - Duration: 0.3 ms to 5 ms
   - Frequency: 5 kHz to 500 kHz
   - Primary cause: Capacitor bank switching, ferroresonance
   - EHS impact: Malfunction of protective relays

3. **Voltage Sags and Swells**
   - Duration: 0.5 cycles to 1 minute
   - Primary cause: Motor starting, large load switching, faults
   - EHS impact: Contactors drop out, PLC resets, motors stall

4. **Sustained Overvoltage/Undervoltage**
   - Duration: > 1 minute
   - Primary cause: Poor load regulation, transformer tap mis-setting
   - EHS impact: Cumulative insulation degradation, overheating

### 2.2 Why and How Protection Works

The **"Why"** behind voltage protection is rooted in three physical limits of any electrical system:
- **Insulation Dielectric Strength** (kV/mm) — exceeded by spikes
- **Thermal Limit of Conductors** (I²R rating) — exceeded by sustained overcurrent from undervoltage-driven high current
- **Logic-Level Thresholds** of semiconductors (5 V, 3.3 V) — corrupted by sags

The **"How"** is achieved by shunting, absorbing, or isolating the disturbance using dedicated devices.

### 2.3 Protection Devices — Operational Principles

- **Surge Protective Device (SPD):** Uses Metal Oxide Varistors (MOVs) or Gas Discharge Tubes (GDTs) to clamp voltage to a safe level by shunting excess current to ground.
- **Automatic Voltage Regulator (AVR):** Continuously samples the input voltage using a buck-boost transformer to maintain output within ±1% of nominal.
- **Uninterruptible Power Supply (UPS):** Stores energy in batteries and provides a clean sine wave via an inverter, isolating the load from grid disturbances.
- **Isolation Transformer:** Provides galvanic isolation, attenuating common-mode noise.
- **Snubber Circuits (RC networks):** Suppress inductive switching transients.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Equation | Meaning | Variables & Units | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| $V_{pu} = \dfrac{V_{actual}}{V_{nominal}}$ | Per-Unit Voltage | $V_{pu}$ = pu voltage, $V_{actual}$ = measured V, $V_{nominal}$ = rated V | Quantifying sag/swell magnitude |
| $E_{surge} = \dfrac{1}{2} C V^2$ | Energy stored in surge capacitor | $E$ = J, $C$ = F, $V$ = V | SPD energy rating |
| $V_{clamp} = V_{ref} \cdot (1 + \alpha \Delta T)$ | MOV Clamping voltage (temperature dependent) | $V_{clamp}$ = V, $V_{ref}$ = reference V, $\alpha$ = temp coeff, $\Delta T$ = K | SPD selection |
| $\tau = R \cdot C$ | RC Snubber time constant | $\tau$ = s, $R$ = Ω, $C$ = F | Transient suppression |
| $f_{res} = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi \sqrt{L C}}$ | LC filter resonance frequency | $f_{res}$ = Hz, $L$ = H, $C$ = F | EMI filter design |
| $V_{drop} = I \cdot Z_{line}$ | Line drop causing sag | $V_{drop}$ = V, $I$ = A, $Z_{line}$ = Ω | Sag analysis |
| $P_{loss} = I^2 R$ | Ohmic heating during undervoltage event | $P_{loss}$ = W, $I$ = A, $R$ = Ω | Fire risk assessment |
| $V_{Let-Through} = V_{clamp} + I_{surge} \cdot Z_{dynamic}$ | Effective voltage reaching load | $V_{Let-Through}$ = V | SPD performance |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Surge Energy Dissipated by an MOV (Metal Oxide Varistor)

An MOV is the heart of a modern SPD. Let us derive the energy it must absorb during a single lightning-induced surge.

**Step 1 — Define the surge waveform.**
The standard test waveform is the **8/20 µs combination wave**, meaning it reaches 90% of peak in 8 µs and decays to 50% by 20 µs. The equivalent double-exponential current is:

$$I(t) = I_{peak} \cdot e^{-\alpha t} - I_{peak} \cdot e^{-\beta t}$$

Where $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are empirically determined decay constants (typically $\alpha \approx 1.0 \times 10^4$ /s and $\beta \approx 5.0 \times 10^5$ /s for an 8/20 µs wave).

**Step 2 — Express the clamping voltage.**
The MOV clamps voltage approximately constant at $V_{clamp}$ for the surge duration $\Delta t$.

**Step 3 — Write the energy integral.**
Energy dissipated is the integral of instantaneous power $V_{clamp} \cdot I(t)$ over the surge duration:

$$E_{MOV} = \int_{0}^{\Delta t} V_{clamp} \cdot I(t) \, dt$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and integrate.**
Substituting the current expression:

$$E_{MOV} = V_{clamp} \cdot I_{peak} \int_{0}^{\Delta t} \left( e^{-\alpha t} - e^{-\beta t} \right) dt$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the integral.**
Using the standard integral $\int e^{-kt} dt = -\dfrac{1}{k} e^{-kt}$:

$$E_{MOV} = V_{clamp} \cdot I_{peak} \left[ \frac{1 - e^{-\alpha \Delta t}}{\alpha} - \frac{1 - e^{-\beta \Delta t}}{\beta} \right]$$

**Step 6 — Apply the engineering approximation.**
For an 8/20 µs wave, $\alpha \Delta t \ll 1$ and $\beta \Delta t \gg 1$ in the early surge window, so $e^{-\alpha \Delta t} \approx 1 - \alpha \Delta t$ and $e^{-\beta \Delta t} \approx 0$:

$$E_{MOV} \approx V_{clamp} \cdot I_{peak} \cdot \Delta t_{effective}$$

**Step 7 — Numerical example.**
A Type 2 SPD rated for a $10/350 \mu s$ test waveform, $I_{peak} = 20 \text{ kA}$, $V_{clamp} = 1500 \text{ V}$, $\Delta t_{effective} = 350 \mu s$:

$$E_{MOV} = 1500 \times 20{,}000 \times 350 \times 10^{-6} = 10{,}500 \text{ J} = 10.5 \text{ kJ}$$

This is the **single-impulse withstanding energy**, a critical EHS specification for SPDs protecting critical infrastructure (hospitals, data centers).

### 3.2 Derivation: RC Snubber Time Constant for Inductive Load

**Step 1 — State the problem.** When an inductive load (solenoid, motor winding) is switched off, the collapsing magnetic field generates a high-voltage spike: $V = -L \dfrac{di}{dt}$.

**Step 2 — Place an RC snubber in parallel with the switch contacts.**

**Step 3 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law.**
Loop equation at the moment of switch opening:

$$L \frac{di}{dt} + R i + \frac{1}{C} \int i \, dt = 0$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate to obtain a second-order ODE.**

$$L \frac{d^2 i}{dt^2} + R \frac{di}{dt} + \frac{1}{C} i = 0$$

**Step 5 — Solve the characteristic equation.**

$$s^2 + \frac{R}{L} s + \frac{1}{LC} = 0$$

**Step 6 — Critical damping condition.**
To prevent oscillation (which itself creates transients), we require critical damping:

$$R = 2 \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}$$

**Step 7 — Resulting time constant.**
At critical damping, the decay time constant is:

$$\tau_{crit} = \frac{2L}{R} = \sqrt{LC}$$

This value is the optimal RC product for suppressing the inductive kick without creating a new resonant frequency.

### 3.3 Worked Example: Voltage Sag Risk to a Motor

**Given:** A 3-phase, 400 V, 50 Hz induction motor draws 50 A rated current at 0.8 pf. A voltage sag to 0.7 pu (280 V) occurs for 200 ms.

**Find:** (a) The new current draw, assuming constant mechanical load. (b) The risk of insulation overheating.

**Step 1 — Current under sag.**
Motor torque $T \propto V^2$. To maintain constant load torque, the motor draws more current:

$$I_{sag} \approx I_{rated} \cdot \frac{V_{rated}}{V_{sag}} = 50 \cdot \frac{1}{0.7} = 71.4 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2 — Ohmic loss comparison.**

$$P_{sag} = I_{sag}^2 R = (71.4)^2 R = 5100 R \text{ W}$$

$$P_{rated} = I_{rated}^2 R = (50)^2 R = 2500 R \text{ W}$$

**Step 3 — Heating ratio.**

$$\frac{P_{sag}}{P_{rated}} = \left( \frac{71.4}{50} \right)^2 = 2.04$$

The motor windings heat at **2× the rated rate** during the sag, raising fire risk if the sag persists beyond thermal time constant.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Hierarchical Protection Architecture (Mermaid)

The following diagram illustrates the **zonal defense concept** used in EHS-grade electrical installations (per IEC 61643-12).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Utility Grid Input 11kV / 230V] --> B[Step Down Transformer]
    B --> C[Zone 0 Type 1 SPD Lightning Current Arrester]
    C --> D[Main Distribution Board MDB]
    D --> E[Zone 1 Type 2 SPD Surge Limiter]
    E --> F[Sub Distribution Board SDB]
    F --> G[Zone 2 Type 3 SPD Fine Protection]
    G --> H[Critical Load UPS or AVR]
    H --> I[End Equipment Hospital ICU PC Lab]
    
    J[Grounding Earth Electrode 5 ohm] -.-> C
    J -.-> E
    J -.-> G
    J -.-> H

    K[Monitoring Module SCADA] -.-> C
    K -.-> E
    K -.-> G
    K -.-> H
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**

- **Type 1 SPD** handles the bulk of lightning current (10/350 µs waveform) at the service entrance.
- **Type 2 SPD** clamps the let-through voltage to below 1500 V.
- **Type 3 SPD** provides fine protection (< 6 V let-through) for sensitive electronics.
- The **grounding system** (dotted lines) provides a low-impedance path for fault currents, critical for EHS.
- **SCADA monitoring** allows predictive maintenance and post-event analysis.

### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Automatic Voltage Regulator (AVR) Operation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input AC Supply] --> B[Voltage Sampling Circuit]
    B --> C{Comparator Reference 230V}
    C -->|Below Setpoint| D[Buck Boost Transformer Tap Up]
    C -->|Above Setpoint| E[Buck Boost Transformer Tap Down]
    D --> F[Output Stabilized 230V]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Load]
    F --> H[Feedback Loop to B]
```

### 4.3 Sequence Diagram — Surge Protection Coordination

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Grid as Power Grid
    participant SPD1 as Type 1 SPD
    participant SPD2 as Type 2 SPD
    participant SPD3 as Type 3 SPD
    participant Load as Critical Load
    Grid->>SPD1: Lightning surge 20kA arrives
    SPD1->>SPD1: Diverts 90 percent to ground
    SPD1->>SPD2: Passes 2kA residual
    SPD2->>SPD2: Clamps voltage to 1500V
    SPD2->>SPD3: Passes 1000V residual
    SPD3->>SPD3: Clamps to 6V at terminals
    SPD3->>Load: Clean power delivered
    Note over Load: Equipment protected
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

Define the term **voltage sag** and **voltage swell** as per IEEE 1159. State two causes of each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Voltage Sag (Dip):** A reduction in RMS voltage to between 0.1 pu and 0.9 pu of nominal, lasting from 0.5 cycles to 1 minute. **Causes:** (i) Motor starting currents, (ii) Single-line-to-ground faults. **(1.5 marks)**
- **Voltage Swell:** An increase in RMS voltage to between 1.1 pu and 1.8 pu of nominal, lasting 0.5 cycles to 1 minute. **Causes:** (i) Sudden load rejection, (ii) Energizing a large capacitor bank. **(1.5 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

What is a **Metal Oxide Varistor (MOV)**? How does it protect against voltage surges?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

An MOV is a non-linear resistor made of sintered zinc oxide grains whose resistance drops dramatically (from MΩ to mΩ range) when the applied voltage exceeds its **clamping voltage (Vc)**. When a surge arrives, the MOV transitions from a high-impedance insulator to a low-impedance conductor within nanoseconds, shunting the excess surge current to ground and clamping the line voltage to a safe level for downstream equipment. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **different categories of voltage fluctuations** recognized in power quality standards. Classify them by magnitude, duration, and typical spectral content. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A hospital ICU has a 230 V single-phase supply. A Type 2 SPD is installed with the following ratings:
- Nominal voltage $U_n$ = 230 V
- Maximum continuous operating voltage $U_c$ = 275 V
- Voltage protection level $U_p$ = 1500 V
- Nominal discharge current $I_n$ = 20 kA (8/20 µs)
- Let-through voltage at 5 kA = 900 V

Calculate the **per-unit voltage at the SPD terminal** during a 5 kA surge event and comment on the safety of downstream medical equipment withstanding voltage of 2500 V. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Categorization (7 Marks):**

| Category | Magnitude | Duration | Spectral Content | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Impulsive Transient | 1.0–6.0 pu | 5 ns–50 µs | MHz range | Lightning |
| Oscillatory Transient | 0–4.0 pu | 0.3–5 ms | 5 kHz–500 kHz | Capacitor switching |
| Sag | 0.1–0.9 pu | 0.5 cyc – 1 min | 50/60 Hz | Motor start |
| Swell | 1.1–1.8 pu | 0.5 cyc – 1 min | 50/60 Hz | Load rejection |
| Sustained Overvoltage | > 1.1 pu | > 1 min | 50/60 Hz | Tap mis-set |
| Sustained Undervoltage | < 0.9 pu | > 1 min | 50/60 Hz | Overloaded feeder |

Each category needs separate protection strategy. Lightning transients need Type 1 SPDs; sags need AVR/UPS; sustained events need regulator tap changers.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify the actual let-through voltage.**
At 5 kA surge current, the SPD clamps the voltage to 900 V (given).

**Step 2 — Convert to per-unit.**

$$V_{pu} = \frac{V_{actual}}{V_{nominal}} = \frac{900 \text{ V}}{230 \text{ V}} = 3.91 \text{ pu}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with equipment rating.**
The medical equipment has a **withstanding voltage of 2500 V** (insulation coordination per IEC 60664-1). Since 900 V < 2500 V, the equipment is **safe** under this 5 kA surge event.

**Step 4 — Safety Margin Calculation.**

$$\text{Margin} = \frac{2500 - 900}{2500} \times 100\% = 64\%$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion.**
A **64% safety margin** is excellent. The SPD rating is appropriate for the hospital ICU application. If a Type 3 SPD (6 V let-through) is added at the equipment terminal, the effective margin becomes near 100%, providing redundant protection as per IEC 61643-12 zoning.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, describe the **zonal concept of surge protection** in a large industrial installation. Explain the role of Type 1, Type 2, and Type 3 SPDs. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **critical damping condition for an RC snubber** used to protect switching contacts from inductive voltage spikes. A relay coil has $L = 100 \text{ mH}$ and internal resistance $R_{coil} = 50 \Omega$. Calculate the **snubber capacitor value** required for critical damping. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Zonal Concept (7 Marks):**
The zonal concept (per IEC 61643-12) divides an installation into **Lightning Protection Zones (LPZ)**:
- **LPZ 0A:** External area exposed to direct lightning. No equipment here.
- **LPZ 0B:** External area protected by air terminals. Type 1 SPD needed at boundary.
- **LPZ 1:** Indoor main distribution. Type 1 or Type 2 SPD at service entrance.
- **LPZ 2:** Sub-distribution. Type 2 SPD at sub-panel.
- **LPZ 3:** Equipment terminal. Type 3 SPD at socket/outlet.

**Type 1 SPD:** Tested with 10/350 µs waveform (simulating direct lightning). Installed at service entrance, handles high I²t energy.

**Type 2 SPD:** Tested with 8/20 µs waveform. Installed at sub-distribution boards. Limits let-through voltage to < 1500 V.

**Type 3 SPD:** Tested with 1.2/50 µs voltage and 8/20 µs current combination. Provides fine protection (< 6 V let-through) for sensitive electronics like PCs and PLCs.

The **coordination** between zones ensures each stage progressively reduces the surge magnitude, preventing any single device from being overstressed.

**(b) RC Snubber Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Recall the critical damping condition** derived in Section 3.2:

$$R = 2 \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for C:**

$$C = \frac{4L}{R^2}$$

**Step 3 — Identify circuit resistance.**
For critical damping, the snubber resistor $R_s$ must combine with the coil's internal resistance. Often $R_s$ is chosen to be **equal to $R_{coil}$** for optimal energy dissipation in the snubber (i.e., the snubber resistance equals the line impedance to maximize absorption):

$$R_{total} = 2 R_{coil} = 2 \times 50 = 100 \text{ Ω}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate C.**

$$C = \frac{4 \times 100 \times 10^{-3}}{(100)^2} = \frac{0.4}{10000} = 4 \times 10^{-5} \text{ F} = 40 \text{ µF}$$

**Step 5 — Verify time constant.**

$$\tau = R \cdot C = 100 \times 40 \times 10^{-6} = 4 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion.**
A **40 µF capacitor** in series with a **100 Ω resistor** (or 50 Ω resistor + 50 Ω coil resistance) provides critical damping, eliminating the inductive spike that would otherwise exceed the relay contact's 1500 V withstand capability.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently **forget to convert to per-unit** when the question asks for pu analysis — this loses 1–2 marks.
> - In surge energy derivations, **do not use the simple $E = \frac{1}{2}CV^2$ alone** for 8/20 µs waves; the integration approach is required for full marks.
> - In snubber problems, students often **omit the coil's internal resistance** in the damping condition, leading to an incorrect C value. Always list every resistance element in the loop.
> - For zonal protection diagrams, examiners expect **all three SPD types** to be clearly labeled with their waveform ratings (10/350, 8/20, 1.2/50). Missing these is a 2-mark deduction.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Voltage fluctuation** is any deviation of RMS voltage from nominal, classified by IEEE 1159 into sags, swells, transients, and sustained events.
- **Sag** = 0.1–0.9 pu (0.5 cyc – 1 min); **Swell** = 1.1–1.8 pu (0.5 cyc – 1 min).
- **Per-unit system:** $V_{pu} = V_{actual} / V_{nominal}$ is the standard for power quality quantification.
- **MOVs** are the workhorse of SPDs, clamping voltage via non-linear zinc oxide grain boundaries.
- **Surge energy formula:** $E = V_{clamp} \cdot I_{peak} \cdot \Delta t_{effective}$ for impulsive events.
- **Snubber critical damping:** $R = 2\sqrt{L/C}$, giving optimal transient suppression without resonance.
- **Zonal protection:** Type 1 (10/350 µs) → Type 2 (8/20 µs) → Type 3 (1.2/50 µs + 8/20 µs combination).
- **EHS linkage:** Voltage fluctuations cause fires, equipment failure, and e-waste; protection is both a safety and environmental mandate.
- **Standards to memorize:** IEEE 1159, IEC 61000-2-2, IEC 61643-12, IEC 60664-1.
- **Common exam trap:** Confusing sag with undervoltage (duration is the discriminator: < 1 min = sag, > 1 min = undervoltage).
- **Motor rule under sag:** Current rises as $I \propto 1/V$, leading to **2× heating** at 0.7 pu sag.
- **AVR** maintains output within ±1%; **UPS** provides full isolation and ride-through.
- **Grounding** at < 5 Ω is essential for effective SPD operation — never install an SPD without verifying earth resistance.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effects of Shock on Human Body
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET722 - ENVIRONMENTAL HEALTH AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Effects of Shock on Human Body

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electric Shock (Physiological Shock):** An abnormal physiological response of the human body to the passage of an electric current of sufficient magnitude to cause involuntary muscular contraction, sensory perception changes, ventricular fibrillation, respiratory paralysis, thermal burns, or death.

According to the **IEC 60479** standard and the KTU 2024 EHS curriculum, the severity of an electric shock is governed by the **magnitude of current** (not voltage alone), the **path of current** through the body, the **duration of exposure**, and the **frequency** of the supply.

**Ohm's Law Applied to the Human Body:**

$$I_{body} = \frac{V_{source}}{Z_{body}}$$

where:
- $I_{body}$ = current flowing through the body (in **Amperes / mA**)
- $V_{source}$ = contact voltage (in **Volts**)
- $Z_{body}$ = total body impedance (in **Ohms**) — a complex combination of skin resistance, internal resistance, and capacitive reactance.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Water-Pipe" Model of the Body**
> Imagine the human body as a complex network of **water pipes filled with salt water (electrolytes)**. The pipes are the nerves and blood vessels, the salt water is the ionic fluid, and any external voltage is like a water pump forcing flow.
> - A **small trickle** (≈ 1 mA) is like a slight vibration in the pipes — you *feel* it (perception).
> - A **moderate flow** (≈ 10–20 mA) is like a strong surge that **freezes the valve** — your hand cannot let go ("let-go" threshold).
> - A **high-pressure jet** (≈ 100 mA) tears through the heart valves chaotically — **ventricular fibrillation**.
> - A **fire-hose blast** (> 5 A) literally boils the internal fluid — **severe burns and tissue destruction**.

The crucial insight: **Voltage drives the current, but CURRENT causes the damage.** A 50,000 V static discharge can be harmless if the current is microscopic; yet a 50 V battery can be lethal if the skin is wet and current finds a path across the heart.

---

### 1.3 Standard Body Impedance Values (IEC 60479-1 Reference)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Body Impedance Benchmarks (50/60 Hz, Hand-to-Hand path):**
> - **Dry skin, dry conditions:** 100,000 Ω to 1,000,000 Ω (100 kΩ – 1 MΩ)
> - **Wet skin, wet conditions:** 1,000 Ω (1 kΩ) — can drop further
> - **Internal body resistance (excluding skin):** ≈ 300 Ω (essentially resistive, with small capacitive component)
> - **Total hand-to-foot / hand-to-hand:** ≈ 1,000 Ω to 2,000 Ω (typical wet assumption for safety calculations)

The standard **"let-go" threshold** used worldwide is **30 mA** (males, 50/60 Hz) — this is the current at which the muscles controlling the hand freeze and the victim cannot release the conductor.

---

### 1.4 Why This Matters in EHS

> [!NOTE]
> Every safety standard in industry — earth leakage circuit breakers (**ELCB**), residual current devices (**RCD/RCBO** at 30 mA / 6 mA), equipotential bonding, double insulation — is engineered around the physiological thresholds of the human body. Understanding **why 30 mA is the magic number** is the foundation of electrical safety design.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Physics of an Electric Shock

When a person contacts a live conductor, the following sequence occurs:

1. **Voltage Application:** A potential difference $V$ is established between the contact point and the ground (or another contact point).

2. **Current Path Formation:** The current seeks the path of **least impedance** through the body — typically hand-to-hand, hand-to-foot, or head-to-foot.

3. **Skin Resistance Breakdown:** Initial skin resistance ($R_{skin}$) is high, but it is **non-linear and voltage-dependent**. As voltage increases, the skin's outer stratum corneum breaks down:
   $$R_{skin}(V) \approx \frac{R_0}{\sqrt{V}}$$
   This is why **high voltage is exponentially more dangerous** — it punches through skin resistance.

4. **Internal Current Distribution:** Once inside, current flows through blood (which is a good conductor due to electrolytes) and muscles. Internal resistance $R_{internal} \approx 300\ \Omega$ is relatively constant.

5. **Frequency-Specific Effects:** At 50/60 Hz, current is at its **most dangerous** for ventricular fibrillation. DC tends to cause sustained muscular contraction ("push-pull" effect), and high-frequency (> 100 kHz) tends to cause surface heating only.

6. **Tissue Heating:** Power dissipated in the body:
   $$P = I^2 R_{body}$$
   This is what causes **electrical burns** (Joule heating).

---

### 2.2 The Physiological Threshold Ladder (50/60 Hz AC)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following thresholds are taken from **IEC 60479-1 / IEEE C2 (National Electrical Code)**, which form the basis of KTU 2024 syllabus content.

| Level | Current (mA) | Physiological Effect | Sensory / Motor Response |
|:---|:---:|:---|:---|
| 0 | < 0.001 | No sensation | Imperceptible |
| 1 | 0.001 – 1 | **Threshold of perception** | Slight tingling |
| 2 | 1 – 5 | **Perceptible but no pain** | Mild shock, no loss of control |
| 3 | 5 – 15 | **Painful shock, muscular control retained** | Can still let go |
| 4 | 15 – 30 (men) / 6 – 15 (women) | **Let-go threshold boundary** | Painful, may struggle to release |
| 5 | 30 – 50 | **Severe shock, muscle freeze ("let-go" exceeded)** | Cannot release conductor |
| 6 | 50 – 100 | **Respiratory muscle paralysis** | Breathing stops, may cause unconsciousness |
| 7 | 100 – 200 (> 0.5 s) | **Ventricular fibrillation begins** | Heart goes into chaotic rhythm → death within minutes |
| 8 | > 200 (> 0.5 s) | **Severe burns, severe Fibrillation** | High mortality |
| 9 | > 5,000 (5 A) | **Cardiac arrest, severe internal burns** | Tissue destruction, often fatal |

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Equations)

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these equations for any numerical problem in the KTU ESE.**

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | $I_{body} = \dfrac{V_{contact}}{R_{body}}$ | Ohm's Law for the body | A |
| 2 | $R_{body} = R_{skin,\,in} + R_{internal} + R_{skin,\,out}$ | Total body resistance model | Ω |
| 3 | $R_{internal} \approx 300\ \Omega$ | Internal body resistance (constant) | Ω |
| 4 | $P = I_{body}^{2} \cdot R_{body}$ | Power dissipated as heat (burns) | W |
| 5 | $E_{thermal} = I^{2} \cdot R \cdot t$ | Energy delivered to tissue (Joule's Law) | J |
| 6 | $V_{fibrillation} \approx I_{fib} \cdot R_{body} = 0.1 \cdot R_{body}$ | Threshold voltage for fibrillation | V |
| 7 | $t_{safe} \le \dfrac{k}{I^{2}}$ (k ≈ 116 for hand-to-foot, IEC) | Maximum safe exposure time | s |
| 8 | $Z = \sqrt{R^{2} + X_{C}^{2}}$ | Body impedance (capacitive at HF) | Ω |
| 9 | $R_{skin} \approx \dfrac{R_{0}}{\sqrt{V}}$ | Voltage-dependent skin resistance | Ω |
| 10 | $I_{let\text{-}go} \approx 10\ mA$ (men), $6\ mA$ (women) | Let-go current (50/60 Hz) | mA |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Threshold Memorization Tip:** The "magic numbers" you MUST remember for KTU exams are **1 mA** (perception), **10 mA** (let-go), **30 mA** (muscle freeze / RCD trip), **100 mA** (fibrillation), and **5 A** (burns).

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In practical safety engineering, these equations drive:
- **Selection of ELCB/RCD ratings** (30 mA for personal protection, 300 mA for fire protection).
- **Touch voltage limits** in IEC 60364: ≤ **50 V AC** in dry conditions, ≤ **25 V AC** in wet/outdoor conditions (this 50 V assumes a body resistance of ~2 kΩ wet and a let-go current of ~25 mA, leaving safety margin).
- **Arc Flash calculations** for PPE category selection in substations.
- **Medical equipment leakage current standards** (IEC 60601-1: patient auxiliary current ≤ 10 µA, patient leakage ≤ 100 µA for cardiac applications).
