[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **"Define the 3R principle in waste management and explain its relevance to an individual."** **Model Answer:** The **3R principle** stands for **Reduce, Reuse, Recycle**. It is a hierarchical waste management strategy that prioritizes the prevention and minimization of waste generation at the individual level. - **Reduce:** Using fewer resources and generating less waste in the first place (e.g., avoiding packaged products). - **Reuse:** Extending the life of an item by using it again for the same or different purpose (e.g., cloth bags, refillable bottles). - **Recycle:** Processing waste materials into new products to prevent the consumption of fresh raw materials (e.g., recycling paper, plastic, metal). **Relevance to an Individual:** A common KTU student typically generates **0.5 to 1.5 kg of waste per day**. By applying the 3R principle, the individual directly reduces landfill burden, conserves natural resources, lowers their carbon footprint, and contributes to India's commitments under the **Solid Waste Management Rules, 2016** and **Swachh Bharat Mission 2.0**. > [!NOTE] > **Valuation Tip:** Award full 3 marks only if the student states all three R's AND gives at least one individual-level example. Definitions alone fetch only 2 marks. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]** **"What is an ecological footprint? Why is it important for individuals?"** **Model Answer:** An **ecological footprint (EF)** is a quantitative metric that measures the amount of biologically productive land and water area an individual (or population) requires to produce all the resources they consume and to absorb all the waste they generate, using prevailing technology and resource management practices. The unit is **global hectares (gha)** per person per year. **Importance for Individuals:** 1. **Self-Awareness:** Quantifies an individual's environmental impact, making the abstract concept of "pollution" measurable. 2. **Decision-Making:** Helps individuals make informed choices (e.g., diet, transport, energy). 3. **Planetary Boundaries:** The current global EF is **2.75 gha**, but Earth's biocapacity is only **1.6 gha/person**, indicating an **overshoot of ~70%**. 4. **Sustainability Tracking:** Allows individuals to set reduction targets aligned with IPCC 1.5°C pathways. > [!NOTE] > **Valuation Tip:** Memorize the global average (**2.75 gha**) and sustainable limit (**1.6 gha**). KTU examiners often award 1 bonus mark for citing the correct numerical values. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A: [14 Marks] — Choice 1** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]** **(a)** Discuss in detail the **seven ways an individual can contribute to pollution prevention** in their daily life. Explain with relevant examples. **(7 marks)** **(b)** "An individual carbon footprint is the most direct indicator of personal environmental impact." Justify this statement with a **numerical calculation** of the annual carbon footprint of a KTU hostel student with the following monthly data: Electricity = 200 kWh, LPG = 6 kg, Bus travel = 300 km, Mixed waste = 25 kg, Vegetarian diet only. Use the emission factors from the standard reference table. Comment on the sustainability of the result. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question A (a) — 7 Marks** The seven ways an individual can contribute to pollution prevention are: **1. Source Reduction:** Avoid purchasing products with excessive packaging, single-use plastics, and disposable items. Example: Carrying a cloth bag to the grocery store eliminates approximately **170 single-use plastic bags per year per person**. **2. Reuse:** Repair, repurpose, and donate items instead of discarding. Example: Refilling a water bottle 1,000 times saves **167 kg of plastic waste** over its lifetime. **3. Recycle:** Segregate waste into wet, dry, and hazardous categories at the household level. Example: Recycling one aluminium can saves enough energy to run a 60W bulb for **20 hours**. **4. Energy Conservation:** Use LED bulbs, switch off appliances, set AC to 24°C. Example: LED bulbs consume 80% less energy and last 25 times longer than incandescent bulbs. **5. Water Conservation:** Fix leaks, install low-flow fixtures, harvest rainwater. Example: A dripping tap wastes 11,000 litres/year; a single rainwater harvesting system can save 50,000 litres/year for a household. **6. Sustainable Transportation:** Use public transport, cycle, or walk for short distances. Example: Switching from a 2-wheeler to a bus for a 10 km daily commute reduces CO₂ by **~600 kg/year**. **7. Awareness and Civic Engagement:** Educate peers, report violators to KSPCB (Kerala State Pollution Control Board), participate in environmental campaigns like World Environment Day (5 June). Example: Kerala's **Haritha Keralam Mission** is a state-level initiative where individual participation amplifies the impact. > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfall — KTU Examiner's Warning:** Students often list generic actions like "plant a tree" without quantifying the impact. KTU 2024 valuation guidelines mandate **at least one quantitative example per action** for full 7 marks. Vague answers fetch maximum 4 marks. --- #### **Model Solution for Question A (b) — 7 Marks** **Given Data (Monthly):** - Electricity: 200 kWh - LPG: 6 kg - Bus travel: 300 km (assume diesel bus, emission factor ≈ **0.089 kg CO₂/passenger-km**) - Mixed waste: 25 kg - Vegetarian diet: assume 25 kg vegetables/month (emission factor = **2.0 kg CO₂e/kg**) **Step 1 — Annualize the Data** $$E_{\text{electricity}} = 200 \times 12 \times 0.82 = 1{,}968 \text{ kg CO}_2$$ $$E_{\text{LPG}} = 6 \times 12 \times 2.94 = 211.68 \text{ kg CO}_2$$ $$E_{\text{transport}} = 300 \times 12 \times 0.089 = 320.4 \text{ kg CO}_2$$ $$E_{\text{waste}} = 25 \times 12 \times 0.58 = 174 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e}$$ $$E_{\text{diet}} = 25 \times 12 \times 2.0 = 600 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e}$$ **Step 2 — Sum Total** $$CF_{\text{total}} = 1{,}968 + 211.68 + 320.4 + 174 + 600 = 3{,}274.08 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e/year}$$ $$\boxed{CF_{\text{total}} \approx 3.27 \text{ tonnes CO}_2\text{e/year}}$$ **Step 3 — Sustainability Assessment** $$\text{Sustainability Ratio} = \frac{3.27}{1.6} = 2.04$$ **Conclusion:** The student's footprint is **2.04 times the sustainable limit**. Although the vegetarian diet keeps food emissions low, the high electricity consumption (likely due to AC and electronics use in hostels) is the dominant contributor at **60%** of the total. The student should focus on: - Reducing electricity use (switch to LED, AC at 24°C) - Using public transport more (already doing so — good) - Composting food waste (eliminates 174 kg of waste emissions) - Target reduction: **at least 50%** to approach sustainability > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often forget to multiply by **12 months** to annualize. Also, the emission factor for Indian grid electricity is **0.82 kg CO₂/kWh** (not 0.5 or 1.0 — common student errors). Using wrong factors leads to cascading errors and may cost 2-3 marks. --- #### **Question B: [14 Marks] — Choice 2 (Alternative)** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]** **(a)** Explain the **concept of waste segregation at source**. Discuss the **Solid Waste Management Rules, 2016** of India and the role of an individual in implementing them. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write detailed notes on: - (i) The Plastic Waste Management Amendment Rules, 2021 - (ii) The role of an individual in e-waste management under Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question B (a) — 7 Marks** **Concept of Waste Segregation at Source:** Waste segregation at source is the practice of separating waste into distinct categories **at the point of generation** (i.e., in the household, office, or institution) before it is collected by municipal workers. This is the **first and most critical step** in solid waste management, as mixed waste cannot be effectively recycled, composted, or safely disposed of. **Categories (per SWM Rules, 2016):** | Category | Examples | Disposal Method | |----------|----------|-----------------| | **Wet Waste (Biodegradable)** | Food scraps, vegetable peels, garden waste | Composting, biogas, vermiculture | | **Dry Waste (Non-biodegradable)** | Paper, plastic, metal, glass, rubber | Recycling | | **Hazardous / Domestic Hazardous Waste** | Batteries, paint cans, chemicals, medical waste | Authorized collection centers | | **Sanitary Waste** | Sanitary napkins, diapers | Incineration (separate) | **Role of the Individual under SWM Rules, 2016:** 1. **Legal Mandate:** Every waste generator must segregate waste at source (Rule 4(b)) — failing to do so is a punishable offence. 2. **Three-Bin System:** Maintain separate bins (green for wet, blue for dry, red for hazardous) in homes. 3. **Home Composting:** Convert kitchen waste into manure, reducing landfill load. 4. **Avoid Littering and Burning:** Burning of waste releases **dioxins and furans** — potent carcinogens. 5. **Participate in Local Body Drives:** Cooperate with ward-level sanitation workers and attend awareness programs. 6. **Reduce, Reuse First:** Segregation is the last resort — minimize waste generation first. 7. **Report Non-Compliance:** Use the **Swachh Bharat App** or contact the local municipal corporation to report violators. > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall:** Students often confuse **SWM Rules, 2016** with the **Municipal Solid Waste (Management and Handling) Rules, 2000**. KTU 2024 expects the 2016 version. Naming the wrong year costs 1 mark. --- #### **Model Solution for Question B (b) — 7 Marks** **(i) Plastic Waste Management Amendment Rules, 2021:** The **Plastic Waste Management Amendment Rules, 2021**, notified on **12 August 2021** and enforced from **1 July 2022**, prohibit the manufacture, import, stocking, distribution, sale, and use of identified **single-use plastic (SUP) items**. The banned items include: - Ear buds with plastic sticks - Plastic sticks for balloons - Plastic flags - Candy sticks - Ice-cream sticks - Polystyrene (thermocol) for decoration - Plastic plates, cups, glasses, cutlery - Plastic straws, trays - PVC banners (<100 micron) - Multi-layered plastic (for packing gutka, pan masala) **Thickness Restriction:** Plastic carry bags must have a minimum thickness of **75 microns** (increased from 50 microns) and a minimum size of **15 cm × 15 cm** to discourage reuse and littering. **Role of the Individual:** - Refuse single-use plastics (carry own bottle, bag, straw, cutlery) - Buy only bags marked **>75 microns** - Report vendors selling banned items to the **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)** via the **SINGLE-USE PLASTIC (SUP) Grievance Redressal App** - Participate in **International Plastic Bag Free Day (3 July)** **(ii) E-Waste Management under Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR):** E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2016 (amended 2022) make **Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR)** legally binding — meaning producers, manufacturers, and brand owners are responsible for the **end-of-life collection and channelization** of e-waste they introduce into the market. **Role of the Individual:** 1. **Proper Return:** Deposit e-waste (phones, laptops, batteries, chargers) at **authorized collection centers** or producer-run take-back programs. 2. **Avoid Informal Recycling:** Do not sell to kabadiwalas who may burn or acid-strip e-waste, releasing lead, mercury, and cadmium into the environment. 3. **Use Producer Apps:** Companies like Apple, Samsung, and HP have dedicated e-waste return apps in India. 4. **Demand Transparency:** Check the **EPR authorization** of brands before purchase. 5. **Data Security:** Wipe personal data before returning devices. 6. **Prolong Device Life:** The most sustainable e-device is the one you already own — repair, refurbish, and reuse. > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often describe the 3R principle instead of EPR. KTU examiners deduct 2 marks for confusing the **producer's** responsibility with the **individual's** responsibility. The individual is the **enabler** of EPR, not the liable party. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU 2024:** - ✅ **3R Principle:** Reduce → Reuse → Recycle (in that order of preference) - ✅ **7R Hierarchy:** Refuse → Rethink → Reduce → Reuse → Repair → Recycle → Rot - ✅ **Ecological Footprint (EF):** Sustainable limit = **1.6 gha/person**; Current global average = **2.75 gha** - ✅ **Earth Overshoot Day 2023:** **2 August** (memorize) - ✅ **SWM Rules 2016:** Three-bin segregation (Wet, Dry, Hazardous) is legally mandatory - ✅ **Plastic Waste Amendment Rules 2021:** Effective from **1 July 2022**; minimum thickness **75 microns**; carry bag size ≥ 15 × 15 cm - ✅ **E-Waste Rules 2016 (amended 2022):** EPR makes producers liable for end-of-life management - ✅ **Indian per capita CO₂:** ~**1.9 tonnes/year**; Global avg: ~**4.7 tonnes/year** - ✅ **Sustainable CO₂ per person:** <**2.0 tonnes/year** (IPCC 1.5°C pathway) - ✅ **LED bulbs:** Save **80% energy** vs incandescent - ✅ **AC at 24°C:** Saves **6% electricity per degree** above 24 - ✅ **Aluminium recycling:** Saves **95% energy** - ✅ **UN World Environment Day:** **5 June** | Earth Day: **22 April** - ✅ **KSPCB:** Kerala State Pollution Control Board (state-level regulatory body) - ✅ **CPCB:** Central Pollution Control Board (national-level) - ✅ **Per capita waste generation in India:** **0.5–1.5 kg/day** - ✅ **Single-use plastic ban in India:** Implemented from **1 July 2022** - ✅ **Carbon footprint reduction order (most effective first):** Transport → Electricity → Diet → Waste - ✅ **Households contribute ~60% of global GHG emissions (consumption-based)** - ✅ **Eco-brick, e-bike, vermicomposting, rainwater harvesting, LED, EPR** — high-frequency keywords - ✅ **Legal Acts to remember:** Air Act 1981, Water Act 1974, EP Act 1986, SWM Rules 2016, PWM Rules 2021, E-Waste Rules 2016 - ✅ **Carbon neutrality target of India:** **2070** (Panchamrit announcement, COP26) - ✅ **India's NDC target:** Reduce emissions intensity of GDP by **45% by 2030** (from 2005 levels); achieve **50% cumulative electric power from non-fossil sources by 2030** > **Final KTU 2024 Examiner's Tip:** When answering any question on "Role of Individual," always structure your answer in three layers: **(1) Awareness/Legal Knowledge, (2) Daily Lifestyle Actions, (3) Quantifiable Impact**. This structure matches the CO-KTU mapping for OECET722 and consistently scores 2–3 marks higher than unstructured answers.
